definition: An argument establishes the existence of God if and only if (i) it proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true, (ii) its logical form is deductively valid, and (iii) its conclusion asserts the existence of a being of maximal perfection.
conditions: proceeds from self-evidently true premises
deductively valid logical form
conclusion asserts existence of being of maximal perfection
counterexample: The design argument, in its classical formulation, proceeds from the observed order and adaptation of the natural world to the existence of an intelligent designer. Its premises — that the world exhibits marks of design, and that design implies a designer — are not self-evidently true but rest upon empirical observation and analogical reasoning. The first condition fails. Yet the design argument has been regarded by a long tradition of theologians and philosophers as a serious, perhaps even compelling, argument for the existence of God.
missing_condition: The definition restricts genuine theistic arguments to those with self-evident premises, thereby excluding the a posteriori arguments — cosmological, teleological, and experiential — that constitute the greater part of natural theology.
passage: It might be supposed that an argument establishes the existence of God if and only if it proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true, its logical form is deductively valid, and its conclusion asserts the existence of a being of maximal perfection. This definition captures the ontological argument in its classical Anselmian form rather well: one begins with the concept of a being than which nothing greater can be conceived, and one purports to show by purely a priori reasoning that such a being must exist. Yet the definition excludes what many have taken to be the most persuasive class of theistic arguments. Consider the argument from design. Its premises are drawn from observation of the natural world — the intricate adaptation of organisms to their environments, the regularity of the celestial motions, the apparent purposiveness of natural processes. These premises are not self-evidently true; they rest upon experience and upon the analogical inference that design implies a designer. The first condition fails. Yet the design argument has occupied a central place in natural theology since antiquity, and many have found it more convincing than any purely a priori demonstration. The definition has modelled theistic argumentation upon a single type — the ontological — and has thereby excluded the broad tradition of a posteriori reasoning that constitutes the larger part of the philosophical case for God's existence.

definition: A cosmological argument for God's existence is sound if and only if (i) it demonstrates that the universe had a temporal beginning, (ii) it shows that whatever begins to exist has a cause, and (iii) it identifies that cause as God.
conditions: demonstrates universe had temporal beginning
shows whatever begins to exist has a cause
identifies the cause as God
counterexample: The Thomistic version of the cosmological argument does not require the universe to have had a temporal beginning. Aquinas explicitly allowed that the world might be eternal and argued instead from the contingency of existing things to the necessity of a first sustaining cause. The first condition fails entirely for this formulation, yet it remains one of the most influential cosmological arguments in the history of philosophy.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that all cosmological arguments rest upon a temporal beginning, but an important class of such arguments proceeds from the contingency of the world rather than from its finitude in time.
passage: One might hold that a cosmological argument for God's existence is sound if and only if it demonstrates that the universe had a temporal beginning, it shows that whatever begins to exist has a cause, and it identifies that cause as God. This formulation captures the structure of the kalam cosmological argument tolerably well: the universe began to exist; whatever begins to exist has a cause; therefore the universe has a cause, which is God. Yet it excludes a venerable and distinct tradition. The cosmological argument as developed by Aquinas in the Summa Theologiae does not require the universe to have had a beginning in time. Aquinas was careful to note that reason alone cannot demonstrate whether the world is eternal or temporally finite; he accepted the temporal creation of the world on the authority of revelation, not of philosophy. His argument proceeds instead from the observation that existing things are contingent — they need not exist — to the conclusion that there must be a necessary being upon which all contingent things depend for their existence at every moment. The first condition of the definition fails entirely for this Thomistic formulation. The definition has taken one species of cosmological argument — the kalam — for the whole genus, and has thereby excluded arguments from contingency that form the backbone of Thomistic natural theology.

definition: The problem of evil is a decisive objection to theism if and only if (i) it demonstrates a logical contradiction between the existence of God and the existence of any evil, (ii) the contradiction holds regardless of what reasons God might have, and (iii) the argument is purely deductive.
conditions: demonstrates logical contradiction between God and any evil
contradiction holds regardless of divine reasons
argument is purely deductive
counterexample: The evidential problem of evil does not claim a strict logical contradiction between God and evil. It argues, rather, that the sheer quantity and distribution of suffering in the world make God's existence improbable, even if not logically impossible. The argument is probabilistic, not deductive. The first and third conditions both fail, yet the evidential problem of evil is widely regarded as a formidable challenge to theism.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the problem of evil to its logical form, ignoring the evidential or probabilistic version, which concedes logical possibility but presses the case that observed suffering renders theism unlikely.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that the problem of evil constitutes a decisive objection to theism if and only if it demonstrates a logical contradiction between the existence of God and the existence of any evil, the contradiction holds regardless of what reasons God might have for permitting evil, and the argument is purely deductive. This characterisation fits the logical problem of evil as formulated by certain twentieth-century philosophers: an omnipotent, omniscient, and perfectly good being would eliminate all evil; evil exists; therefore no such being exists. Yet this is not the only form of the problem. Consider the evidential problem of evil. Its proponent does not claim that God and evil are logically incompatible. He concedes that it is logically possible for God to have morally sufficient reasons for permitting some evil. What he argues is that the sheer quantity, intensity, and seemingly pointless distribution of suffering in the world — the prolonged agony of animals in forest fires, the suffering of children from disease — makes it improbable that such reasons exist. The argument is probabilistic rather than deductive, and it does not purport to establish a logical contradiction. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the evidential problem is widely considered the more pressing challenge to theism in contemporary discussion. The definition has identified the problem of evil with its logical version alone, overlooking the probabilistic formulation that many regard as the stronger objection.

definition: A theodicy is adequate if and only if (i) it explains every instance of suffering by reference to a greater good, (ii) the greater good in question is one that could not have been achieved without the suffering, and (iii) the explanation is intelligible to the sufferer.
conditions: explains every instance of suffering by reference to greater good
greater good could not have been achieved without suffering
explanation intelligible to sufferer
counterexample: The soul-making theodicy holds that suffering is necessary for the development of moral virtues such as courage, compassion, and patience. It offers a general account of why God might permit suffering, but it does not pretend to explain every particular instance — the death of an infant, for example, does not obviously contribute to the moral development of the infant. The first condition fails. Moreover, the explanation is frequently unintelligible or even offensive to those who suffer. The third condition fails. Yet the soul-making theodicy is regarded as one of the more serious contributions to the problem.
missing_condition: The definition demands that a theodicy explain every individual case and be comprehensible to the afflicted, but the most respected theodicies offer general frameworks rather than case-by-case explanations, and they acknowledge that the divine purposes may exceed human understanding.
passage: One might propose that a theodicy is adequate if and only if it explains every instance of suffering by reference to a greater good, the greater good could not have been achieved without the suffering, and the explanation is intelligible to the sufferer. This definition sets a high standard: each particular evil must be accounted for, and the account must make sense to those who endure it. Yet the most influential theodicies in the tradition fall short of this standard. Consider the soul-making theodicy, associated with Irenaeus and developed in modern form by John Hick. It holds that suffering provides the conditions necessary for the development of moral and spiritual virtues — courage, patience, compassion, fortitude — and that a world without suffering would be a world without the possibility of such growth. This is a powerful general account. But it does not explain every particular instance of suffering. The death of an infant from a painful disease does not contribute to the moral development of the infant, who is too young to develop virtues of any kind. The first condition fails for such cases. Furthermore, the explanation is frequently unintelligible or even deeply offensive to those in the midst of suffering; to tell a bereaved parent that her child's death served a higher purpose may strike her as callous rather than illuminating. The third condition fails. Yet the soul-making theodicy remains one of the most carefully developed responses to the problem of evil. The definition has demanded too much specificity and too much transparency from a project that, by its nature, operates at the level of general principle.

definition: An event is a miracle if and only if (i) it is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) it is brought about by the direct intervention of God, and (iii) it serves a discernible religious purpose.
conditions: violation of a law of nature
brought about by direct divine intervention
serves discernible religious purpose
counterexample: Consider an event in which a man recovers from a terminal illness after earnest prayer, and the recovery is medically inexplicable but does not violate any known law of nature — it is merely extremely improbable given current medical understanding. Many believers would call this a miracle. The first condition fails, since no law of nature has been violated, yet the event is taken as a sign of divine favour and is regarded as miraculous by the religious community.
missing_condition: The definition requires a violation of natural law, but many events regarded as miraculous are not violations of law but rather extraordinarily improbable occurrences whose timing and circumstances suggest providential significance.
passage: A familiar account holds that an event is a miracle if and only if it is a violation of a law of nature, it is brought about by the direct intervention of God, and it serves a discernible religious purpose. This definition, which owes much to Hume's formulation, captures the dramatic biblical miracles tolerably well: the parting of the Red Sea, the raising of Lazarus, the turning of water into wine. Each appears to involve the suspension or violation of a natural regularity by divine power for a manifest religious end. Yet the definition proves too narrow for the broader class of events that religious communities regard as miraculous. Consider a man who has been diagnosed with a terminal illness and given weeks to live. His congregation prays fervently on his behalf. Against all medical expectation, he recovers completely. His physicians can offer no explanation, though they do not say that any law of nature has been violated — only that the recovery is extraordinarily improbable. Many in the congregation call the event a miracle, and they regard it as a sign of divine favour. The first condition fails: no law of nature has been violated. The event is merely supremely improbable and providentially timed. Yet it is treated as miraculous. The definition has insisted upon a strict violation of natural law, when in practice the concept of miracle extends to remarkable coincidences and improbable events whose significance is discerned by the faithful.

definition: An event qualifies as a miracle if and only if (i) it occurs in the physical world, (ii) it cannot be explained by any natural cause, and (iii) it is witnessed by multiple independent observers.
conditions: occurs in physical world
cannot be explained by any natural cause
witnessed by multiple independent observers
counterexample: The conversion of St Paul on the road to Damascus is recounted as a miracle in the Christian tradition. Paul reports a blinding light and a voice from heaven. Yet the event, as described in Acts, was experienced by Paul alone in its full significance — his companions heard a sound but saw no one, or saw a light but heard nothing, depending on the account. The third condition, requiring multiple independent witnesses of the same event, fails or is at best equivocal. Yet the conversion of Paul is one of the most celebrated miracles in the Christian tradition.
missing_condition: The definition requires independent multiple attestation, but many of the most significant miracles in religious history are reported primarily on the testimony of a single individual whose experience is taken as authoritative by the community of faith.
passage: One might hold that an event qualifies as a miracle if and only if it occurs in the physical world, it cannot be explained by any natural cause, and it is witnessed by multiple independent observers. The requirement of multiple witnesses seems prudent: it guards against hallucination, fraud, and misperception. Yet it excludes a great many events that the religious traditions regard as genuinely miraculous. Consider the conversion of St Paul on the road to Damascus. Paul reports that he was struck down by a blinding light and heard the voice of Christ addressing him. The experience transformed him from a persecutor of the early Church into its most tireless apostle. Yet the accounts in Acts are inconsistent regarding what Paul's companions perceived: in one version they heard the voice but saw nothing; in another they saw the light but heard no voice. The event, in its full significance, was experienced by Paul alone. The third condition fails, or is at best deeply uncertain. Yet the conversion of Paul is one of the most consequential miracles in the Christian tradition, and its authenticity has been accepted by the Church for nearly two thousand years. The definition has imposed an evidential standard — multiple independent attestation — that many of the most important miracles in religious history do not meet, precisely because they are personal encounters with the divine.

definition: A person has religious faith if and only if (i) he believes certain propositions about God, (ii) his belief is held without sufficient evidence, and (iii) he regards the absence of evidence as irrelevant to his commitment.
conditions: believes propositions about God
belief held without sufficient evidence
regards absence of evidence as irrelevant
counterexample: Aquinas held that certain truths about God — for instance, that God exists — can be demonstrated by natural reason and are supported by philosophical evidence. A person who accepts the existence of God on the basis of the cosmological argument holds his belief on what he takes to be sufficient evidence. The second condition fails, yet Aquinas would say that this person has faith, for faith in the Thomistic sense encompasses truths of reason as well as truths that exceed reason.
missing_condition: The definition equates faith with belief in the absence of evidence, but an important theological tradition holds that faith may be supported by rational evidence and that its distinctive character lies in trust and commitment rather than in evidential deficit.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that a person has religious faith if and only if he believes certain propositions about God, his belief is held without sufficient evidence, and he regards the absence of evidence as irrelevant to his commitment. This characterisation captures a certain voluntarist picture of faith — the picture according to which faith is a leap beyond what reason can support. Yet it misrepresents a substantial theological tradition. Consider the position of Aquinas, who argued that the existence of God can be demonstrated by five ways, each of which constitutes a rational argument from premises accessible to natural reason. A person who accepts the existence of God on the strength of such arguments holds his belief on what he takes to be good and sufficient evidence. The second condition fails: his belief is not held without evidence but because of it. Yet Aquinas would not deny that this person has faith. In the Thomistic account, faith encompasses both truths that can be demonstrated by reason and truths that surpass reason, such as the doctrine of the Trinity. The definition has identified faith with its fideist variant — the tradition of Tertullian and Kierkegaard — and has thereby excluded the rationalist tradition in which faith and evidence are not opposed but complementary.

definition: Religious faith is rational if and only if (i) the believer can articulate reasons for his belief, (ii) those reasons would persuade a neutral enquirer, and (iii) the belief is revisable in the light of contrary evidence.
conditions: believer can articulate reasons
reasons would persuade neutral enquirer
belief revisable in light of contrary evidence
counterexample: A mystic who has undergone a profound religious experience — a vision or a sense of the immediate presence of God — may be unable to articulate reasons that would persuade a neutral enquirer. The experience is self-authenticating to the mystic but incommunicable in propositional form. The second condition fails. Yet the mystic's faith may be entirely rational from his own standpoint, grounded as it is in direct experience rather than in discursive argument.
missing_condition: The definition requires publicly communicable reasons, but some forms of rational faith rest upon private experience that is compelling to the subject yet resistant to propositional articulation or public demonstration.
passage: One might propose that religious faith is rational if and only if the believer can articulate reasons for his belief, those reasons would persuade a neutral enquirer, and the belief is revisable in the light of contrary evidence. This definition sets a standard of public rationality: the believer must be able to state his case in terms accessible to anyone. Yet it excludes a form of faith that many have regarded as paradigmatically rational. Consider a mystic who has undergone a profound experience of the divine — a vision, perhaps, or an overwhelming sense of the presence of God that pervades his entire being. He is wholly convinced of God's reality, and his conviction is grounded not in argument but in what he takes to be direct acquaintance. Asked to articulate reasons that would persuade a sceptical interlocutor, he finds himself unable to do so: the experience resists propositional formulation. The second condition fails. Yet there is a long tradition, from Plotinus through the medieval contemplatives to William James, that regards such experience as a legitimate and even privileged ground of religious belief. The definition has identified rationality with discursive, publicly communicable reasoning, and has thereby excluded the experiential ground of faith that many mystics and philosophers have taken to be the most secure foundation of all.

definition: An utterance is a prayer if and only if (i) it is addressed to God, (ii) it contains a petition or request, and (iii) the speaker believes that God may grant the request.
conditions: addressed to God
contains petition or request
speaker believes God may grant request
counterexample: The prayer of contemplative silence — practised in the Christian mystical tradition under such names as centering prayer or the prayer of quiet — involves no utterance, no petition, and no request. The contemplative simply rests in attentive stillness before God, seeking not to obtain anything but to be present. The second condition fails entirely, yet the contemplative tradition regards this as prayer of the highest order.
missing_condition: The definition reduces prayer to petitionary speech, but prayer encompasses contemplation, adoration, thanksgiving, and silent communion, none of which need involve a request.
passage: A natural account holds that an utterance is a prayer if and only if it is addressed to God, it contains a petition or request, and the speaker believes that God may grant the request. This definition captures petitionary prayer — the most familiar form in popular devotion — tolerably well. The man who prays for rain, the mother who prays for her sick child, the soldier who prays for safety — each addresses God with a specific petition. Yet the definition excludes what the contemplative tradition regards as the deepest form of prayer. Consider the practice of contemplative silence, commended by mystics from Meister Eckhart to the anonymous author of The Cloud of Unknowing. The contemplative enters prayer not with words but with stillness. He makes no petition and formulates no request. He seeks not to obtain anything from God but simply to rest in the divine presence, receptive and attentive. There is no utterance, no petition, and no expectation of a granted favour. The second condition fails entirely. Yet the contemplative tradition has consistently maintained that this silent prayer is not inferior to petitionary prayer but superior to it — that the soul which has passed beyond petition into wordless communion has attained a higher form of the practice. The definition has identified prayer with its petitionary species and has missed the genus.

definition: Prayer is efficacious if and only if (i) the petitioner prays with sincere intention, (ii) the prayer is directed towards a specific outcome, and (iii) the outcome occurs as a consequence of the prayer.
conditions: petitioner prays with sincere intention
prayer directed towards specific outcome
outcome occurs as consequence of prayer
counterexample: A monk who prays the daily office does so with sincere intention, yet his prayer is not directed towards any specific worldly outcome. He prays in obedience and devotion, and he regards the discipline of regular prayer as efficacious in shaping his character and deepening his relationship with God — effects that are not specific outcomes in the sense the definition requires. The second condition fails, yet the monk would insist that his prayer is profoundly efficacious.
missing_condition: The definition ties efficacy to the production of specific external outcomes, but prayer may be efficacious in the sense that it transforms the character and spiritual disposition of the one who prays, without producing any particular external result.
passage: One might hold that prayer is efficacious if and only if the petitioner prays with sincere intention, the prayer is directed towards a specific outcome, and the outcome occurs as a consequence of the prayer. This definition models efficacy upon the pattern of successful petition: the man prays for recovery from illness, and he recovers. Yet the definition proves too narrow when applied to the full range of what the religious traditions regard as efficacious prayer. Consider a monk who prays the divine office seven times daily, as prescribed by his rule. He prays with the utmost sincerity. But his prayer is not directed towards any specific worldly outcome — he does not pray for health, wealth, or particular favours. He prays in obedience to his rule and in devotion to God. Over the years, the discipline of prayer shapes his character: he becomes more patient, more compassionate, more attentive to the needs of others. He would say that his prayer has been profoundly efficacious. Yet the second condition of the definition is not satisfied, for there is no specific outcome towards which the prayer was directed. The definition has modelled the efficacy of prayer upon petitionary success, when in practice many of the most devout regard the transformative effect of prayer upon the soul of the one who prays as its deepest and most important efficacy.

definition: An object is sacred if and only if (i) it has been formally consecrated by a religious authority, (ii) it is set apart from profane use, and (iii) its desecration is regarded as a grave offence.
conditions: formally consecrated by religious authority
set apart from profane use
desecration regarded as grave offence
counterexample: A mountain regarded as sacred by an indigenous people may never have been formally consecrated by any religious authority in the institutional sense. Its sacredness is primordial: it was sacred before any rite was performed upon it, and the people's reverence for it arises from a sense that the place is inherently numinous. The first condition fails, yet the mountain is sacred to the community and its desecration would be regarded as a profound wrong.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal institutional consecration, but many sacred sites and objects derive their sacred character from communal recognition of inherent numinosity rather than from any official rite of dedication.
passage: A plausible account holds that an object is sacred if and only if it has been formally consecrated by a religious authority, it is set apart from profane use, and its desecration is regarded as a grave offence. This definition captures the sacredness of the church chalice, the Torah scroll, and the consecrated host reasonably well: each has been set apart by institutional rite, each is reserved for sacred purposes, and each is protected by strong prohibitions against misuse. Yet consider a mountain held sacred by an indigenous people. No priest or institutional authority has performed a rite of consecration upon it. Its sacredness is not conferred by ceremony but is, so to speak, discovered: the people regard the mountain as numinous in itself, as a place where the boundary between the ordinary world and the world of spirits grows thin. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet the mountain is indisputably sacred to those who revere it. They would not permit mining upon its slopes, and they regard any interference with the mountain as a violation of the deepest kind. The definition has modelled sacredness upon the institutional practices of organised religion, where sacred status is conferred by official rite, and has overlooked the more primordial form of sacredness in which a place or thing is recognised as inherently set apart by the community's collective sense of the numinous.

definition: A text is sacred scripture if and only if (i) it claims to be the word of God, (ii) it was composed by authors who were divinely inspired, and (iii) it has been accepted into the official canon of a religious tradition.
conditions: claims to be word of God
composed by divinely inspired authors
accepted into official canon
counterexample: The Analects of Confucius do not claim to be the word of God; Confucius did not claim divine inspiration, and the Analects are not the product of any notion of supernatural dictation. Yet in the Confucian tradition the Analects have been treated with a reverence closely analogous to that accorded to sacred scripture in theistic religions. They were central to the imperial examination system, memorised by scholars, and regarded as a supreme moral and spiritual authority. The first and second conditions fail, yet the text functions as scripture within its tradition.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a theistic model of scripture in which a personal God is the author or inspirer, but in non-theistic or differently theistic traditions, texts may function as scripture by virtue of their supreme authority and centrality to the community's spiritual life, without any claim of divine authorship.
passage: It might be supposed that a text is sacred scripture if and only if it claims to be the word of God, it was composed by authors who were divinely inspired, and it has been accepted into the official canon of a religious tradition. This definition captures the status of the Bible and the Quran in their respective traditions: each claims divine origin, each is attributed to inspired authors or to direct divine dictation, and each belongs to a recognised canon. Yet the definition encounters difficulty when applied beyond the Abrahamic religions. Consider the Analects of Confucius. The text does not claim to be the word of God; Confucius did not assert divine inspiration, and the work is presented as a record of his sayings and conversations. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the Analects have occupied a position in the Confucian tradition closely analogous to that of sacred scripture in Christianity or Islam. For more than two millennia they were memorised, commented upon, and treated as the supreme authority on moral and political questions. The definition has modelled scripture upon the theistic paradigm in which God speaks through human authors, and has thereby excluded the sacred texts of traditions — Confucianism, certain schools of Buddhism — in which the concept of divine authorship plays no part but the texts are nonetheless received with the highest veneration.

definition: An event constitutes divine revelation if and only if (i) God communicates a propositional truth to a human being, (ii) the truth could not have been discovered by natural reason alone, and (iii) the communication takes the form of speech or writing.
conditions: God communicates propositional truth to human being
truth could not be discovered by natural reason
communication takes form of speech or writing
counterexample: Moses before the burning bush perceives the holiness of God not through speech alone but through a theophany — a manifestation of divine presence in fire and light. The revelation is not primarily propositional; it is an encounter with a numinous reality that overwhelms the senses before any words are spoken. The third condition, requiring the form of speech or writing, fails to capture the experiential and non-linguistic dimension of this revelation, yet the event is paradigmatic of divine self-disclosure.
missing_condition: The definition restricts revelation to propositional and linguistic communication, but revelation may take the form of a non-propositional encounter with the divine — a theophany, a vision, an overwhelming sense of presence — that discloses something about God without reducing it to a statement.
passage: One might define divine revelation as an event in which God communicates a propositional truth to a human being, the truth could not have been discovered by natural reason alone, and the communication takes the form of speech or writing. This definition captures the paradigm of prophetic revelation as understood in much of the Abrahamic tradition: God speaks to the prophet, and the prophet records the divine words. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider the theophany at the burning bush described in the book of Exodus. Moses sees a bush that burns without being consumed, and he is seized with awe and fear. The text records that God speaks from the bush, but the prior and fundamental disclosure is not propositional: it is the manifestation of divine holiness through fire and light, an encounter with a numinous reality that strikes Moses with terror before any words are uttered. The third condition, which requires the form of speech or writing, fails to account for the experiential dimension of this event. The revelation is not exhausted by the words that follow; it encompasses the theophanic event itself. The definition has reduced revelation to the transmission of propositional content in linguistic form, overlooking the broader theological tradition in which God reveals himself not merely by saying things but by showing himself — through signs, theophanies, and the felt presence of the holy.

definition: A claim of revelation is authoritative if and only if (i) the recipient is a recognised prophet within a religious tradition, (ii) the content of the revelation is consistent with prior revelations in that tradition, and (iii) the revelation is confirmed by miraculous signs.
conditions: recipient is recognised prophet
content consistent with prior revelations
revelation confirmed by miraculous signs
counterexample: The prophets of the Hebrew Bible frequently delivered revelations that were strikingly inconsistent with the expectations and established teachings of their contemporaries. Amos denounced the cult of sacrifice that was central to Israelite worship; Hosea proclaimed divine mercy towards a people that the prevailing theology held to be under judgement. The second condition — consistency with prior revelations — fails in these cases, yet the prophetic messages were eventually accepted as authoritative.
missing_condition: The definition requires consistency with prior revelation, but prophetic authority sometimes rests precisely upon the willingness to challenge received tradition, and genuine revelation may correct or overthrow earlier understandings rather than merely confirming them.
passage: It might be held that a claim of revelation is authoritative if and only if the recipient is a recognised prophet within a religious tradition, the content of the revelation is consistent with prior revelations in that tradition, and the revelation is confirmed by miraculous signs. This definition captures a conservative picture of prophetic authority in which each new revelation reinforces and elaborates what has gone before. Yet the Hebrew prophets frequently disrupted precisely this expectation. Consider the prophet Amos, who declared that God despised the festivals and burnt offerings that were central to Israelite cultic practice. His message was not consistent with the priestly tradition that regarded sacrifice as divinely instituted and pleasing to God. The second condition fails: the content of the prophetic revelation stands in tension with prior teaching rather than in harmony with it. Yet the words of Amos were eventually received into the canon as authoritative scripture. A similar case arises with Hosea, who proclaimed that God desired steadfast love rather than sacrifice — a message that challenged the prevailing cultic theology. The definition has required that revelation conform to what has gone before, but the prophetic vocation, as understood within the tradition itself, sometimes consists precisely in overturning received teaching and calling the community to a deeper or corrected understanding of the divine will.

definition: An action is morally obligatory if and only if (i) God has commanded it, (ii) the command is clearly expressed in a sacred text, and (iii) the command admits of no exceptions.
conditions: God has commanded it
command clearly expressed in sacred text
command admits of no exceptions
counterexample: The command to Abraham to sacrifice his son Isaac is clearly expressed in Genesis and is issued by God. Yet the command appears to be an exception to the general prohibition against the killing of the innocent, and it is ultimately revoked — God stays Abraham's hand at the last moment. The third condition, which requires that divine commands admit of no exceptions, fails, for God himself introduces an exception to his own command. Yet the episode is treated in the tradition as a paradigm of moral obedience.
missing_condition: The definition treats divine commands as exceptionless rules, but the theological tradition acknowledges that God may issue particular commands for particular circumstances, and that he may revoke or suspend his own directives.
passage: One might suppose that an action is morally obligatory if and only if God has commanded it, the command is clearly expressed in a sacred text, and the command admits of no exceptions. This definition yields a tidy moral theory: one consults the sacred text, identifies the divine commands, and follows them without exception. Yet the definition encounters a celebrated difficulty in the story of Abraham and Isaac. God commands Abraham to sacrifice his only son upon a mountain in the land of Moriah. The command is clearly expressed in the text of Genesis. Abraham sets out in obedience. But at the decisive moment, an angel stays his hand: God revokes the command and provides a ram as a substitute. The third condition — that the command admits of no exceptions — fails in a dramatic fashion, for God himself introduces the exception. The command was issued, it was clearly expressed, and Abraham was prepared to obey it, yet it was not an exceptionless rule but a particular directive addressed to a particular person in a particular circumstance. The definition has treated divine commands as universal, unalterable prescriptions, when the tradition itself shows that God may command, test, and relent, issuing directives that are specific to the occasion and subject to his sovereign will to modify or withdraw them.

definition: The divine command theory of morality is coherent if and only if (i) moral obligations are constituted by God's commands, (ii) God's commands are issued for reasons that are themselves moral, and (iii) God could not have commanded otherwise.
conditions: moral obligations constituted by God's commands
commands issued for moral reasons
God could not have commanded otherwise
counterexample: A central tenet of many divine command theorists is that God's commands are acts of sovereign will and that God could, in principle, have commanded otherwise — he could have commanded different things, and those things would then have been obligatory. The third condition, which requires that God could not have commanded otherwise, conflicts with this voluntarist strand of the theory. Yet the voluntarist version of divine command theory is a coherent and historically important position.
missing_condition: The definition builds in the necessity of the divine commands, excluding the voluntarist tradition in which God's freedom to command otherwise is essential to the theory's account of the source of moral obligation.
passage: It might be held that the divine command theory of morality is coherent if and only if moral obligations are constituted by God's commands, God's commands are issued for reasons that are themselves moral, and God could not have commanded otherwise. The third condition appears to protect the theory from the objection that God might have commanded cruelty or injustice: if God could not have commanded otherwise, then the moral order is necessary and stable. Yet the voluntarist tradition within divine command theory regards this third condition as not merely unnecessary but contrary to the spirit of the theory. William of Ockham and others maintained that God's commands are free acts of sovereign will, and that what gives them their moral force is precisely that they are acts of divine authority rather than reflections of an independent moral order. On this view, God could have commanded differently, and had he done so, the resulting commands would have been obligatory. The third condition fails for the voluntarist, yet his position is neither incoherent nor marginal; it represents one of the two principal strands of the divine command tradition. The definition has built the necessity of the divine commands into the conditions of coherence, thereby excluding the voluntarist theory by stipulation rather than by argument.

definition: A person is immortal if and only if (i) he possesses an immaterial soul, (ii) the soul survives the death of the body, and (iii) the soul retains personal identity after death.
conditions: possesses immaterial soul
soul survives death of body
soul retains personal identity after death
counterexample: The Christian doctrine of the resurrection of the body holds that the dead will be raised in bodily form at the last judgement. On this account, immortality is not the survival of a disembodied soul but the reconstitution of the whole person — body and soul together — in a glorified state. The first condition, requiring an immaterial soul as the vehicle of survival, is not strictly necessary on this view: what survives is the person in his bodily totality. The doctrine of bodily resurrection offers a form of immortality that does not depend upon the separability of an immaterial soul.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a dualist model of immortality in which the soul alone survives, but the resurrection tradition holds that immortality is achieved through the restoration of the embodied person, not merely through the persistence of a disembodied soul.
passage: A familiar account holds that a person is immortal if and only if he possesses an immaterial soul, the soul survives the death of the body, and the soul retains personal identity after death. This definition captures the Platonic picture of immortality: the soul, being immaterial and simple, cannot be destroyed by the dissolution of the body, and it carries the person's identity into an afterlife. Yet the definition proves too narrow when applied to the Christian doctrine of the resurrection of the body. St Paul writes that the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and the creeds affirm the resurrection of the body. On this account, immortality does not consist in the survival of a disembodied soul wandering in some ethereal realm. It consists in the reconstitution of the whole person — body and soul reunited — in a glorified and incorruptible state. The first condition, which requires an immaterial soul as the sole vehicle of survival, does not capture this doctrine, for what is promised is not the escape of the soul from the body but the transformation and restoration of the body itself. The definition has modelled immortality upon the Platonic paradigm and has thereby excluded the bodily resurrection tradition, which understands immortality as the redemption of the whole person rather than the liberation of one of his parts.

definition: Belief in immortality is rationally warranted if and only if (i) there is empirical evidence of the soul's survival, (ii) the evidence is publicly verifiable, and (iii) the survival hypothesis is the best explanation of the evidence.
conditions: empirical evidence of soul's survival
evidence publicly verifiable
survival hypothesis is best explanation
counterexample: Kant argued that immortality is a postulate of practical reason: the moral law demands that virtue and happiness be ultimately united, and since this union is not achieved in the present life, reason requires the postulation of a future life in which it is. The warrant for belief in immortality is not empirical but moral and rational. The first and second conditions both fail, yet Kant regarded the belief as rationally warranted — indeed, as rationally necessary for the coherence of the moral life.
missing_condition: The definition restricts rational warrant to empirical evidence, but an important philosophical tradition holds that belief in immortality may be warranted on moral or practical grounds, without any empirical evidence of survival.
passage: One might hold that belief in immortality is rationally warranted if and only if there is empirical evidence of the soul's survival, the evidence is publicly verifiable, and the survival hypothesis is the best explanation of the evidence. This definition sets an empiricist standard: belief in the afterlife must be grounded in observable data. Yet it excludes what is perhaps the most careful philosophical argument for the rationality of such belief. Kant argued in the Critique of Practical Reason that immortality is a postulate of practical reason. The moral law commands us to pursue the highest good — the perfect union of virtue and happiness. We observe that this union is not achieved in the present life: the virtuous suffer and the wicked prosper. Yet the moral law is unconditionally binding, and its demands must be fulfillable. Reason therefore requires us to postulate a future life in which the disproportion between virtue and happiness is rectified. The warrant for this belief is not empirical but practical and moral: it is required by the internal coherence of the moral life itself. The first and second conditions fail entirely — there is no publicly verifiable empirical evidence in Kant's argument. Yet Kant regarded the postulate as rationally necessary, not as a speculative guess. The definition has identified rational warrant with empirical evidence, overlooking the Kantian tradition in which practical reason provides its own distinctive grounds for belief.

definition: An experience is a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) the subject perceives the presence of a personal God, (ii) the experience is accompanied by intense emotion, and (iii) the experience produces a lasting moral transformation in the subject.
conditions: subject perceives presence of personal God
accompanied by intense emotion
produces lasting moral transformation
counterexample: The Buddhist experience of nirvana — the cessation of suffering and the dissolution of the self — is widely regarded as a profound religious experience. Yet it does not involve the perception of a personal God, for Buddhism, in many of its forms, is non-theistic. The first condition fails. Nevertheless, the experience of nirvana is central to the Buddhist path and is treated with the same seriousness as the experience of God in the theistic traditions.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a theistic framework, but genuine religious experiences occur in non-theistic traditions where the object of the experience is not a personal God but an impersonal ultimate reality, a state of liberation, or the dissolution of the self.
passage: A natural definition holds that an experience is a genuine religious experience if and only if the subject perceives the presence of a personal God, the experience is accompanied by intense emotion, and the experience produces a lasting moral transformation in the subject. This definition captures the paradigmatic theistic experiences described by Christian, Jewish, and Islamic mystics: the overwhelming sense of God's nearness, the mingled awe and joy, the permanent reorientation of the moral life. Yet the definition excludes the religious experiences of non-theistic traditions. Consider the Buddhist experience of nirvana. The practitioner who attains nirvana experiences the cessation of craving, the dissolution of the illusory self, and a profound peace that the Buddhist tradition regards as the highest spiritual attainment. This experience does not involve the perception of a personal God, for the Buddhist framework, in its Theravada form at least, does not posit such a being. The first condition fails. Yet the experience of nirvana is taken as seriously by Buddhists as the experience of divine presence is taken by Christians. The definition has built theism into the concept of religious experience and has thereby excluded the contemplative achievements of non-theistic traditions, which are no less religious for being directed towards an impersonal ultimate rather than a personal deity.

definition: A religious experience provides grounds for belief in God if and only if (i) the experience is vivid and phenomenologically distinct from ordinary perception, (ii) it occurs spontaneously and without the subject's contrivance, and (iii) the subject was not predisposed to have such an experience by prior religious training.
conditions: vivid and phenomenologically distinct
occurs spontaneously without contrivance
subject not predisposed by prior religious training
counterexample: Many of the most celebrated mystics — Teresa of Avila, John of the Cross, Rumi — underwent their deepest religious experiences only after years of disciplined prayer, ascetical practice, and intensive study of their traditions. Their experiences were the fruit of sustained effort and deep religious formation, not spontaneous occurrences in unprepared subjects. The second and third conditions both fail, yet the experiences of these mystics are among the most commonly cited grounds for belief in the reality of the divine.
missing_condition: The definition privileges spontaneous experience in untrained subjects, but the contemplative traditions hold that the most profound religious experiences arise precisely from sustained practice and formation, and that disciplined preparation enhances rather than undermines their evidential value.
passage: It might be supposed that a religious experience provides grounds for belief in God if and only if the experience is vivid and phenomenologically distinct from ordinary perception, it occurs spontaneously and without the subject's contrivance, and the subject was not predisposed to have such an experience by prior religious training. The motivation for the third condition is understandable: one might worry that a person trained to expect a vision of God will produce one through the power of suggestion. Yet the condition excludes the most important class of religious experiences in the history of the traditions. Teresa of Avila spent decades in prayer before she experienced the transverberation — the piercing of her heart by an angel's lance — that became one of the most celebrated mystical events in Christian history. John of the Cross underwent his dark night of the soul only after years of Carmelite formation. Rumi's ecstatic experiences were inseparable from his immersion in the Sufi tradition. In each case, the experience was the culmination of prolonged spiritual discipline, not a bolt from the blue. The second and third conditions fail. Yet these are precisely the experiences that the traditions hold up as the most authoritative. The definition has treated prior training as a source of contamination, when the contemplative traditions regard it as the necessary preparation without which the deepest experiences cannot occur.

definition: The concept of God is coherent if and only if (i) God is defined as an omnipotent being, (ii) omnipotence means the ability to do anything whatsoever, and (iii) no attribute of God conflicts with any other.
conditions: God defined as omnipotent being
omnipotence means ability to do anything whatsoever
no divine attribute conflicts with any other
counterexample: The paradox of the stone asks whether an omnipotent being can create a stone so heavy that he cannot lift it. If he can, there is something he cannot do (lift the stone); if he cannot, there is something he cannot do (create such a stone). Omnipotence defined as the ability to do anything whatsoever generates an internal contradiction. The third condition fails, since the attribute of omnipotence, on this reading, conflicts with itself.
missing_condition: The definition construes omnipotence as absolutely unrestricted power, but many theologians have refined omnipotence to mean the ability to do anything that is logically possible, thereby avoiding self-referential paradoxes without diminishing the concept of God.
passage: One might hold that the concept of God is coherent if and only if God is defined as an omnipotent being, omnipotence means the ability to do anything whatsoever, and no attribute of God conflicts with any other. The first two conditions seem uncontroversial: God is typically defined as all-powerful, and all-powerfulness appears to mean the capacity to perform any action. Yet these conditions, taken together, generate a difficulty. Consider the paradox of the stone: can God create a stone so heavy that he himself cannot lift it? If he can create such a stone, then there is something he cannot do — namely, lift it — and his omnipotence is limited. If he cannot create such a stone, then again there is something he cannot do, and his omnipotence is equally limited. On either horn of the dilemma, the attribute of omnipotence conflicts with itself. The third condition fails. Yet this result has been thought to show not that God is incoherent but that omnipotence has been crudely defined. Aquinas and others have argued that omnipotence should be understood as the power to do anything that is logically possible, and that the creation of a self-defeating stone is not a logically possible task but a piece of disguised nonsense. The definition has construed omnipotence too broadly and has thereby manufactured a contradiction where a more careful analysis would find none.

definition: The free-will defence succeeds if and only if (i) it shows that all evil results from the misuse of human free will, (ii) the value of free will outweighs the evil it produces, and (iii) God could not have created free beings who always choose rightly.
conditions: shows all evil results from misuse of human free will
value of free will outweighs evil produced
God could not have created free beings who always choose rightly
counterexample: Natural evils — earthquakes, floods, diseases that strike the innocent — do not result from the misuse of human free will. An earthquake that destroys a city and kills thousands of children is not the consequence of anyone's free choice. The first condition fails for the entire category of natural evil. Yet the free-will defence remains influential, for its proponents typically argue that it addresses moral evil specifically, while natural evil requires a separate treatment.
missing_condition: The definition requires the free-will defence to account for all evil, but the defence is standardly offered as an explanation of moral evil only; natural evil falls outside its scope and must be addressed by other theodical considerations.
passage: It might be supposed that the free-will defence succeeds if and only if it shows that all evil results from the misuse of human free will, the value of free will outweighs the evil it produces, and God could not have created free beings who always choose rightly. This definition demands that the free-will defence be a comprehensive theodicy, explaining the totality of evil by reference to human freedom. Yet it fails to recognise the limited scope that the defence's proponents typically claim for it. Consider natural evil: the earthquake that levels a village, the disease that strikes an infant, the flood that drowns livestock and farmers alike. These evils are not the result of any human choice; they arise from the natural operations of a physical world that is indifferent to human well-being. The first condition fails entirely for this class of evil. No misuse of free will causes the tectonic plates to shift or the pathogen to multiply. Yet the free-will defence remains one of the most carefully developed responses to the problem of evil in philosophical theology. Its proponents typically acknowledge that it addresses moral evil — the evil that arises from human choice — and not natural evil, which requires separate consideration. The definition has demanded that the defence do more than its advocates claim, and has judged it a failure for not accomplishing what it never set out to accomplish.

definition: Faith and reason are in conflict if and only if (i) faith asserts propositions that reason cannot verify, (ii) reason demands verification as a condition of assent, and (iii) no proposition can be both an article of faith and a delivrance of reason.
conditions: faith asserts propositions reason cannot verify
reason demands verification for assent
no proposition both article of faith and delivrance of reason
counterexample: Aquinas maintained that certain truths — such as the existence of God and the immortality of the soul — are both articles of faith and deliverances of natural reason. They can be believed on the authority of revelation by those who have not studied philosophy, and they can be demonstrated by philosophical argument by those who have. The third condition fails, for there exists a class of propositions that belong simultaneously to faith and to reason.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that faith and reason have entirely disjoint domains, but the Thomistic tradition holds that their domains overlap, with some truths accessible by both routes, and that this overlap is a source of harmony rather than conflict.
passage: It is sometimes held that faith and reason are in conflict if and only if faith asserts propositions that reason cannot verify, reason demands verification as a condition of assent, and no proposition can be both an article of faith and a delivrance of reason. This construal presents faith and reason as occupying mutually exclusive territories: what is believed on faith cannot be known by reason, and what is known by reason need not be believed on faith. Yet the Thomistic tradition rejects this sharp separation. Aquinas argued that certain truths about God — pre-eminently that God exists — can be arrived at by natural reason through philosophical demonstration. The same truths are also articles of faith, revealed by God in scripture for the benefit of those who lack the leisure or ability to follow a philosophical proof. The third condition of the definition fails for these truths: they are simultaneously articles of faith and deliverances of reason. Aquinas did not regard this overlap as paradoxical; on the contrary, he took it as evidence of the harmony between faith and reason, each approaching the same truth from a different direction. The definition has assumed a disjunction where the tradition sees a convergence.

definition: Revelation is progressive if and only if (i) later revelations contain more truth than earlier ones, (ii) each stage of revelation supersedes the one before it, and (iii) the final stage renders all earlier stages obsolete.
conditions: later revelations contain more truth than earlier
each stage supersedes the one before it
final stage renders earlier stages obsolete
counterexample: The Christian claim that the New Testament fulfils the Old does not render the Old Testament obsolete. The Psalms remain in use in Christian worship; the creation narrative in Genesis retains its theological significance; the prophetic literature is read as pointing forward to Christ. The third condition fails, for the earlier revelation is not discarded but taken up and reinterpreted within the later. The relationship is one of fulfilment rather than obsolescence.
missing_condition: The definition models progression upon replacement, but the theological traditions more commonly understand progressive revelation as cumulative: earlier stages are not abolished but deepened and reinterpreted in the light of later ones.
passage: One might define progressive revelation as a process in which later revelations contain more truth than earlier ones, each stage of revelation supersedes the one before it, and the final stage renders all earlier stages obsolete. This definition models revelation upon the pattern of scientific progress, in which later theories replace earlier ones that are found to be inadequate. Yet the theological understanding of progressive revelation is quite different. Consider the Christian understanding of the relationship between the Old and New Testaments. Christians hold that the New Testament fulfils the Old — that the promises made to Abraham, the law given to Moses, and the visions of the prophets find their completion in the person and work of Christ. But this fulfilment does not render the Old Testament obsolete. The Psalms are sung in every Christian liturgy. The creation narrative in Genesis continues to shape Christian theology. The prophetic writings are read not as superseded documents but as living texts that point forward to a fulfilment they anticipate. The third condition fails. The Old Testament is not discarded but taken up, reinterpreted, and deepened in the light of the New. The definition has treated progression as replacement, when the tradition understands it as cumulative enrichment in which nothing of genuine value is lost.

definition: The Euthyphro dilemma refutes divine command theory if and only if (i) it forces a choice between God commanding something because it is good and something being good because God commands it, (ii) neither horn of the dilemma is acceptable, and (iii) no third option is available.
conditions: forces choice between two horns
neither horn acceptable
no third option available
counterexample: Several philosophers have proposed that God's nature — his essential goodness — is the standard of moral value, and that God commands in accordance with his nature. On this view, things are not good independently of God (so the first horn is avoided), nor are they good merely because God wills them arbitrarily (so the second horn is avoided). A third option is available. The third condition fails, yet the dilemma is commonly presented as if it were inescapable.
missing_condition: The definition assumes an exhaustive dilemma with exactly two horns, but the modified divine command theory, which grounds morality in God's essential nature rather than in his bare will, provides a third possibility that dissolves the dilemma.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that the Euthyphro dilemma refutes the divine command theory if and only if it forces a choice between God commanding something because it is good and something being good because God commands it, neither horn of the dilemma is acceptable, and no third option is available. If the first horn is taken, then goodness is independent of God and the divine command theory is abandoned. If the second is taken, then morality is arbitrary, for God could have commanded cruelty. The dilemma appears devastating. Yet the third condition — that no third option is available — has been challenged. Several philosophers have argued that God's essential nature, which is necessarily and perfectly good, is the ultimate standard of moral value. God commands what he does not because goodness is independent of him, nor because his will creates goodness from nothing, but because his commands flow from his nature, which is goodness itself. On this view, the Euthyphro dilemma presents a false dichotomy. A third option is available, and it is one that preserves the dependence of morality upon God without making morality arbitrary. The dilemma, it appears, is less decisive than its proponents suppose, for the modified divine command theory provides an escape between the horns.

definition: An argument A is a sound argument for the existence of God if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true, (ii) A employs only deductively valid inferential steps, and (iii) A concludes that there exists a being possessing omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true
A employs only deductively valid inferential steps
A concludes that there exists a being possessing omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness
counterexample: Consider the cosmological argument from contingency. Its central premise — that every contingent being requires a cause — is not self-evidently true in the manner of a logical axiom; it is, rather, a substantive metaphysical principle whose truth is disputed. Yet many theologians and philosophers have regarded the cosmological argument as sound. The first condition fails, yet the argument is widely taken to establish the existence of God.
missing_condition: The definition requires that premises be self-evidently true, but a sound theistic argument may proceed from premises that are well-supported by experience, philosophical reflection, or cumulative reasoning without being self-evident. What is wanted is a broader notion of rational warrant for the premises, not the narrow requirement of self-evidence.
passage: One might propose that an argument is a sound argument for the existence of God if and only if it proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true, it employs only deductively valid inferential steps, and it concludes that there exists a being possessing omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness. The definition has a certain appealing rigour. A self-evidently true premise admits of no reasonable doubt; a deductively valid inference preserves truth with certainty; and the conclusion, if reached by such means, would establish the existence of God beyond the possibility of rational dispute. One might suppose that nothing less than this could constitute a genuinely sound theistic argument. Yet one must consider the cosmological argument from contingency — the argument that proceeds from the observation that contingent beings exist, through the principle that every contingent being requires a cause external to itself, to the conclusion that there must exist a necessary being upon which all contingent beings depend. This argument has been regarded as sound by philosophers of considerable acumen, from Leibniz onwards. Yet its central premise — that every contingent being requires a cause — is not self-evidently true. It is a substantive metaphysical principle, and its truth has been disputed by Hume and others. It derives its force not from self-evidence but from a kind of explanatory compulsion: the universe, one feels, demands an explanation, and the principle of sufficient reason supplies one. The first condition of the definition fails. The argument proceeds from a premise that is rationally compelling to many minds but not self-evident in the strict sense. The definition has confused one especially exacting standard of premissary warrant — self-evidence — with the more general notion of rational acceptability, and in so doing it has excluded arguments that are widely and not unreasonably regarded as sound.

definition: A being B qualifies as God if and only if (i) B is the creator of all things other than itself, (ii) B is a person capable of intention and action, and (iii) B exists necessarily — that is, B's non-existence is logically impossible.
conditions: B is the creator of all things other than itself
B is a person capable of intention and action
B exists necessarily — that is, B's non-existence is logically impossible
counterexample: Consider the God of certain process theologians, who hold that God did not create the world ex nihilo but rather fashions order from a pre-existing chaos, and whose existence, whilst everlasting, is not logically necessary but contingent upon the metaphysical structure of reality. Such a being is personal, capable of intention and action, and is worshipped as God. Yet the first and third conditions fail: this God neither created all things nor exists with logical necessity.
missing_condition: The definition ties divinity too closely to the classical theistic attributes of creation ex nihilo and necessary existence, when in fact many coherent theological traditions ascribe divinity to a being that shapes rather than creates the world and whose existence, though everlasting, is not logically necessary. What is essential to divinity may be something more like maximal worthiness of worship rather than any particular metaphysical status.
passage: One might define God as follows: a being qualifies as God if and only if it is the creator of all things other than itself, it is a person capable of intention and action, and it exists necessarily — that is, its non-existence is logically impossible. This definition captures much of what classical theism affirms. The God of Aquinas creates all things, acts with intention and purpose, and exists by the necessity of its own nature. The definition appears to identify the essential features of the divine. Yet one must consider the God described by certain process theologians — thinkers who hold that God did not create the universe ex nihilo but rather confronts a pre-existing chaos and fashions order from it, much as the Demiurge of Plato's Timaeus shapes the receptacle into a cosmos. On this view, God is personal, capable of intention and action, and is the supreme being worthy of worship and devotion. But God did not create all things other than itself; the primordial chaos exists independently. Moreover, God's existence, though everlasting, is not logically necessary; it is contingent upon the broader metaphysical structure within which God and the world are situated. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet the being so described is recognisably God — the object of prayer, of worship, and of theological reflection. The definition has taken the distinctive commitments of one theological tradition, classical theism, and treated them as necessary conditions of divinity as such, thereby excluding conceptions of God that are, within their own traditions, perfectly coherent and religiously significant.

definition: A proof P of the existence of God is successful if and only if (i) P is formalised in first-order predicate logic, (ii) every axiom of P is accepted by all rational persons, and (iii) the conclusion of P entails the existence of a maximally perfect being.
conditions: P is formalised in first-order predicate logic
every axiom of P is accepted by all rational persons
the conclusion of P entails the existence of a maximally perfect being
counterexample: Consider the argument from religious experience, which proceeds not by formal deduction but by inference to the best explanation: the widespread, cross-cultural occurrence of experiences as of a divine presence is best explained by the existence of God. This argument has persuaded many thoughtful persons and is regarded by some philosophers as a successful proof. Yet it is not formalised in first-order predicate logic, and its premises are not accepted by all rational persons. The first and second conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition demands a degree of formal rigour and universal rational assent that may be inappropriate to the subject matter. A successful proof of God's existence might proceed by probabilistic reasoning, inference to the best explanation, or cumulative case argumentation, none of which requires first-order formalisation or universal acceptance.
passage: Suppose one holds that a proof of the existence of God is successful if and only if it is formalised in first-order predicate logic, every axiom is accepted by all rational persons, and the conclusion entails the existence of a maximally perfect being. The standard is austere but seemingly well-motivated: a proof of such momentous import ought, one might think, to meet the highest standards of logical rigour and rational compulsion. One might point to the ontological argument, which at least aspires to proceed from premises that rational persons cannot deny to a conclusion established by deductive necessity. Yet one must consider the argument from religious experience. This argument observes that across cultures, across centuries, and across widely varying psychological and social conditions, human beings have reported experiences as of a divine presence — experiences of overwhelming reality, of encounter with a being of transcendent holiness and power. The argument proceeds not by deduction but by inference to the best explanation: the best explanation of these experiences, it is urged, is that they are veridical, that there really is a divine being whose presence is apprehended. Many thoughtful philosophers have found this argument compelling, and some have regarded it as a successful proof of God's existence. But it is not formalised in first-order predicate logic; it is an informal argument employing abductive reasoning. And its premises — that religious experiences occur, that their best explanation is the existence of God — are not accepted by all rational persons. The first two conditions fail. The definition has imposed upon the concept of a successful proof a standard of formal rigour and universal assent that may be quite inappropriate to the domain of natural theology, where probabilistic, abductive, and cumulative reasoning may be the best one can hope for.

definition: A theodicy T is adequate if and only if (i) T identifies a morally sufficient reason for every instance of suffering, (ii) T is consistent with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good God, and (iii) T appeals only to goods that are logically unobtainable without the suffering in question.
conditions: T identifies a morally sufficient reason for every instance of suffering
T is consistent with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good God
T appeals only to goods that are logically unobtainable without the suffering in question
counterexample: Consider the free-will theodicy, which holds that the good of free moral agency is so great that God is justified in permitting the evil that free agents bring about. This theodicy is consistent with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good God, and it appeals to a genuine good — free will. Yet it does not identify a morally sufficient reason for every instance of suffering; natural evils such as earthquakes and childhood diseases are not caused by free agents and thus fall outside its scope. The first condition fails, yet the free-will theodicy is widely regarded as at least partially adequate.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a theodicy account for every instance of suffering, but a partially adequate theodicy may illuminate a significant range of evils without covering all cases. Adequacy in theodicy may be a matter of degree rather than an all-or-nothing affair.
passage: One might propose that a theodicy is adequate if and only if it identifies a morally sufficient reason for every instance of suffering, it is consistent with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good God, and it appeals only to goods that are logically unobtainable without the suffering in question. The definition sets a high standard, but the subject demands nothing less: if God is omnipotent and perfectly good, then there must be, for every instance of suffering, a reason sufficient to justify an omnipotent and perfectly good being in permitting it. Consider, then, the free-will theodicy. This theodicy holds that God endowed creatures with genuine freedom of the will, and that this freedom is so great a good that God is morally justified in permitting the evils that free agents bring about. The theodicy is consistent with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good God, and the good it cites — free moral agency — is arguably unobtainable in a world where God prevents all misuse of freedom. Yet the free-will theodicy does not identify a morally sufficient reason for every instance of suffering. Earthquakes, tsunamis, childhood leukaemia, the suffering of animals in the wild — these are not the products of free moral choice. They are natural evils, and the free-will theodicy has nothing to say about them. The first condition fails. Yet the free-will theodicy is not thereby dismissed as wholly inadequate; it is widely regarded as a significant partial achievement that illuminates a substantial range of moral evil even if it leaves natural evil unexplained. The definition has treated adequacy as an all-or-nothing matter, when in truth a theodicy may be partially adequate — genuinely illuminating with respect to some evils whilst remaining silent about others.

definition: A state of affairs S constitutes an evil if and only if (i) S involves the suffering of a sentient being, (ii) S is contrary to the will of God, and (iii) S serves no redemptive or compensatory purpose.
conditions: S involves the suffering of a sentient being
S is contrary to the will of God
S serves no redemptive or compensatory purpose
counterexample: Consider the suffering of an innocent child who dies of a painful disease in infancy, where no redemptive or compensatory purpose is discernible — and yet consider that some theologians hold God permits such suffering in accordance with a providential plan, so that it is not, strictly speaking, contrary to God's will. On this view the second condition fails, yet the state of affairs is nonetheless an evil. The child's suffering is evil whether or not God has willed to permit it.
missing_condition: The definition makes the classification of a state of affairs as evil dependent upon its relation to the divine will, but evil is recognisable as evil independently of any theological framework. What is wanted is an account of evil that does not presuppose a particular theological position on God's will and providence.
passage: Suppose one defines evil as follows: a state of affairs constitutes an evil if and only if it involves the suffering of a sentient being, it is contrary to the will of God, and it serves no redemptive or compensatory purpose. The definition has a certain theological neatness. Evil, on this account, is suffering that God does not will and from which no good comes — suffering, as it were, that is wholly pointless from the divine perspective. One might think this captures what is most terrible about evil: that it is pointless, unwilled by God, and productive of nothing but misery. Yet one must consider a case that troubles this definition from within theology itself. A child is born with a painful congenital disease and dies in infancy after months of suffering. No compensatory good is apparent; the suffering seems wholly gratuitous. Yet certain theologians — and not only crude or unsophisticated ones — hold that God permits all things that occur, that nothing happens contrary to the divine will in the strongest sense, and that the child's suffering is encompassed within a providential plan whose purposes may be inscrutable to finite minds. On this view, the suffering is not contrary to God's will; God has willed to permit it. The second condition fails. Yet the child's suffering is evil — manifestly, undeniably evil. The definition has made the classification of evil parasitic upon a contested theological claim about the divine will, when in truth the evil of a child's suffering is recognisable as such independently of any theological framework. One need not settle the question of what God wills in order to recognise that the suffering of an innocent is an evil.
