definition: Suffering S is gratuitous if and only if (i) S is not a logically necessary condition of any greater good, (ii) no sentient being benefits from S either directly or indirectly, and (iii) S is not a consequence of the free choice of the sufferer.
conditions: S is not a logically necessary condition of any greater good
no sentient being benefits from S either directly or indirectly
S is not a consequence of the free choice of the sufferer
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who freely chooses to attempt a dangerous ascent and suffers a broken leg in a fall. The suffering is a consequence of the mountaineer's free choice, so the third condition is not met — yet the suffering is gratuitous in the relevant sense if no greater good comes of it and no one benefits from it. The mountaineer's freedom of choice does not render the suffering any less pointless.
missing_condition: The definition excludes freely chosen suffering from the category of gratuitous suffering, but suffering may be both freely incurred and gratuitous. The origin of suffering in free choice is irrelevant to the question of whether the suffering serves any purpose or produces any good.
passage: One might hold that suffering is gratuitous if and only if it is not a logically necessary condition of any greater good, no sentient being benefits from it either directly or indirectly, and it is not a consequence of the free choice of the sufferer. The definition aims to capture the notion of pointless suffering — suffering that serves no purpose and for which no justification can be found. The third condition is motivated by the thought that if one freely chooses to undergo suffering, one has in some sense consented to it, and suffering to which one has consented is not, properly speaking, gratuitous. Yet one must consider the mountaineer who freely elects to climb a dangerous peak, fully aware of the risks, and who suffers a severe injury in the course of the ascent — a broken leg, let us say, sustained in a fall upon a remote glacier. The mountaineer freely chose the ascent; the third condition is not satisfied, and the definition classifies the suffering as non-gratuitous. But suppose that no greater good comes of the suffering: the mountaineer does not become a better person for it, no one else is benefited by it, and the injury leaves a permanent disability that diminishes the quality of the mountaineer's remaining life. The suffering is, by any reasonable standard, pointless. That the mountaineer freely chose to incur the risk does not make the suffering any less gratuitous; it merely makes it freely incurred. The definition has confused the voluntariness of suffering with its purposefulness, when in truth these are quite distinct notions. Suffering may be both freely chosen and entirely gratuitous, and an adequate definition of gratuitous suffering must acknowledge this.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E violates a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by the direct intervention of God, and (iii) E is witnessed by multiple independent observers.
conditions: E violates a law of nature
E is brought about by the direct intervention of God
E is witnessed by multiple independent observers
counterexample: Consider a case in which a single hermit, living alone in the desert, prays for rain during a prolonged drought, and rain falls from a cloudless sky in a manner that violates all known meteorological laws. If this event is brought about by the direct intervention of God and violates a law of nature, it is a miracle. Yet no one else witnesses it; the hermit is the sole observer. The third condition fails, yet the event is no less miraculous for being unwitnessed.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the metaphysical character of an event with the epistemological conditions for its verification. Whether an event is a miracle depends upon its causal origin and its relation to natural law, not upon how many persons happen to observe it. An unwitnessed miracle is still a miracle.
passage: One might define a miracle as follows: an event is a miracle if and only if it violates a law of nature, it is brought about by the direct intervention of God, and it is witnessed by multiple independent observers. The first two conditions capture the traditional theological understanding of miracles: they are divine interventions that contravene the ordinary course of nature. The third condition is added, one supposes, as a safeguard against credulity — for if a miracle is witnessed by several independent observers, one has better reason to think that the event really occurred and was not merely the product of hallucination or deception. Yet one must consider whether the number of witnesses bears upon the question of whether an event is a miracle. Imagine a hermit living in solitude in a remote desert. He has prayed for rain during a prolonged drought, and rain falls suddenly from a cloudless sky in a manner that contravenes all known laws of meteorology. Let us suppose, for the sake of the argument, that this event is genuinely brought about by the direct intervention of God and genuinely violates a law of nature. The first two conditions are satisfied. But the hermit is alone; no one else witnesses the event. The third condition fails. Yet the event is a miracle: it is a divine intervention that violates natural law. Its miraculous character does not depend upon whether anyone besides the hermit happens to observe it. The definition has confused the nature of a miracle with the conditions under which we might be warranted in asserting that a miracle has occurred. The number of witnesses is relevant to our grounds for accepting that a miracle has taken place, but it is wholly irrelevant to whether the event itself is miraculous.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is an event of a type that has never previously occurred, (ii) E is inexplicable by current natural science, and (iii) E produces a religiously significant outcome.
conditions: E is an event of a type that has never previously occurred
E is inexplicable by current natural science
E produces a religiously significant outcome
counterexample: Consider the parting of the Red Sea as described in the book of Exodus. If this event occurred, it was a miracle. Yet events of a broadly similar type — the sudden recession of waters — have occurred before and since, driven by strong winds and seismic activity. The first condition fails: the event is not of a type that has never previously occurred. Nevertheless, if it was brought about by divine agency at the precise moment required for the deliverance of the Israelites, it would be a miracle.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a miracle be unprecedented in type, but a miracle may involve a natural event-type occurring at a divinely appointed moment and in divinely arranged circumstances. What makes an event miraculous is not its novelty as an event-type but its causal relation to the divine and the purposive character of its timing.
passage: Suppose one proposes that an event is a miracle if and only if it is of a type that has never previously occurred, it is inexplicable by current natural science, and it produces a religiously significant outcome. The definition is meant to capture the extraordinary character of miracles: they are unprecedented, scientifically inexplicable, and religiously momentous. One might think that these three features, taken together, suffice to distinguish miracles from all other events. Yet one must consider a case drawn from the scriptural tradition. The parting of the Red Sea, as recounted in the book of Exodus, is among the most celebrated miracles in the Western religious tradition. If the event occurred, it was a miracle — a divine act of deliverance. But events of a broadly similar physical type are not unprecedented. Strong winds can drive shallow waters back and expose a land bridge; seismic events can produce sudden recessions of the sea. Such events have been documented by naturalists and historians. The parting of the sea, considered merely as a physical event-type — the sudden recession of a body of water — has occurred before. The first condition fails. What makes the event miraculous, if it was miraculous, is not that no event of this physical type had ever occurred before, but rather that it occurred at the precise moment required for the deliverance of the Israelites, in circumstances that bore the marks of divine purpose and agency. The definition has confused the novelty of an event-type with the miraculous character of an event-token, when in truth what is essential to a miracle is not unprecedented physical novelty but the purposive intervention of a divine agent.

definition: A person S has faith that p if and only if (i) S assents to the proposition p, (ii) S lacks conclusive evidence for p, and (iii) S assents to p in the absence of any evidence whatsoever.
conditions: S assents to the proposition p
S lacks conclusive evidence for p
S assents to p in the absence of any evidence whatsoever
counterexample: Consider a devout person who assents to the proposition that God exists. She lacks conclusive evidence — she cannot prove God's existence with certainty. But she does not lack all evidence: she points to the order of nature, to her own religious experiences, to the testimony of saints, and to the moral argument. She has evidence, though not conclusive evidence. The third condition fails, yet her assent is a paradigm case of faith.
missing_condition: The definition equates faith with assent in the complete absence of evidence, when in truth faith characteristically involves assent that goes beyond the evidence without being wholly unsupported by it. What is wanted is a condition specifying that the evidence is insufficient to compel assent, not that evidence is entirely absent.
passage: One might propose that a person has faith that p if and only if the person assents to the proposition p, lacks conclusive evidence for p, and assents to p in the absence of any evidence whatsoever. The definition captures a popular conception of faith: faith, on this view, is assent without evidence — a leap in the dark, as it is sometimes described. The first two conditions are relatively uncontroversial: faith does involve assent, and it is generally agreed that faith is exercised precisely where conclusive evidence is lacking. It is the third condition that causes difficulty. Consider a devout person who has faith that God exists. She assents to the proposition; she lacks conclusive evidence. But she does not lack all evidence. She observes the order and beauty of the natural world and takes it as evidence of a designing intelligence. She has had experiences that she describes as encounters with a divine presence, and she takes these as evidence. She is moved by the testimony of persons whose sanctity and wisdom she respects. She finds the moral argument for God's existence rationally compelling, if not conclusive. She has, in short, a body of evidence that supports her assent without being sufficient to compel it. The third condition of the definition fails: she does not assent in the absence of any evidence whatsoever. Yet her assent is a paradigm case of religious faith. The definition has confused faith with credulity. Faith, as it is actually exercised by thoughtful religious persons, characteristically involves going beyond the evidence — assenting with a confidence that outstrips what the evidence strictly warrants — but it does not involve ignoring the evidence or proceeding in its total absence.

definition: A person S has religious faith if and only if (i) S holds certain doctrinal propositions to be true, (ii) S participates regularly in communal worship, and (iii) S belongs to an organised religious institution.
conditions: S holds certain doctrinal propositions to be true
S participates regularly in communal worship
S belongs to an organised religious institution
counterexample: Consider a mystic living in solitude in the mountains, who holds no particular doctrinal propositions but who has an overwhelming sense of the presence of the divine, a profound trust in the goodness of ultimate reality, and a life wholly ordered by prayer and contemplation. She belongs to no religious institution and participates in no communal worship. The second and third conditions fail, and arguably the first as well, yet her faith is genuine and deep.
missing_condition: The definition reduces faith to institutional affiliation and doctrinal assent, when in truth faith may be a matter of personal trust, surrender, and orientation of the whole person towards the divine, independent of any institutional or doctrinal framework.
passage: Suppose one defines religious faith as follows: a person has religious faith if and only if that person holds certain doctrinal propositions to be true, participates regularly in communal worship, and belongs to an organised religious institution. The definition has some initial plausibility. The typical person of faith — a churchgoer, a member of a synagogue, a regular attendee at a mosque — holds certain doctrines to be true, worships in community, and belongs to a recognised religious body. The three conditions appear to identify what it is to have faith. Yet one must consider the solitary mystic. Throughout the history of religion there have been persons of undeniable faith who have withdrawn from all institutional religion — hermits, anchorites, wandering ascetics — and who have ordered their lives entirely around a direct, personal apprehension of the divine. Such a person may hold no doctrinal propositions in the ordinary sense; she may be indifferent to creeds and catechisms, finding in them at best imperfect expressions of a reality that exceeds all doctrinal formulation. She participates in no communal worship, for she lives in solitude. She belongs to no organised institution, having withdrawn from all such structures. The second and third conditions fail, and the first is at best doubtful. Yet no one who has encountered the literature of mysticism could deny that such persons possess faith — indeed, a faith of extraordinary depth and intensity. The definition has confused the sociological accompaniments of faith — institution, communal worship, doctrinal subscription — with faith itself, when in truth faith may be a matter of personal trust, surrender, and total orientation of the self towards the divine, quite independent of any institutional or doctrinal framework.

definition: An act A is an act of prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal address directed to God, (ii) A involves a petition for some benefit or favour, and (iii) A is performed in a posture of physical submission such as kneeling or prostration.
conditions: A is a verbal address directed to God
A involves a petition for some benefit or favour
A is performed in a posture of physical submission such as kneeling or prostration
counterexample: Consider the practice of contemplative prayer, in which the practitioner sits in silence, emptying the mind of all thoughts, images, and desires, seeking nothing but the wordless presence of God. No verbal address is made; no petition is offered; the posture is one of relaxed stillness rather than physical submission. All three conditions fail, yet contemplative prayer is prayer in the fullest sense — indeed, many spiritual writers regard it as the highest form of prayer.
missing_condition: The definition reduces prayer to petitionary verbal address in a submissive posture, when in truth prayer encompasses a vast range of practices including silent contemplation, wordless adoration, and meditative attention to the divine. What is essential to prayer is the intentional turning of the self towards God, not any particular form of words, petition, or bodily posture.
passage: One might propose that an act is an act of prayer if and only if it is a verbal address directed to God, involves a petition for some benefit or favour, and is performed in a posture of physical submission such as kneeling or prostration. The definition captures a familiar image of prayer: the supplicant kneels, speaks words addressed to God, and asks for something — health, forgiveness, guidance, the well-being of another. This is indeed prayer, and perhaps the most commonly practised form of prayer. Yet the tradition of Christian spirituality, to say nothing of the contemplative traditions of other religions, contains forms of prayer that differ radically from this picture. Consider the practice of contemplative or centering prayer, commended by spiritual masters from the author of the Cloud of Unknowing to the great Carmelite mystics. In this practice the one who prays sits in stillness and silence, relinquishing all thoughts, all images, all desires, all petitions, seeking nothing but the bare, wordless presence of God. No verbal address is made; the practitioner is silent. No petition is offered; the practitioner asks for nothing, not even for the experience of God's presence, but simply waits in receptive openness. The posture is not one of kneeling or prostration but of relaxed, alert stillness — often simply sitting. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet contemplative prayer is prayer, and many of the most authoritative spiritual writers have held it to be the highest and most authentic form of prayer, beside which petitionary prayer is a preliminary and relatively elementary exercise. The definition has mistaken one particular mode of prayer for the essence of prayer as such.

definition: Prayer is efficacious if and only if (i) the petitioner requests a specific outcome, (ii) the requested outcome occurs, and (iii) the outcome occurs within a definite period specified by the petitioner.
conditions: the petitioner requests a specific outcome
the requested outcome occurs
the outcome occurs within a definite period specified by the petitioner
counterexample: Consider a person who prays for the recovery of a sick friend without specifying any time-frame. The friend recovers, but only after many months. On the proposed definition, the prayer is not efficacious because no definite period was specified by the petitioner. Yet the person who prayed is fully convinced, and perhaps rightly so, that the prayer was answered. The third condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a temporal condition that is foreign to the actual practice of prayer. Prayer may be efficacious without the petitioner's having specified any deadline, and the absence of a time-frame does not diminish the connection between the prayer and the outcome.
passage: Suppose one holds that prayer is efficacious if and only if the petitioner requests a specific outcome, the requested outcome occurs, and the outcome occurs within a definite period specified by the petitioner. The definition aims to provide a clear, testable criterion for the efficacy of prayer: a prayer is answered if and only if the thing prayed for comes about within the time the petitioner has allowed. One might think such precision is necessary if claims about the efficacy of prayer are to be taken seriously, for without it any subsequent occurrence of the desired outcome could be counted as an answer to prayer, no matter how remote in time. Yet one must consider the practice of prayer as it is actually conducted by devout persons. A mother prays for the recovery of her gravely ill child. She does not specify a time-frame; she simply prays, with all the fervour of her heart, that the child may be restored to health. Months pass. The child's condition worsens, then stabilises, then gradually improves, until at last, after many months, the child is well. The mother regards her prayer as answered. She did not set a deadline, and the recovery did not occur within any period she specified — for she specified no period. The third condition fails. Yet if we grant that prayer can be efficacious at all, this seems a clear case of efficacious prayer. The definition has imported a requirement of temporal specificity that is foreign to the actual practice and phenomenology of petitionary prayer, in which the petitioner characteristically entrusts both the outcome and its timing to the divine will.

definition: An object O is sacred if and only if (i) O has been formally consecrated by a recognised religious authority, (ii) O is set apart from ordinary use, and (iii) O is housed within a designated place of worship.
conditions: O has been formally consecrated by a recognised religious authority
O is set apart from ordinary use
O is housed within a designated place of worship
counterexample: Consider a natural spring in a remote forest, regarded as sacred by the indigenous people of the region for centuries. No formal consecration has been performed by any recognised religious authority; the spring's sacredness is held to inhere in the place itself, not to have been conferred upon it by any human act. And the spring is not housed within any designated place of worship; it is in the open forest. The first and third conditions fail, yet the spring is sacred.
missing_condition: The definition makes sacredness a function of institutional acts and architectural settings, when in truth sacredness may be inherent in natural places and objects, apprehended rather than conferred, and wholly independent of institutional consecration or ecclesiastical housing.
passage: One might define the sacred as follows: an object is sacred if and only if it has been formally consecrated by a recognised religious authority, it is set apart from ordinary use, and it is housed within a designated place of worship. The definition accommodates many familiar cases. The consecrated host of the Eucharist is sacred; it has been consecrated by a priest, it is set apart from ordinary use, and it is housed within a church. The Torah scrolls are sacred; they are prepared according to strict religious law, set apart from profane handling, and kept within the synagogue. Yet one must consider the sacred places of the natural world — a spring in a remote forest, for instance, regarded as sacred by the indigenous people of a region for generations beyond memory. No formal act of consecration has been performed upon the spring by any recognised religious authority; the people hold that the spring's sacredness inheres in the place itself, that it is a point at which the divine or numinous manifests itself in the natural world. The spring has not been consecrated; its sacredness is apprehended, not conferred. And it is not housed within any designated place of worship; it lies in the open forest, exposed to the sky and the weather. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. Yet the spring is sacred — as sacred, to those who revere it, as any altar or sanctuary. The definition has taken the institutional model of the sacred, in which sacredness is conferred by authoritative human acts within built religious spaces, and treated it as exhaustive, thereby excluding the vast domain of natural and pre-institutional sacredness.

definition: A text T is a sacred text if and only if (i) T purports to be divinely authored or divinely inspired, (ii) T prescribes a code of moral and ritual conduct, and (iii) T contains narrative accounts of divine acts in history.
conditions: T purports to be divinely authored or divinely inspired
T prescribes a code of moral and ritual conduct
T contains narrative accounts of divine acts in history
counterexample: Consider the Tao Te Ching, attributed to Lao Tzu, which is regarded as a sacred text within the Taoist tradition. It does not purport to be divinely authored or divinely inspired; it presents itself as the reflections of a sage. It does not prescribe a code of ritual conduct, though it offers guidance of a broadly ethical and practical nature. And it contains no narrative accounts of divine acts in history. All three conditions fail, yet the Tao Te Ching is a sacred text.
missing_condition: The definition is modelled upon the Abrahamic scriptures and their distinctive features — divine authorship, moral and ritual law, salvation history — and treats these features as essential to sacred textuality. But sacred texts in other traditions may be attributed to human sages, may eschew ritual prescription, and may contain no historical narrative, yet remain no less sacred.
passage: Suppose one defines a sacred text as follows: a text is sacred if and only if it purports to be divinely authored or divinely inspired, it prescribes a code of moral and ritual conduct, and it contains narrative accounts of divine acts in history. The definition fits the major scriptures of the Abrahamic traditions rather well. The Torah purports to record the words of God, prescribes an elaborate code of moral and ritual conduct, and narrates God's acts in the history of Israel. The Qur'an is understood by Muslims to be the literal speech of God, dictated to the Prophet, and it prescribes both moral and ritual duties whilst recounting the deeds of God among the nations. Yet one must consider the Tao Te Ching. This brief, enigmatic text is regarded as sacred within the Taoist tradition; it is chanted in temples, studied by monks, and treated with a reverence comparable to that accorded to any scripture. Yet it does not purport to be divinely authored or inspired; it presents itself as the reflections of a sage — a human being of extraordinary insight, but a human being nonetheless. It does not prescribe a code of ritual conduct; it offers, rather, a series of meditations on the nature of the Tao and the way of living in harmony with it. And it contains no narrative accounts of divine acts in history; it is not a historical document at all but a work of philosophical and mystical reflection. All three conditions fail. The definition has been constructed from the features of one particular family of sacred texts — the Abrahamic scriptures — and has mistaken features peculiar to that family for necessary conditions of sacred textuality as such.

definition: A communication C is a divine revelation if and only if (i) C is propositional in form — that is, it consists of statements that are either true or false, (ii) C is communicated by God to a human recipient, and (iii) C discloses information that the recipient could not have obtained by natural means.
conditions: C is propositional in form — that is, it consists of statements that are either true or false
C is communicated by God to a human recipient
C discloses information that the recipient could not have obtained by natural means
counterexample: Consider the experience of Moses before the burning bush. What is revealed, according to the narrative, is not primarily a set of propositions but a presence — the presence of God, terrible and holy, before which Moses hides his face. The revelation is God's self-disclosure, an encounter with the divine reality, not the communication of propositional information. The first condition fails: the revelation is not propositional in form but experiential and personal. Yet it is a paradigm case of divine revelation.
missing_condition: The definition reduces revelation to the communication of propositional information, when in truth revelation may take the form of personal encounter, self-disclosure, or the manifestation of a presence that exceeds all propositional formulation. What is essential to revelation is God's making himself known, not the delivery of statements.
passage: One might propose that a communication is a divine revelation if and only if it is propositional in form — consisting of statements that are either true or false — it is communicated by God to a human recipient, and it discloses information that the recipient could not have obtained by natural means. The definition captures a certain model of revelation: God tells a human being something, and the content of what is told can be expressed in propositions. On this view, revelation is essentially the disclosure of truths — truths about God's nature, God's purposes, the moral law, or the future course of events. Yet one must consider the experience of Moses before the burning bush, as recounted in the book of Exodus. Moses approaches the bush and is told to remove his sandals, for the ground on which he stands is holy. What follows is an encounter with the divine presence — a presence so overwhelming that Moses hides his face, afraid to look upon God. Certain propositions are communicated in the course of the encounter — God's name, God's intention to deliver Israel — but the heart of the revelation is not propositional. It is the self-disclosure of God as a living presence, a reality of holiness and power that exceeds anything that can be captured in statements. Many theologians have held that revelation is fundamentally personal rather than propositional — that what God reveals is not a set of truths but himself, and that the propositional content of revelation is secondary to and derivative from this primary act of personal self-disclosure. The first condition of the definition fails in such cases, yet the encounter is paradigmatically revelatory.

definition: A putative revelation R is genuine if and only if (i) R is consistent with previously established revelations, (ii) R is confirmed by miraculous signs accompanying its delivery, and (iii) R is accepted as genuine by the established religious community.
conditions: R is consistent with previously established revelations
R is confirmed by miraculous signs accompanying its delivery
R is accepted as genuine by the established religious community
counterexample: Consider the preaching of the Hebrew prophets, who frequently delivered revelations that contradicted the prevailing religious consensus. Amos declared that God despised the festivals and sacrifices of Israel; Jeremiah prophesied the destruction of the Temple, the very centre of established worship. Their revelations were not accepted by the established religious community — indeed, the prophets were persecuted precisely because their message was rejected. The third condition fails, yet the prophetic revelations are now regarded as genuine.
missing_condition: The definition makes communal acceptance a criterion of genuine revelation, when in truth genuine revelation may be rejected by the established community. The history of prophetic religion is replete with cases in which the authentic voice of God was opposed by the very community to which it was addressed.
passage: Suppose one holds that a putative revelation is genuine if and only if it is consistent with previously established revelations, it is confirmed by miraculous signs accompanying its delivery, and it is accepted as genuine by the established religious community. The definition reflects a conservative principle of theological discernment: a genuine revelation will cohere with what has been revealed before, will be attested by signs, and will win the recognition of those best placed to judge — the community of faith. Yet one must consider the case of the Hebrew prophets. The prophets of Israel frequently delivered messages that were sharply at odds with the prevailing religious consensus. Amos declared that God had no delight in the solemn assemblies and burnt offerings of Israel, a message that contradicted the established cultic practice. Jeremiah prophesied the destruction of the Temple itself — an utterance so shocking that he was arrested and nearly put to death. The prophetic revelations were not accepted by the established religious community; they were rejected, and the prophets were persecuted, imprisoned, and in some cases killed. The third condition fails. Yet these same prophetic utterances are now almost universally regarded as genuine revelations — indeed, as among the most important revelations in the history of Western religion. The definition has elevated communal consensus to the status of a necessary condition of genuine revelation, when the history of prophecy demonstrates that authentic revelation may be precisely that which the established community resists, rejects, and condemns.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by God, (ii) the command is explicitly stated in a sacred text, and (iii) A is practically possible for the agent.
conditions: A is commanded by God
the command is explicitly stated in a sacred text
A is practically possible for the agent
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who encounters a stranger in desperate need and feels a powerful moral obligation to render assistance. No sacred text explicitly commands this particular act of assistance in these particular circumstances; the obligation arises from the immediate moral demand of the situation. Yet the obligation is real, and a divine command theorist might well hold that God commands such acts through the deliverances of conscience rather than through explicit scriptural statement. The second condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition restricts divine commands to those explicitly stated in sacred texts, when in truth God may command through conscience, through the natural moral law, or through the demands of particular situations. The channels of the divine will are not limited to the written word.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory if and only if it is commanded by God, the command is explicitly stated in a sacred text, and the action is practically possible for the agent. The definition has a certain appealing clarity: one determines one's moral obligations by consulting the sacred text, identifying the divine commands contained therein, and acting upon those commands insofar as they are practically feasible. The third condition guards against the absurdity of being obligated to do the impossible. Yet one must consider the innumerable situations of moral life that are not addressed by any sacred text. A traveller encounters a stranger lying injured by the roadside. She feels a powerful moral obligation to stop and render assistance. But no sacred text explicitly commands her to assist this particular stranger in these particular circumstances. The sacred texts may command charity and compassion in general terms, but they do not and cannot anticipate every situation in which these virtues are to be exercised. The second condition fails: there is no explicit scriptural command covering this case. Yet the obligation is real, and many theologians within the divine command tradition would readily affirm that God commands such acts — not through the letter of scripture but through the deliverances of conscience, through the natural moral law written upon the heart, through the immediate moral demand of the situation itself. The definition has restricted the channels of divine command to a single medium — the sacred text — when in truth the divine will may be communicated through many channels, and a morally sensitive person may discern God's commands in the exigencies of lived experience.

definition: An ethical theory T is a divine command theory if and only if (i) T holds that moral properties are constituted by the commands of God, (ii) T maintains that God's commands are arbitrary — that is, not grounded in any prior reasons, and (iii) T entails that in the absence of God there would be no moral truths.
conditions: T holds that moral properties are constituted by the commands of God
T maintains that God's commands are arbitrary — that is, not grounded in any prior reasons
T entails that in the absence of God there would be no moral truths
counterexample: Consider a theory that holds moral properties to be constituted by the commands of God, but maintains that God's commands are grounded in God's perfectly good nature — that God commands what is right because God's nature is the standard of goodness, not because the commands are arbitrary. Such a theory is a divine command theory, and it entails that in the absence of God there would be no moral truths. Yet the second condition fails: the commands are not arbitrary but are grounded in the divine nature.
missing_condition: The definition requires that divine commands be arbitrary, but many sophisticated divine command theories ground God's commands in the divine nature, holding that God necessarily commands in accordance with perfect goodness. The arbitrariness of divine commands is a feature of crude voluntarism, not of divine command theory as such.
passage: Suppose one defines a divine command theory as follows: an ethical theory is a divine command theory if and only if it holds that moral properties are constituted by the commands of God, it maintains that God's commands are arbitrary — not grounded in any prior reasons — and it entails that in the absence of God there would be no moral truths. The definition identifies three putatively essential features: constitutivism about moral properties and divine commands, the arbitrariness of those commands, and the dependence of moral truth upon God's existence. The first and third conditions are relatively uncontroversial as features of divine command theories. But the second condition — that God's commands are arbitrary — is deeply problematic. Many of the most careful and philosophically sophisticated proponents of divine command ethics have strenuously denied that God's commands are arbitrary. They hold, rather, that God's commands flow from God's perfectly good nature, and that God commands what is right because God is essentially good, loving, and just. The commands are not arbitrary decrees issued without reason; they are expressions of a nature that is the ultimate standard of goodness. The second condition of the definition fails for such theories. Yet they are divine command theories: they hold that moral properties are constituted by divine commands and that moral truth depends upon God. The definition has confused divine command theory with crude voluntarism — the view that God's commands are sheer acts of will unconstrained by any standard — when in truth the most defensible versions of the theory ground the commands in the divine nature.

definition: A person S is immortal if and only if (i) S's soul survives the death of the body, (ii) S retains personal identity — that is, memories, character, and self-awareness — after death, and (iii) S's post-mortem existence is conscious and uninterrupted.
conditions: S's soul survives the death of the body
S retains personal identity — that is, memories, character, and self-awareness — after death
S's post-mortem existence is conscious and uninterrupted
counterexample: Consider the doctrine of bodily resurrection affirmed by many Christian theologians, according to which the person does not survive death as a disembodied soul but is raised to new life in a transformed body at the end of time. On this view, there is a gap — a period between death and resurrection during which the person does not exist in any conscious state. The third condition fails: the post-mortem existence is not uninterrupted. Yet the person is immortal in the theologically relevant sense: death is not the end, and the person lives again.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous conscious existence after death, but immortality may involve a gap between death and resurrection. What is essential to immortality is that death is not the final annihilation of the person, not that the person's existence is temporally continuous.
passage: One might propose that a person is immortal if and only if that person's soul survives the death of the body, the person retains personal identity after death, and the person's post-mortem existence is conscious and uninterrupted. The definition captures a particular picture of immortality: the soul, bearing the person's memories, character, and self-awareness, passes from the body at death and continues in an unbroken conscious existence thereafter. This is the picture one finds in certain strands of Platonism and in popular religious imagination. Yet one must consider the doctrine of bodily resurrection as affirmed within the Christian theological tradition. On this view, the person does not survive death as a disembodied soul enjoying continuous conscious existence. Rather, the person dies — wholly and genuinely dies — and is raised to new life in a transformed body at the general resurrection at the end of time. Between death and resurrection there is, on many accounts, a gap: a period during which the person simply does not exist in any conscious state. The person who is raised is the same person who died — personal identity is restored — but the existence is not continuous. There has been an interruption, perhaps of vast duration. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the person who is raised from the dead is immortal in the theologically significant sense: death has not had the last word, and the person lives again in a new and glorified mode of existence. The definition has mistaken one model of immortality — the Platonic model of the soul's continuous survival — for the concept of immortality itself, thereby excluding the resurrection model that is arguably more central to Christian theology.

definition: The doctrine of immortality is coherent if and only if (i) personal identity can be maintained without a physical body, (ii) disembodied mental states are logically possible, and (iii) the soul is a simple, indivisible substance incapable of dissolution.
conditions: personal identity can be maintained without a physical body
disembodied mental states are logically possible
the soul is a simple, indivisible substance incapable of dissolution
counterexample: Consider a dualist who holds that the soul is not a simple substance but a complex entity — a structured unity of capacities, dispositions, and powers — and yet maintains that it survives death because God sustains it in being. On this view, the soul is divisible in principle but preserved from dissolution by divine power. The third condition fails, yet the doctrine of immortality remains coherent for this thinker.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the soul be a simple, indivisible substance, but the coherence of immortality may rest upon divine sustenance rather than upon the metaphysical simplicity of the soul. A complex soul may be immortal if God preserves it from dissolution.
passage: Suppose one holds that the doctrine of immortality is coherent if and only if personal identity can be maintained without a physical body, disembodied mental states are logically possible, and the soul is a simple, indivisible substance incapable of dissolution. The third condition reflects the classical Platonic argument: what is simple cannot be decomposed, and what cannot be decomposed cannot perish; therefore the soul, being simple, is immortal. The argument has a venerable history and a certain logical elegance. Yet one must consider the position of a dualist who holds that the soul is not a simple substance. On this view, the soul is a complex entity — a structured unity of cognitive, volitional, and affective powers, each distinguishable from the others. Such a soul is, in principle, divisible: its components might come apart, and the soul might thereby cease to exist. The third condition of the definition fails: the soul is not a simple, indivisible substance. Yet this thinker maintains that the soul survives death, and does so coherently, by appealing not to the soul's metaphysical simplicity but to the sustaining power of God. God, being omnipotent and benevolent, preserves the soul in being after death, holding its complex structure together by an act of continuous divine will. The coherence of immortality, on this account, rests not upon the soul's intrinsic indestructibility but upon the faithfulness and power of God. The definition has taken one particular metaphysical ground for the soul's survival — its alleged simplicity — and treated it as a necessary condition of the doctrine's coherence, when in truth the coherence of immortality may rest upon quite different foundations.

definition: An experience E is a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sensory perception of a divine being, (ii) E is accompanied by intense emotion, and (iii) E produces a lasting moral transformation in the subject.
conditions: E involves a vivid sensory perception of a divine being
E is accompanied by intense emotion
E produces a lasting moral transformation in the subject
counterexample: Consider a contemplative monk who, after years of disciplined prayer, experiences what he describes as a subtle, quiet awareness of the divine presence — not a vivid sensory perception but a gentle, almost imperceptible sense of being held in the presence of God. The experience is accompanied not by intense emotion but by a deep, still peace. And while it reinforces his existing moral commitments, it does not produce a transformation, for he was already living a life of exemplary virtue. The first and third conditions fail, and arguably the second as well, yet the experience is genuinely religious.
missing_condition: The definition requires vivid sensory perception and moral transformation, but religious experience may be subtle, quiet, and non-sensory, and it may confirm rather than transform the moral life of a person already advanced in virtue. What is essential is the apprehension of a transcendent reality, however quiet or subtle that apprehension may be.
passage: One might propose that an experience is a genuine religious experience if and only if it involves a vivid sensory perception of a divine being, it is accompanied by intense emotion, and it produces a lasting moral transformation in the subject. The definition captures certain dramatic cases: the conversion of Paul on the road to Damascus, with its blinding light and commanding voice; the visions of the saints, accompanied by ecstasy and tears; the sudden moral reversals of persons whose lives are transformed in an instant by an encounter with the divine. These are among the most frequently discussed examples of religious experience, and all three conditions are satisfied in each case. Yet one must consider the experience of the contemplative monk who, after decades of silent prayer, arrives at a state in which he is quietly and continuously aware of the presence of God — not through any vivid sensory perception, but through a subtle, interior sense of being held, sustained, and accompanied by a reality that transcends all sensory apprehension. The experience is not accompanied by intense emotion; it is characterised by a deep, still peace that is almost the opposite of emotional intensity. And it does not produce a moral transformation, for the monk has been living a life of disciplined virtue for many years; the experience confirms and deepens his existing moral commitments without altering them. The first condition fails, the second is at best doubtful, and the third fails. Yet the monk's experience is genuinely religious — indeed, the contemplative tradition regards such quiet, sustained awareness of the divine as a higher and more mature form of religious experience than the dramatic visions and conversions upon which the definition is modelled.

definition: An experience E has evidential value for the existence of God if and only if (i) E is reported by a psychologically healthy individual, (ii) E is corroborated by the independent reports of others who have had similar experiences, and (iii) E is consistent with an established theological framework.
conditions: E is reported by a psychologically healthy individual
E is corroborated by the independent reports of others who have had similar experiences
E is consistent with an established theological framework
counterexample: Consider a person living in a culture with no established theological framework who, in a moment of crisis, has an overwhelming experience of a transcendent, personal presence — an experience that transforms her life and that she struggles to articulate, since her culture provides no concepts or categories for what she has encountered. Her experience is not consistent with any established theological framework, for she has none. The third condition fails, yet if religious experience can have evidential value at all, this experience has it.
missing_condition: The definition makes evidential value dependent upon consistency with an established theological framework, but a religious experience may have evidential value precisely because it introduces the experiencer to a reality for which no prior framework exists. The evidential force of an experience does not depend upon its fitting neatly within pre-existing theological categories.
passage: Suppose one holds that an experience has evidential value for the existence of God if and only if it is reported by a psychologically healthy individual, it is corroborated by independent reports of similar experiences, and it is consistent with an established theological framework. The definition aims to distinguish evidentially significant religious experiences from those that are delusory, idiosyncratic, or pathological. The requirement of psychological health excludes hallucinations born of illness; the requirement of corroboration guards against private delusion; and the requirement of theological consistency ensures that the experience fits within a coherent religious understanding. Yet one must consider a person living in a culture with no established theological framework — an isolated community, perhaps, with no developed theology, no tradition of religious reflection, no inherited categories for the divine. In a moment of great crisis, this person has an experience of overwhelming power: a sense of being addressed by a personal, transcendent presence of infinite goodness and authority. The experience is unlike anything in her prior experience or in the experience of anyone she knows. She is psychologically healthy, and the experience is not the product of illness or disturbance. But it is not corroborated by the independent reports of others in her community, for no one else has had such an experience. And it is not consistent with any established theological framework, for her culture has none. The second and third conditions fail. Yet if religious experience has evidential value at all — if the occurrence of an experience as of God is evidence for the existence of God — then this experience has evidential value. Its force does not depend upon whether others have had similar experiences or whether a theological framework exists to accommodate it.

definition: The design argument for the existence of God is cogent if and only if (i) the universe exhibits features that are best explained by intelligent design, (ii) no naturalistic explanation of those features is available, and (iii) the designer inferred is a single, personal God.
conditions: the universe exhibits features that are best explained by intelligent design
no naturalistic explanation of those features is available
the designer inferred is a single, personal God
counterexample: Consider a version of the design argument that acknowledges the availability of naturalistic explanations for particular features of the universe — the evolution of complex organisms by natural selection, for instance — yet maintains that the overall framework of natural law within which such processes operate is itself best explained by intelligent design. On this view, a naturalistic explanation is available for the specific features, yet the argument from design remains cogent at a deeper level. The second condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition requires the complete absence of naturalistic explanations, but the design argument may be cogent even where naturalistic explanations exist for particular phenomena, provided that the broader framework presupposed by those explanations is itself in need of a design-based account. The argument shifts from particular features to the framework of natural law itself.
passage: One might hold that the design argument for the existence of God is cogent if and only if the universe exhibits features best explained by intelligent design, no naturalistic explanation of those features is available, and the designer inferred is a single, personal God. The second condition reflects a common view: the design argument is persuasive only where naturalistic explanations fail, and the advance of natural science progressively undermines the argument by supplying naturalistic accounts of phenomena previously attributed to design. Yet one must consider a more sophisticated version of the argument. Suppose one concedes that natural selection provides an adequate explanation of the complexity and adaptation of living organisms, and that physics provides adequate explanations of the formation of stars, galaxies, and planetary systems. Naturalistic explanations are available for these features. But suppose one then observes that natural selection and physics operate within a framework of natural law whose precise form is not itself explained by any naturalistic account — that the laws of nature are finely tuned for the possibility of complexity, life, and consciousness, and that this fine-tuning is best explained by intelligent design. The design argument, on this view, is not undermined by the availability of naturalistic explanations for particular features; it is relocated to a deeper level, where it addresses the framework within which naturalistic explanations operate. The second condition of the definition fails: naturalistic explanations are available for the particular features. Yet the design argument retains its cogency, directed now at the framework of natural law itself. The definition has confused the absence of all naturalistic explanation with the cogency of the design argument, when in truth the argument may be cogent precisely in the domain where naturalistic explanation reaches its limits.

definition: An agent S is morally praiseworthy for performing action A if and only if (i) A is commanded by God, (ii) S performs A because S recognises it as divinely commanded, and (iii) S performs A solely from the motive of obedience to God.
conditions: A is commanded by God
S performs A because S recognises it as divinely commanded
S performs A solely from the motive of obedience to God
counterexample: Consider a person who gives generously to the poor because she is moved by compassion for their suffering. She is also a devout theist who recognises that charity is divinely commanded, and this recognition plays some role in her motivation. But her primary motive is compassion, not obedience. The third condition fails: she does not act solely from the motive of obedience to God. Yet she is morally praiseworthy — indeed, many would say more praiseworthy than a person who gives solely from obedience without any fellow-feeling.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the sole motive be obedience to divine command, but moral praiseworthiness may be enhanced rather than diminished when the agent acts from compassion, love, or other intrinsically good motives in addition to obedience. A richer motivational structure does not undermine praiseworthiness.
passage: Suppose one holds that an agent is morally praiseworthy for performing an action if and only if the action is commanded by God, the agent performs it because she recognises it as divinely commanded, and she performs it solely from the motive of obedience to God. The definition reflects a rigorously theocentric conception of moral worth: the praiseworthy agent is the one who acts from obedience to God and from that motive alone. The purity of the motive — its exclusive orientation towards the divine will — is what confers moral worth upon the action. Yet one must consider the case of a generous woman who gives freely to the poor. She is a devout person and she recognises that charity is divinely commanded; this recognition plays a genuine role in her motivation. But her primary motive is compassion. She is moved by the suffering of those she helps; she feels their need as a claim upon her, and she responds with a generosity that flows from the depths of her sympathetic nature. She does not act solely from obedience; she acts from a complex of motives in which compassion is predominant and obedience is one element among others. The third condition fails. Yet her action is morally praiseworthy. Indeed, many moralists would hold that she is more praiseworthy than a person who gives to the poor solely because God commands it, without feeling any compassion whatsoever. The definition has elevated one motive — obedience — to the exclusion of all others, when in truth the morally praiseworthy act may spring from a rich motivational soil in which obedience, compassion, love, and a sense of solidarity are all present and mutually reinforcing.

definition: The hope for immortality is rational if and only if (i) there exists empirical evidence of survival after death, (ii) the concept of disembodied existence is free from logical contradiction, and (iii) the person hoping for immortality has a coherent account of personal identity that permits survival.
conditions: there exists empirical evidence of survival after death
the concept of disembodied existence is free from logical contradiction
the person hoping for immortality has a coherent account of personal identity that permits survival
counterexample: Consider a person who hopes for immortality not on the basis of empirical evidence — she concedes that no such evidence is forthcoming — but on the basis of a deep trust in the goodness and faithfulness of God, whom she takes to be the sort of being who would not allow the persons he has created and loves to perish utterly. Her hope is grounded in a theological conviction about the character of God, not in empirical data. The first condition fails, yet her hope is rational — it is grounded in reasons, even if those reasons are theological rather than empirical.
missing_condition: The definition requires empirical evidence for the rationality of the hope for immortality, but such hope may be rationally grounded in theological considerations — in convictions about the nature and goodness of God — even in the complete absence of empirical evidence. Rationality admits of broader grounds than the empirical.
passage: One might propose that the hope for immortality is rational if and only if there exists empirical evidence of survival after death, the concept of disembodied existence is free from logical contradiction, and the person hoping for immortality has a coherent account of personal identity that permits survival. The definition imposes three conditions, of which the first is the most demanding: it requires empirical evidence that persons survive death. One might think of reports of near-death experiences, or of communications purportedly received from the dead, as candidates for such evidence. Yet many thoughtful persons who hope for immortality are entirely sceptical of such reports. Consider a woman of deep religious conviction who hopes for life after death. She is trained in the natural sciences and is well aware that no empirical evidence of survival after death has been established to the satisfaction of the scientific community. She does not base her hope on near-death experiences, which she regards as neurological phenomena, or on séance reports, which she dismisses as fraudulent or delusory. Her hope is grounded, rather, in a theological conviction: she holds that God is perfectly good and perfectly loving, and that a perfectly good and loving God would not create persons capable of communion with himself only to allow them to be annihilated at death. Her hope is rational, she maintains, because it is grounded in a coherent and well-considered understanding of the divine character. The first condition of the definition fails: she has no empirical evidence. Yet her hope is not irrational; it is grounded in reasons — theological reasons — that she can articulate and defend. The definition has restricted the grounds of rational hope to the empirical, when in truth the rationality of hope may rest upon moral, theological, or metaphysical considerations that are not empirical in character.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition P if and only if (i) S believes P to be true, (ii) S lacks sufficient evidence for P, and (iii) S's belief in P is contrary to the available evidence.
conditions: S believes P to be true
S lacks sufficient evidence for P
S's belief is contrary to the available evidence
counterexample: Consider the faith of a devout person who believes in the goodness of God. She does not lack evidence entirely; she points to the beauty of the natural world, to the testimony of the saints, and to her own experience of grace. Her belief is not contrary to the available evidence; she acknowledges the problem of evil but holds that the evidence, on balance, supports rather than undermines her conviction. She would say that she has faith in the goodness of God not because the evidence is against her but because the evidence, whilst not conclusive, is sufficient to sustain a trust that goes beyond what the evidence alone can demonstrate. The second and third conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition characterises faith as belief against the evidence, but faith may involve belief that goes beyond the evidence without being contrary to it. What is wanted is an account that distinguishes faith from mere credulity and recognises that faith may be a trusting commitment that exceeds, but does not contradict, the available evidence.
passage: One might propose that a person has faith in a proposition if and only if the person believes it to be true, lacks sufficient evidence for it, and holds the belief contrary to the available evidence. The definition characterises faith as essentially irrational — a matter of believing what the evidence tells against. This conception has a certain currency; one encounters it in popular polemic and in certain philosophical treatments. Yet one must consider the faith of a thoughtful and devout person who believes in the goodness of God. She does not believe in God's goodness in a vacuum of evidence. She points to the beauty and order of the natural world, which she takes to reflect a benevolent creator. She appeals to the testimony of the saints and mystics, whose writings she has studied with care. She draws upon her own experience of what she regards as divine grace — moments of consolation in grief, of strength in adversity, of peace that surpasses understanding. She acknowledges the problem of evil; she is aware of the suffering of the innocent and does not dismiss it. But she holds that the evidence, taken as a whole, supports rather than undermines her conviction. Her faith is not contrary to the available evidence; it is a trusting commitment that goes beyond what the evidence can demonstratively establish, but it does not fly in the face of the evidence. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has confused faith with credulity, when in truth faith may be a form of trust that exceeds the evidence without contradicting it — a commitment sustained by reasons that are genuinely felt to be adequate even if they fall short of proof.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E violates a known law of nature, (ii) E is witnessed by a large number of credible observers, and (iii) E is immediately preceded by a prayer or invocation addressed to God.
conditions: E violates a known law of nature
E is witnessed by a large number of credible observers
E is immediately preceded by a prayer or invocation addressed to God
counterexample: Consider the conversion of St Paul on the road to Damascus. Paul experienced a blinding light and heard a voice that he identified as that of Christ. The event transformed his life and, through his subsequent missionary activity, the history of the Western world. Those within the Christian tradition regard it as a miracle. Yet the event was not witnessed by a large number of credible observers; only Paul's travelling companions were present, and by some accounts they perceived the light but did not hear the voice. It was not preceded by a prayer or invocation; Paul was travelling to persecute Christians, not to seek divine favour. The second and third conditions fail, yet the event is regarded as one of the most important miracles in Christian history.
missing_condition: The definition requires public attestation and a preceding invocation, but miracles may be private, unexpected, and unsought. What is wanted is an account that locates the essence of the miraculous in divine agency rather than in the circumstances of human observation and petition.
passage: Suppose one holds that an event is a miracle if and only if it violates a known law of nature, is witnessed by a large number of credible observers, and is immediately preceded by a prayer or invocation addressed to God. The definition captures a certain paradigm of the miraculous: the public, dramatic, petitioned wonder — the parting of the sea, the stilling of the storm. Yet one must consider the conversion of St Paul on the road to Damascus. Paul — then Saul, a persecutor of the early Christians — was travelling to Damascus to arrest followers of Christ when he was struck down by a blinding light and heard a voice that he identified as that of the risen Christ. The experience transformed him utterly; he became the most energetic and influential of the early Christian missionaries, and his epistles shaped the theology of the Church for centuries. The event is regarded within the Christian tradition as a miracle of the first importance. Yet it was not witnessed by a large number of credible observers. Paul's travelling companions were present, but the accounts differ as to what they perceived; by one report they heard the voice but saw no one, by another they saw the light but did not hear the voice. The event was not preceded by a prayer or invocation addressed to God. Paul was not seeking divine favour; he was engaged in an enterprise hostile to the followers of Christ. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The definition has treated the public, petitioned miracle as the paradigm and has excluded the private, unexpected irruption of the divine into a human life — precisely the kind of miracle that many believers regard as most transformative.

definition: An object O is sacred if and only if (i) O has been formally consecrated by an authorised religious official, (ii) O is set apart from ordinary use and housed in a designated sacred space, and (iii) O is an artefact — that is, a product of human manufacture.
conditions: O has been formally consecrated by an authorised religious official
O is set apart from ordinary use and housed in a designated sacred space
O is an artefact — a product of human manufacture
counterexample: Consider a sacred spring — a natural source of water regarded by the surrounding community as holy, as a place where the divine is peculiarly present. Such springs are found in many religious traditions; pilgrims visit them, prayers are offered beside them, and their waters are believed to possess healing powers. The spring has not been formally consecrated by an authorised official; it is regarded as sacred by virtue of its own character, perhaps because of an ancient theophany believed to have occurred there. It is not housed in a designated sacred space; it is the space itself. And it is not an artefact; it is a natural phenomenon, not a product of human manufacture. All three conditions fail, yet the spring is regarded as sacred by those who venerate it.
missing_condition: The definition restricts sacredness to consecrated artefacts housed in sacred spaces, but natural objects and places may be regarded as sacred without formal consecration and without being products of human manufacture. What is wanted is an account that recognises that sacredness may inhere in nature as well as in culture.
passage: One might propose that an object is sacred if and only if it has been formally consecrated by an authorised religious official, it is set apart from ordinary use and housed in a designated sacred space, and it is an artefact — a product of human manufacture. The definition captures many familiar cases. The communion vessels of a Christian church are consecrated by a bishop, set apart from ordinary use, and housed in the sanctuary. The Torah scroll is a manufactured object, treated with elaborate reverence, and kept in the ark of a synagogue. Yet one must consider the case of a sacred spring. In many religious traditions, natural springs are regarded as holy places — sites where the divine is peculiarly present, where pilgrims come to pray and to seek healing. The waters of such a spring may be believed to possess curative powers; the site itself may be the object of annual processions and acts of devotion. Yet the spring has not been formally consecrated by an authorised religious official. It is regarded as sacred not by virtue of any ceremony performed over it but by virtue of its own character — because of an ancient tradition associating it with a divine manifestation, or because of the experience of holiness reported by those who visit it. It is not set apart from ordinary use and housed in a sacred space; it is itself the sacred space, and its waters flow freely. And it is not an artefact; it is a natural phenomenon, a product of geological processes rather than of human manufacture. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has confined sacredness to the domain of consecrated artefacts, when in truth the sense of the sacred may attach to natural objects and places that have received no human consecration and owe their holiness to no human act.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) God has explicitly commanded A in a sacred text, (ii) the command is unambiguous — admitting of only one interpretation, and (iii) the command is addressed to all persons without exception.
conditions: God has explicitly commanded A in a sacred text
the command is unambiguous — admitting of only one interpretation
the command is addressed to all persons without exception
counterexample: Consider the obligation of charity. Most theistic traditions hold that charity towards the poor is morally obligatory. Yet in many sacred texts, the command to be charitable is not explicit in the form of a single, unambiguous directive; it is conveyed through parables, exhortations, exemplary narratives, and the general tenor of the text. The precise scope and manner of charitable obligation are matters of ongoing interpretive dispute. And certain commands concerning charity — the tithe, for instance — may be addressed specifically to members of a particular community rather than to all persons without exception. The second and third conditions fail, yet the obligation of charity is among the most firmly established moral requirements in the theistic traditions.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit, unambiguous, universal commands, but moral obligations grounded in divine authority may be conveyed through narrative, parable, and the cumulative witness of a tradition rather than through explicit directives. What is wanted is an account that recognises the interpretive richness of sacred texts rather than reducing divine command to unambiguous decree.
passage: Suppose one holds that an action is morally obligatory if and only if God has explicitly commanded it in a sacred text, the command is unambiguous, and the command is addressed to all persons without exception. The definition has the merit of clarity: one consults the text, identifies the command, and acts accordingly. Yet one must consider the obligation of charity. Most theistic traditions hold, with considerable firmness, that charity towards the poor is morally obligatory — that one is bound, by divine command, to give to those in need. But in many sacred texts, the command to be charitable is not delivered as a single, explicit, unambiguous directive. It is conveyed through the parable of the Good Samaritan, through the denunciations of the prophets against those who oppress the poor, through the exemplary generosity of saints and patriarchs, and through the general tenor of the scriptures as a whole. The precise scope of the obligation — how much one must give, to whom, under what circumstances — is a matter of ongoing interpretive dispute among theologians and jurists. The command is not unambiguous. Moreover, certain specific injunctions concerning charitable giving — the command to tithe, for instance, in the Hebrew scriptures — may be addressed to the members of a particular community rather than to all persons without exception. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has reduced divine command to explicit, unambiguous decree, when in truth the moral authority of sacred texts operates through a far richer range of communicative modes — parable, exhortation, narrative, prophetic denunciation — that resist reduction to a set of clear-cut, universally addressed imperatives.

definition: An experience E qualifies as a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sensory perception — a vision, a voice, or a tactile sensation — attributable to the divine, (ii) E occurs during an act of formal worship, and (iii) E is accompanied by an overwhelming emotion of fear or awe.
conditions: E involves a vivid sensory perception attributable to the divine
E occurs during an act of formal worship
E is accompanied by an overwhelming emotion of fear or awe
counterexample: Consider the experience reported by many contemplatives of a quiet, sustained sense of the presence of God, arising not during formal worship but in the course of ordinary daily activity — whilst walking in a garden, perhaps, or sitting in silence. The experience involves no vivid sensory perception; there is no vision, no voice, no tactile sensation. It does not occur during formal worship; it comes unbidden, in the midst of the mundane. And it is not accompanied by fear or awe but by a gentle, pervasive peace. Yet contemplatives within many traditions regard such experiences as among the most profound and authentic encounters with the divine.
missing_condition: The definition restricts genuine religious experience to dramatic, sensory, liturgical episodes accompanied by fear or awe, but religious experience may be quiet, non-sensory, and arise outside formal worship. What is wanted is an account that encompasses the contemplative experience of divine presence as well as the dramatic theophany.
passage: One might hold that an experience qualifies as a genuine religious experience if and only if it involves a vivid sensory perception attributable to the divine, it occurs during an act of formal worship, and it is accompanied by an overwhelming emotion of fear or awe. The definition captures the dramatic theophany: Moses before the burning bush, Isaiah in the Temple, Arjuna confronted by the universal form of Krishna. These are experiences of overwhelming sensory vividness, set within contexts of worship, and accompanied by the deepest awe. Yet one must consider the experience reported by many contemplatives — Christian, Jewish, Muslim, Hindu — of a quiet, sustained awareness of the presence of God that arises not during formal worship but in the course of ordinary activity. A woman walks in her garden on a spring morning and becomes aware, with a certainty she cannot explain, that she is in the presence of God. There is no vision, no voice, no tactile sensation — nothing that could be called a vivid sensory perception. The experience does not occur during formal worship; she is not in a church or at prayer. And the emotion that accompanies it is not fear or awe but a gentle, pervasive peace — a sense of being held, of resting in something infinitely good. Yet contemplatives within many traditions regard such experiences as among the most authentic and transformative encounters with the divine. The definition has taken the dramatic sensory theophany as the paradigm and has excluded the quiet, non-sensory, contemplative awareness that constitutes, for many believers, the deepest form of religious experience.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal address directed to God, (ii) A is performed at a designated time and place set aside for worship, and (iii) A employs a fixed liturgical formula prescribed by a religious authority.
conditions: A is a verbal address directed to God
A is performed at a designated time and place set aside for worship
A employs a fixed liturgical formula prescribed by a religious authority
counterexample: Consider the prayer of a mother who, upon learning that her child is gravely ill, falls to her knees in the hospital corridor and pours out her heart to God in words that are entirely her own — unscripted, unrehearsed, and inarticulate with grief. She is not at a designated place of worship; she is in a hospital. She does not employ a fixed liturgical formula; her words are spontaneous and formless. One might even question whether her prayer is, in the strictest sense, verbal; much of it consists in sighs, tears, and an inward reaching towards God that resists articulation. Yet her prayer is as genuine as any prayer can be — perhaps more genuine, in its raw sincerity, than any formulaic recitation.
missing_condition: The definition restricts genuine prayer to verbal, liturgical, and institutionally situated acts, but prayer may be wordless, spontaneous, and offered in any place and at any time. What is wanted is an account that recognises the interior and spontaneous dimensions of prayer rather than confining it to formal, verbal, liturgical performance.
passage: One might propose that an act constitutes genuine prayer if and only if it is a verbal address directed to God, it is performed at a designated time and place set aside for worship, and it employs a fixed liturgical formula prescribed by a religious authority. The definition captures the formal, institutional practice of prayer as it is observed in many religious traditions. One thinks of the canonical hours of the Benedictine monastery, in which the monks gather at appointed times in the chapel to recite the psalms according to a prescribed order. One thinks of the five daily prayers of Islam, performed at designated times, in the direction of Mecca, following a fixed sequence of postures and recitations. The definition identifies prayer with its most disciplined and institutionally regulated forms. Yet one must consider the prayer of a mother who learns that her child is gravely ill. She falls to her knees in the corridor of a hospital — not a designated place of worship, not a chapel, not a mosque — and pours out her heart to God in words that are entirely her own. She does not recite a formula; she has no formula to recite. Her words are spontaneous, disordered, broken by sobs, and inarticulate with grief. Much of what passes within her in those moments is not verbal at all; it is an inward reaching, a desperate clinging to a presence she cannot see, an appeal that goes beyond anything that language can express.
