She prays at no designated time; the moment has been forced upon her by circumstance. She employs no prescribed liturgical form; her prayer is as formless as her anguish. Yet her prayer is genuine — as genuine as any prayer that has ever been offered. The definition has identified prayer with its formal, institutional, liturgical expression and has thereby excluded the spontaneous, wordless, situationally compelled prayer that constitutes, for many believers, the most authentic form of communion with God.

definition: An argument A for the existence of God is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted, (ii) A employs only deductively valid inferences, and (iii) the conclusion of A — that God exists — follows with the same certainty as a theorem of mathematics.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted
A employs only deductively valid inferences
the conclusion follows with the same certainty as a theorem of mathematics
counterexample: Consider the argument from the existence of contingent beings to the existence of a necessary being. The argument proceeds from the observation that the things we encounter in the world are contingent — they might not have existed — and reasons that if everything were contingent, there would be no explanation of why anything exists at all; therefore there must be a necessary being whose existence is not contingent. The argument is of considerable philosophical interest, and many able thinkers have found it compelling. But its premises are not self-evident or universally accepted; the premise that contingent existence requires explanation is denied by some philosophers. The first condition fails. Yet the argument may be sound in the ordinary philosophical sense.
missing_condition: The definition demands that the premises be self-evident or universally accepted, but sound arguments may proceed from premises that are controversial yet well-supported. What is wanted is an account of soundness that does not require universal assent to the premises.
passage: One might propose that an argument for the existence of God is sound if and only if it proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted, employs only deductively valid inferences, and yields a conclusion that follows with the same certainty as a theorem of mathematics. The definition sets a high standard, and it is a standard that has been attractive to philosophers who have sought to establish the existence of God upon foundations as secure as those of geometry. Yet one must consider the cosmological argument in its classical form. The argument begins with the observation that the objects of our experience are contingent beings — they exist, but they might not have existed. A particular tree, a particular stone, a particular man — each of them came into existence at some time and will cease to exist at some later time; none of them exists by necessity. From this observation the argument reasons that if every being were contingent, there would be no ultimate explanation of why anything exists at all; the chain of contingent beings, each depending for its existence upon some prior being, would have no foundation. Therefore, the argument concludes, there must be a necessary being — a being that exists not contingently but by its own nature — and this being is God. The argument is of the greatest philosophical interest, and thinkers of the first rank — Aristotle, Avicenna, Aquinas — have found it compelling. It employs deductively structured reasoning, and its conclusion is, if the premises are granted, difficult to resist. But its premises are not self-evident or universally accepted. The premise that contingent existence requires explanation — that there must be a reason why contingent things exist rather than not — has been denied by philosophers of distinction, who have held that the existence of the world is a brute fact that requires no explanation. The first condition of the definition fails. The definition has set a standard for soundness that no philosophical argument about the existence of God has ever met and that, in all likelihood, none could meet, for the questions at issue are precisely those upon which rational persons disagree.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature — that is, E is an event that is physically impossible given the laws that govern the natural world, (ii) E is caused by the direct intervention of God, and (iii) E is witnessed by credible observers who testify to its occurrence.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is caused by the direct intervention of God
E is witnessed by credible observers
counterexample: Consider the recovery of a gravely ill person for whom the physicians had given no hope. The patient, suffering from a disease that is invariably fatal, recovers completely and inexplicably. The recovery is witnessed by physicians, nurses, and members of the patient's family — credible observers all. Many would call this event a miracle. But it is not, in any strict sense, a violation of a law of nature. Spontaneous remissions of fatal diseases, though exceedingly rare, are documented in the medical literature; they are not physically impossible but merely extremely improbable. The first condition fails, yet the event has every appearance of a miracle.
missing_condition: The definition requires a violation of natural law, but events that are merely extremely improbable — not strictly impossible — may be regarded as miraculous. What is wanted is an account that accommodates the miraculous character of events that, whilst not violating any known law, transcend the normal course of nature in a manner that invites a religious interpretation.
passage: Suppose one holds that an event is a miracle if and only if it is a violation of a law of nature, is caused by the direct intervention of God, and is witnessed by credible observers. The definition draws upon a venerable tradition in natural theology, according to which a miracle is a divine suspension of the natural order — an event that could not have occurred by natural means and that therefore attests to the power and purposes of the Deity. The parting of the Red Sea, the raising of Lazarus, the turning of water into wine — these are, on this account, violations of natural law effected by God and witnessed by those present. Yet one must consider the case of a gravely ill person who recovers in circumstances that astonish the attending physicians. The patient is suffering from a disease that, in the whole of medical experience, has never been known to remit. The physicians have pronounced the case hopeless and have told the patient's family to prepare for the worst. Yet the patient recovers — completely, inexplicably, within a matter of days. The recovery is witnessed by physicians, nurses, and family members, all of whom testify to its occurrence. The event is widely regarded as miraculous, and it has, in actual cases, been adduced in proceedings for the canonisation of saints. But it is not, in any strict sense, a violation of a law of nature. The laws of nature do not forbid the spontaneous remission of disease; they render it overwhelmingly improbable, but not impossible. Medical literature records a small number of such remissions, and it is not inconceivable that the mechanisms of the immune system might, on rare occasions, overcome even the most aggressive disease. The first condition of the definition fails. The event is not a violation of law but an occurrence of extreme improbability, and yet it has all the marks of the miraculous — the inexplicability, the timing, the sense of divine favour. The definition has identified the miraculous with the physically impossible, when in truth many events that are experienced and reported as miracles are not impossible but merely extraordinarily unlikely.

definition: A person P has faith in a proposition Q if and only if (i) P believes Q to be true, (ii) P has no evidence for the truth of Q, and (iii) P's belief in Q is entirely a matter of the will — that is, P has chosen to believe Q in the absence of rational grounds.
conditions: P believes Q to be true
P has no evidence for the truth of Q
P's belief in Q is entirely a matter of the will — P has chosen to believe in the absence of rational grounds
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who has faith in the goodness of God. His faith is not held in the absence of all evidence; he points to the beauty of the natural world, the capacity of human beings for love and self-sacrifice, and the testimony of those who have experienced divine grace. These are not, perhaps, conclusive evidence, but they are evidence of a kind — considerations that support the belief and make it, in his view, reasonable. The second condition fails: the person does have evidence, though it may be inconclusive. Yet his attitude is properly called faith.
missing_condition: The definition requires the complete absence of evidence, but faith may be held in the presence of partial, inconclusive, or ambiguous evidence. What is wanted is an account that allows faith to be informed by evidence without requiring that the evidence be sufficient to compel belief.
passage: One might hold that a person has faith in a proposition if and only if the person believes the proposition to be true, has no evidence for its truth, and has chosen to believe it by an act of the will in the absence of rational grounds. The definition reflects a picture of faith as the polar opposite of reason: where reason proceeds from evidence to conclusion, faith leaps in the dark, choosing to affirm what it cannot prove. This picture has been influential, and it captures one strand of the theological tradition — the tradition of Tertullian's 'credo quia absurdum,' of Kierkegaard's leap of faith, of the insistence that faith is meritorious precisely because it is unsupported by evidence. Yet one must consider the case of a person who has faith in the goodness of God. This person believes that God is good, and his belief is firm and central to his life. But his belief is not held in the absence of all evidence. He points to the beauty of the natural world — the intricacy of a snowflake, the splendour of the night sky, the delicate adaptation of organisms to their environments — as evidence of a benevolent creator. He points to the human capacity for love, for self-sacrifice, for moral heroism, as evidence that the world is informed by a moral purpose. He points to the testimony of saints and mystics who have reported direct experience of divine love. These considerations do not amount to a proof; they do not compel assent in the way that a mathematical demonstration compels assent. But they are evidence of a kind — considerations that, in the judgement of the believer, support and render reasonable the belief that God is good. The second condition of the definition fails: the person has evidence, though it is partial, inconclusive, and susceptible of alternative interpretation. Yet his attitude is, by any ordinary standard, an attitude of faith. The definition has polarised faith and evidence into exclusive categories, when in truth faith may be informed by evidence without being compelled by it, and the person of faith may be a person who attends to evidence but goes beyond what the evidence strictly warrants.

definition: An argument A is a sound argument for the existence of a supreme being if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or known by the natural light of reason, (ii) the conclusion — that a supreme being exists — follows from the premises by valid deductive inference, and (iii) the premises do not presuppose or tacitly assume the existence of the being whose existence is to be proved.
conditions: A proceeds from premises self-evident or known by natural reason
The conclusion follows by valid deductive inference
The premises do not presuppose the existence of the being to be proved
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument in its classical form. The premise — that we can conceive of a being than which nothing greater can be conceived — appears to be self-evident, or at least accessible to natural reason. The conclusion is said to follow deductively. And the argument does not obviously presuppose the existence of the supreme being, since it begins from the mere concept of such a being. All three conditions appear to be met, yet the argument has been widely rejected on the grounds that existence cannot be established by conceptual analysis alone — that one cannot argue from a concept to a reality without some further bridge between thought and being.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the argument's premises contain or entail some connection between the conceptual or logical order and the order of actual existence. Without such a condition, purely conceptual arguments that never engage with the question of what actually exists may satisfy the stated conditions.
passage: One might propose that a sound argument for the existence of a supreme being is one that proceeds from premises self-evident or known by natural reason, whose conclusion follows by valid deductive inference, and whose premises do not presuppose the existence of the being to be proved. This definition lays down stringent requirements: the premises must be accessible to reason without the aid of revelation, the logic must be impeccable, and the argument must not beg the question. One would think that any argument satisfying these three conditions would be irresistible. Yet the history of philosophy reveals a persistent difficulty. Consider the ontological argument, which proceeds from the premise that we can conceive of a being than which nothing greater can be conceived, and argues that such a being must exist, since a being that lacked existence would not be the greatest conceivable being. The premise appears to be self-evident — a deliverance of natural reason. The inference, if one grants the implicit principle that existence is a perfection, is deductively valid. And the argument does not presuppose the existence of the supreme being, beginning as it does from a mere concept. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the argument has been rejected by many philosophers, from Gaunilo to Kant, on the grounds that one cannot establish the existence of anything by conceptual analysis alone. The concept of a supreme being, however carefully analysed, remains a concept, and the transition from concept to reality requires something more than logical manipulation. The definition, it seems, lacks a condition requiring that the premises provide some bridge between the order of thought and the order of being — some ground for supposing that the conceptual necessities established by the argument correspond to actualities in the world.

definition: A defence D constitutes an adequate theodicy if and only if (i) D explains why a benevolent and omnipotent creator permits the existence of suffering, (ii) D shows that the existence of suffering is logically compatible with the existence of such a creator, and (iii) D appeals only to considerations that are accessible to reason without the aid of revelation.
conditions: D explains why a benevolent and omnipotent creator permits suffering
D shows logical compatibility of suffering with such a creator
D appeals only to considerations accessible to reason without revelation
counterexample: Consider a theodicy that appeals to the value of free will: suffering exists because the creator endowed rational creatures with freedom, and freedom entails the possibility of moral evil. This theodicy explains why suffering is permitted, establishes logical compatibility, and proceeds from considerations of reason rather than revelation. Yet it fails to account for natural suffering — the suffering caused by earthquakes, plagues, and famines — which has nothing to do with the exercise of free will. All three conditions are met with respect to moral evil, but the theodicy leaves an entire category of suffering unexplained.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the theodicy account for all categories of suffering — both moral and natural. A theodicy that explains one kind of suffering but leaves others unaddressed satisfies the stated conditions but is plainly inadequate.
passage: One might define an adequate theodicy as a defence that explains why a benevolent and omnipotent creator permits suffering, that demonstrates the logical compatibility of suffering with the existence of such a creator, and that appeals only to considerations accessible to reason. The definition captures the essential task of theodicy: to reconcile the existence of suffering with the existence of a being that is both perfectly good and all-powerful. The explanatory condition requires that the theodicy actually tell us why suffering is permitted, not merely assert that it is. The logical condition demands rigour — the theodicy must show that there is no formal contradiction. And the restriction to natural reason reflects the philosophical, as opposed to the theological, character of the enterprise. Yet one might observe that a theodicy may satisfy these conditions while leaving large categories of suffering entirely unaddressed. Consider the free-will theodicy, which is perhaps the most influential defence in the tradition. It explains the existence of moral evil — the suffering caused by human cruelty, wickedness, and negligence — by appealing to the value of free will: a world containing free agents who may choose wrongly is better than a world of automata who cannot. This theodicy meets all three conditions with respect to moral evil. But what of natural suffering — the agony caused by earthquakes, by cancer, by the predation of animals upon one another? These sufferings have nothing to do with the exercise of free will, and the free-will theodicy offers no account of them. The theodicy satisfies the three conditions for one class of suffering but leaves another class wholly unexplained. It appears that the definition must include a further requirement — that the theodicy address all principal categories of suffering, not merely those most amenable to the defence on offer.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature — that is, E is an event that could not have occurred if the laws of nature had operated without interference, (ii) E is brought about by the direct intervention of a supernatural agent, and (iii) E serves a religious or moral purpose — that is, E is intended to convey a message or accomplish a good.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is brought about by direct supernatural intervention
E serves a religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Consider the case of a man who survives a fall from a great height, striking the ground and suffering no injury whatever. Physicians can offer no explanation; the laws of physics, as we understand them, would predict severe injury or death. The event appears to violate a law of nature. Let us suppose — for the sake of the argument — that a supernatural agent did intervene. And suppose the man is a notorious criminal whose survival serves no evident religious or moral purpose; indeed, his survival enables further crime. All three conditions appear to be met only if we stretch the notion of religious purpose to vacuity. If we insist on a genuine religious or moral purpose, the third condition fails, yet the event seems no less miraculous for being purposeless.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a miracle serve a religious or moral purpose, but this excludes events that are in every other respect miraculous — violations of natural law brought about by supernatural agency — but lack a discernible purpose. The concept of miracle, it seems, does not require that we know the purpose of the event.
passage: One might define a miracle as an event that violates a law of nature, is brought about by direct supernatural intervention, and serves a religious or moral purpose. This definition has a long pedigree, drawing upon elements found in the writings of Hume, Aquinas, and others. The first condition establishes the extraordinary character of the event — its departure from the natural order. The second condition identifies the agent responsible — a supernatural being. And the third condition ensures that the event has significance, that it is not a mere anomaly but a communication or an act of beneficence. Yet one might observe that the third condition introduces a requirement that is not, upon reflection, essential to the concept of miracle. Consider a man who falls from a great height and strikes the ground without the slightest injury. Physicians are at a loss to explain his survival; the laws of mechanics, as we understand them, would predict his death. Let us suppose, for the sake of argument, that a supernatural agent did intervene to preserve his life. But suppose the man is a criminal of the worst sort, and his survival enables him to commit further crimes. There is no discernible religious or moral purpose served by the event. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet the event appears to be, in every relevant sense, a miracle — an event that defies the natural order and is brought about by supernatural agency. The definition, it seems, is too restrictive in requiring that every miracle serve a purpose intelligible to us. What is wanted is either a weakening of the third condition or its elimination, leaving the concept of miracle to be defined by its extraordinary character and its supernatural provenance.

definition: A person S has faith that a proposition p is true if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) S lacks conclusive evidence for p — that is, S's evidence does not logically entail p, and (iii) S regards the truth of p as of supreme personal importance.
conditions: S believes that p
S lacks conclusive evidence for p
S regards the truth of p as of supreme personal importance
counterexample: Consider a woman who believes that her husband, missing at sea, is still alive. She lacks conclusive evidence — the search has been abandoned, and the authorities have declared him dead. She regards the truth of the proposition as of supreme personal importance. All three conditions are met, yet we should not ordinarily say that she has faith in the religious sense; rather, she has hope, or perhaps a desperate conviction born of love. The word 'faith,' as it figures in religious and philosophical discourse, carries connotations of commitment, trust, and orientation of the will that go beyond mere belief under uncertainty about matters of personal importance.
missing_condition: The definition omits the volitional and dispositional dimension of faith — the element of trust, commitment, and willingness to act in accordance with the proposition believed. Faith, in its fullest sense, involves not merely believing without conclusive evidence but orienting one's life around that belief.
passage: One might propose that a person has faith that a proposition is true if and only if the person believes the proposition, lacks conclusive evidence for it, and regards its truth as of supreme personal importance. This definition captures three features commonly associated with faith: belief, evidential deficit, and existential seriousness. The belief condition ensures that faith is not mere wish or fantasy; the evidential condition distinguishes faith from ordinary confidence grounded in proof; and the condition of personal importance marks off faith from the countless beliefs we hold without conclusive evidence but about which we are indifferent. Yet one might observe that the definition, though it captures the cognitive and affective dimensions of faith, omits something essential. Consider a woman who believes that her husband, lost at sea, is still alive. She lacks conclusive evidence — the ship has sunk, the search has been abandoned, the authorities presume him dead. She regards the matter as of supreme personal importance. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be natural to describe her state as one of hope, or perhaps of desperate conviction, rather than of faith in the sense in which the term is used in religious and philosophical discourse. What the definition appears to lack is the volitional dimension — the element of trust, of commitment, of willingness to orient one's conduct and one's life in accordance with the proposition believed. Faith, in its fullest sense, is not merely believing something important without proof; it is committing oneself to act upon that belief, to live as though the proposition were true, and to accept the risks that such commitment entails. The definition requires a further condition concerning the will and its engagement with the belief.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a prayer if and only if (i) U is addressed to a divine or supernatural being, (ii) U expresses a petition — that is, U asks the divine being to bring about or prevent some state of affairs, and (iii) U is performed in a context of worship or religious observance.
conditions: U is addressed to a divine or supernatural being
U expresses a petition
U is performed in a context of worship or religious observance
counterexample: Consider a monk who, in the course of religious observance, addresses his words to God — but the content of his utterance is not a petition at all. He offers praise, expressing wonder and gratitude at the beauty of creation. He asks for nothing; he seeks no intervention. The utterance is addressed to a divine being and is performed in a context of worship, but it does not express a petition. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not, yet the monk's words are, by universal recognition, a prayer.
missing_condition: The definition restricts prayer to petitionary utterances, but prayer encompasses praise, thanksgiving, confession, and contemplation, none of which involve asking the divine being to act. The definition is too narrow in identifying prayer with petition.
passage: One might define prayer as an utterance addressed to a divine or supernatural being, expressing a petition, and performed in a context of worship or religious observance. This definition captures what is perhaps the most familiar form of prayer — petitionary prayer, in which the suppliant asks the divine being to grant some request, to heal a sickness, to avert a calamity, or to bestow a blessing. The condition that the utterance be addressed to a divine being distinguishes prayer from mere wishing; the petitionary condition identifies the purpose of the utterance; and the contextual condition ties prayer to the practice of religion. Yet one might observe that the definition, by restricting prayer to petition, excludes forms of prayer that are recognised by every religious tradition as central to the practice of prayer. Consider a monk who, in the course of his daily devotions, addresses his words to God but asks for nothing. He offers praise — expressing awe at the divine majesty and gratitude for the gift of existence. His utterance is addressed to a divine being and is performed in a context of worship, but it does not express a petition. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. Yet no one acquainted with the religious life would deny that the monk is praying. Indeed, many spiritual traditions regard contemplative or laudatory prayer as a higher form of prayer than the petitionary variety. The definition, it appears, has taken one species of prayer — the petitionary — for the genus, and has thereby excluded praise, thanksgiving, confession, and contemplative communion from the class of things that count as prayer.

definition: An object, place, or practice O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary or profane use by a community, (ii) O is associated with a divine being, a supernatural power, or a religious narrative, and (iii) O is treated with reverence — that is, the members of the community observe specific prohibitions or rituals in their dealings with O.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary or profane use
O is associated with a divine being, supernatural power, or religious narrative
O is treated with reverence through specific prohibitions or rituals
counterexample: Consider a national flag. It is set apart from ordinary use — it may not, by convention and often by law, be used as a tablecloth or a rag. It is treated with elaborate reverence — there are prescribed rituals for its display, folding, and disposal, and violations of these norms provoke intense indignation. Yet the flag is not associated with a divine being, a supernatural power, or a religious narrative; it is a symbol of the nation, a political entity. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. Yet there is a strong sense in which the flag functions as something sacred — it commands the kind of awe, respect, and ritual attention that we associate with the sacred.
missing_condition: The definition ties the sacred exclusively to the religious or supernatural, but there exist objects and practices that are sacred in function — commanding reverence, ritual treatment, and separation from the profane — without any connection to the divine or supernatural. The definition requires a broader account of what generates sacredness.
passage: One might propose that an object, place, or practice is sacred if and only if it is set apart from ordinary use, is associated with a divine being or supernatural power or religious narrative, and is treated with reverence through specific prohibitions or rituals. This definition draws upon a distinction between the sacred and the profane that has been central to the study of religion since Durkheim. The condition of separation marks off the sacred from the everyday; the association with the divine provides the ground or reason for the separation; and the reverence condition captures the behavioural dimension — the characteristic way in which communities relate to what they hold sacred. Yet one might observe that the definition, by requiring association with the divine or supernatural, excludes objects and practices that function as sacred without any religious connection. Consider a national flag. It is set apart from ordinary use with the utmost care; one may not, by convention and often by law, treat it as an ordinary piece of cloth. It is surrounded by elaborate rituals — prescribed methods of display, folding, and disposal — and violations of the norms governing its treatment provoke an intensity of indignation that is difficult to account for in purely rational terms. Yet the flag is not associated with any divine being or supernatural power. It is a symbol of the political community, not of the transcendent. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. And yet there is a sense — difficult to deny — in which the flag is sacred, or at least functions as the sacred does. The definition, it appears, identifies the sacred too closely with the religious, and fails to capture the broader phenomenon of secular sacredness.

definition: An event or communication R constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) R originates from a divine source — that is, R is the act of a divine being, (ii) R discloses a truth that could not have been discovered by human reason alone, and (iii) R is received by a human agent who recognises it as divine in origin.
conditions: R originates from a divine source
R discloses a truth undiscoverable by reason alone
R is received by a human agent who recognises it as divine
counterexample: Consider a prophet who receives a communication that originates, let us suppose, from a divine being, and which discloses a truth beyond the reach of natural reason. But the prophet does not recognise the communication as divine in origin; he takes it to be a product of his own imagination, a moment of poetic inspiration. He writes down the truth and publishes it as philosophy or poetry. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, yet it seems strange to deny that a revelation has occurred merely because the recipient failed to identify its source.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the recipient recognise the divine origin of the communication, but this makes revelation dependent upon the recipient's discernment. A genuine revelation might be unrecognised as such, and the definition ought to allow for this possibility.
passage: One might define divine revelation as an event or communication that originates from a divine source, discloses a truth undiscoverable by human reason alone, and is received by a human agent who recognises it as divine in origin. This definition captures the traditional understanding of revelation as a disclosure from above — a lifting of the veil between the human and the divine that grants access to truths beyond the natural reach of the mind. The condition of divine origin distinguishes revelation from human insight or discovery. The condition concerning the content ensures that revelation tells us something we could not have figured out for ourselves. And the recognition condition ensures that the human recipient is aware of the character of what has been received. Yet one might observe that the third condition is more problematic than it first appears. Consider a prophet who receives, let us suppose, a genuine communication from a divine being — a disclosure of a truth beyond the reach of natural reason. But the prophet, for whatever reason, does not recognise the communication as divine. He takes it to be a moment of poetic inspiration, a flash of imagination. He records the truth and publishes it, and it enters the world as philosophy or literature. The first two conditions are satisfied — the communication is divine in origin and it discloses a truth reason could not have reached — but the third is not, for the prophet has not recognised the communication for what it is. Yet it seems strange to say that no revelation has occurred. The divine being has communicated; the truth has been disclosed; a human agent has received it. The definition, it appears, makes revelation hostage to the discernment of the recipient, and this seems to get the order of dependence wrong. What is wanted, perhaps, is a definition that allows for revelation to occur even when the recipient fails to recognise its source.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by a divine being, (ii) the divine being's command is an expression of that being's perfectly good will, and (iii) the command has been made known to human agents through revelation or the natural moral law.
conditions: A is commanded by a divine being
The command expresses the being's perfectly good will
The command has been made known through revelation or natural moral law
counterexample: Consider two religious communities, each claiming to have received divine commands through revelation, where the commands are contradictory — one community is commanded to perform an action, the other is commanded to refrain from it. Both communities hold that their respective commands issue from a perfectly good divine will, and both have received the command through what they take to be revelation. All three conditions are satisfied, symmetrically, for contradictory obligations. The definition offers no means of adjudicating between the conflicting commands, and thus generates a moral contradiction.
missing_condition: The definition provides no criterion for distinguishing genuine from spurious claims of divine command, and thus no means of resolving conflicts between incompatible commands each purporting to satisfy the three conditions. A condition concerning the verification or authentication of divine commands is needed.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory if and only if it is commanded by a divine being, the command expresses that being's perfectly good will, and the command has been made known to human agents. This definition places the foundation of morality in the divine will and specifies how that will is communicated — through revelation or through the natural moral law inscribed in human reason. The first condition establishes the source of obligation; the second guards against the objection that a divine command might be arbitrary or malevolent, by insisting that the divine will is perfectly good; and the third ensures that the obligation is, in principle, knowable by those upon whom it falls. Yet one might observe that the definition, for all its apparent completeness, is unable to resolve a difficulty that arises in practice. Suppose two religious communities each claim to have received divine commands through revelation, and suppose the commands are contradictory. One community is commanded to perform an action that the other is commanded to avoid. Each community maintains that its command issues from a perfectly good divine will, and each has received its command through what it takes to be authentic revelation. All three conditions are symmetrically satisfied for both commands, yet the commands cannot both be obligatory. The definition offers no means of adjudicating between the conflicting claims. It lacks a condition that would enable one to distinguish genuine from spurious claims of divine communication — a criterion of authentication that would break the symmetry and resolve the conflict. Without such a condition, the definition generates contradictions whenever competing revelatory claims are in play.

definition: A person S is immortal if and only if (i) S possesses a soul or immaterial substance that is distinct from the body, (ii) this soul persists after the destruction of the body, and (iii) the soul retains the personal identity of S — that is, the surviving soul is recognisably the same person as the embodied S.
conditions: S possesses a soul or immaterial substance distinct from the body
The soul persists after the destruction of the body
The soul retains the personal identity of S
counterexample: Consider a doctrine of reincarnation according to which the soul of a deceased person persists and is reborn in a new body but retains no memories, no character traits, and no continuity of consciousness with the previous life. The soul is an immaterial substance distinct from the body; it persists after the body's destruction; and by hypothesis it is numerically the same soul. But if the reborn person has no memory of the previous life, no awareness of having lived before, and no psychological continuity with the former self, one might well ask in what sense the personal identity of the original person has been retained. All three conditions appear to be met if identity is understood as numerical identity of the soul, yet the survival is, in an important sense, empty — it is not the survival of a person but merely the persistence of a metaphysical entity.
missing_condition: The definition identifies personal identity with the persistence of the soul but does not require that the surviving entity retain any psychological continuity — memories, character, consciousness — with the embodied person. Without such continuity, the 'survival' of the soul does not amount to the survival of the person in any humanly meaningful sense.
passage: One might define immortality as the possession of a soul or immaterial substance that is distinct from the body, persists after the body's destruction, and retains the personal identity of the person. This definition embodies a dualist metaphysics — the view that a person consists of two substances, body and soul, and that the soul is capable of existing independently. The first condition establishes the metaphysical framework; the second ensures that the soul is not destroyed along with the body; and the third — the most crucial — ensures that the surviving entity is the same person as the one who lived and died. Without the third condition, survival of the soul would be trivially compatible with the annihilation of the person. Yet one might observe that the third condition, as stated, does not specify what personal identity requires. Consider a doctrine of reincarnation according to which the soul persists after death and is reborn in a new body, but retains no memories, no character traits, and no continuity of consciousness with the former life. The soul is immaterial and distinct from the body; it persists; and it is, by hypothesis, numerically identical with the soul of the deceased. But the reborn person remembers nothing of the previous life, exhibits none of the former personality, and has no awareness of having lived before. In what sense, one might ask, is this the survival of the person? It is the persistence of a metaphysical entity, certainly, but it is not the survival of a self — not the continuation of a life with its memories, its character, and its consciousness. The definition, it appears, needs a condition specifying that the surviving entity must retain some substantial degree of psychological continuity with the embodied person, lest the bare persistence of the soul be mistaken for the immortality of the person.

definition: An experience E is a religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sense of the presence or reality of a divine or transcendent being, (ii) E is accompanied by intense emotion — awe, joy, peace, or dread, and (iii) E occurs spontaneously — that is, E is not deliberately induced by the subject.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a divine or transcendent being
E is accompanied by intense emotion
E occurs spontaneously and is not deliberately induced
counterexample: Consider a contemplative who, through years of disciplined meditation and spiritual exercises, attains an experience of the presence of the divine that is vivid, accompanied by intense peace and awe, and by any phenomenological criterion indistinguishable from the spontaneous experiences described by the mystics. Yet this experience has been deliberately cultivated; it is the product of a sustained discipline of prayer, fasting, and contemplation. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, since the experience was deliberately pursued. Yet it would be strange to deny the contemplative's experience the title of religious experience, particularly since the great contemplative traditions regard such disciplined experiences as the highest form of religious life.
missing_condition: The definition requires spontaneity, thereby excluding experiences that are the product of deliberate spiritual discipline. Yet many of the most profound religious experiences reported in the contemplative traditions are the fruit of sustained practice rather than of spontaneous occurrence. The definition should accommodate experiences produced by disciplined spiritual practice.
passage: One might propose that a religious experience is one that involves a vivid sense of the presence of a divine or transcendent being, is accompanied by intense emotion, and occurs spontaneously. The definition aims to capture the dramatic, unsought encounters with the transcendent that figure so prominently in the literature of religion — the burning bush, the conversion on the road to Damascus, the sudden illumination of the mystic. The condition of vividness ensures that the experience has a definite phenomenological character, distinguishing it from vague feelings of uplift or generalised piety. The emotional condition reflects the overwhelming affective quality that is, by common testimony, inseparable from genuine encounters with the divine. And the spontaneity condition ensures that the experience is a gift rather than an artefact — something that comes unbidden, not something manufactured by the subject's own efforts. Yet one might observe that the third condition is more problematic than it appears. Consider a contemplative monk who, through decades of disciplined meditation and prayer, attains an experience of the divine presence that is vivid, accompanied by profound peace and awe, and phenomenologically indistinguishable from the spontaneous experiences of the great mystics. This experience is not spontaneous; it is the hard-won fruit of sustained spiritual discipline. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet the contemplative traditions of Christianity, Islam, and Buddhism unanimously regard such disciplined encounters as the highest form of religious experience — not inferior to spontaneous illuminations but superior to them, precisely because they are the product of a purified and prepared consciousness. The definition, it appears, has been drawn too narrowly, excluding the very experiences that the contemplative traditions hold in highest esteem.

definition: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) the premises of A are all true, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premises by valid deductive inference, and (iii) the premises are more evident to reason than the conclusion they are intended to establish.
conditions: The premises are all true
The conclusion follows by valid deductive inference
The premises are more evident than the conclusion
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument, which proceeds from the premise that a being than which none greater can be conceived must exist in the understanding, through the premise that existence in reality is greater than existence in the understanding alone, to the conclusion that such a being exists in reality. Even if one grants the premises and the validity of the inference, one might observe that the premises — involving the comparative greatness of modes of existence — are, if anything, less evident to reason than the conclusion they are meant to establish. The third condition is violated: the premises are at least as controversial as the conclusion. The argument may be formally valid and its premises may even be true, yet it fails to be probative because it reasons from the obscure to the even more obscure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises be independently credible — that is, credible on grounds that do not already presuppose the truth of the conclusion. Without such a condition, arguments whose premises are accepted only by those who already accept the conclusion count as sound.
passage: One might propose that an argument for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if its premises are true, its conclusion follows validly from them, and the premises are more evident than the conclusion. This last condition is intended to ensure that the argument is genuinely probative — that it moves from what is better known to what is less well known, in accordance with the classical conception of demonstration. An argument whose premises are less evident than its conclusion fails to illuminate; it merely recasts a plausible claim in terms of dubious ones. Yet one might observe that even this threefold requirement is insufficient. Consider the ontological argument, which reasons from a conception of a supremely great being to the conclusion that such a being must exist. The argument, one may concede for the sake of discussion, is valid. Its premises may even be true. But the premise that existence in reality is greater than existence in the understanding alone is, to many minds, no more evident than the conclusion it is intended to establish — and is perhaps less so. More importantly, one may suspect that the premise is acceptable only to those who already believe the conclusion. The argument satisfies the letter of the three conditions, yet it achieves its apparent force by a kind of circularity — the premises derive their plausibility from the very conclusion they purport to demonstrate. What the definition lacks is a requirement that the premises be independently credible: credible, that is, on grounds that do not presuppose the truth of the conclusion.

definition: A response R to the problem of suffering constitutes an adequate theodicy if and only if (i) R identifies a good or set of goods whose realisation requires the permission of suffering, (ii) R demonstrates that the goods identified could not have been realised by any means that did not involve suffering, and (iii) R shows that the goods outweigh the suffering in question.
conditions: R identifies goods whose realisation requires the permission of suffering
R demonstrates that the goods could not have been realised without suffering
R shows that the goods outweigh the suffering
counterexample: Consider the claim that suffering is necessary for the development of moral virtue — that courage, compassion, and fortitude can be cultivated only in a world where there is genuine hardship. One might grant that some suffering is necessary for some virtues, and that these virtues outweigh the suffering required. But consider the suffering of a child who dies of a painful disease at the age of two. The child has not developed any virtue through the suffering; no moral growth has occurred. The suffering is not outweighed by any good accruing to the sufferer. All three conditions may be met in the aggregate — virtue is a genuine good, some suffering is necessary for it, and virtue outweighs that suffering — but the conditions are not met in every particular case.
missing_condition: The definition evaluates the adequacy of the theodicy in the aggregate but fails to require that the justification apply to each particular instance of suffering. Without a condition requiring that the good identified be connected to each individual case, the theodicy may justify suffering in general while leaving the most troubling particular cases — especially the suffering of the innocent — unaddressed.
passage: One might define an adequate theodicy as a response that identifies goods whose realisation requires the permission of suffering, demonstrates that those goods could not have been achieved without suffering, and shows that the goods outweigh the suffering. This definition captures the essential structure of the classical theodicies: suffering is justified by its contribution to a good that could not otherwise be realised, and the good is sufficient to outweigh the cost. The requirement of indispensability — that the goods could not have been achieved without suffering — is intended to exclude theodicies that invoke goods which, however valuable, could have been achieved by less painful means. And the requirement that the goods outweigh the suffering ensures a favourable moral balance. Yet one might observe that the definition permits a theodicy that succeeds in the aggregate but fails in the particular. Consider the claim that suffering is necessary for moral virtue. It is plausible that some suffering is required for the development of courage, compassion, and endurance, and that these virtues are of great value. But consider the case of a child who dies of a painful disease at the age of two. The child has developed no virtue through the suffering; no moral growth has been achieved; the suffering serves no purpose that is discernible in relation to that particular life. The theodicy may justify suffering in general — some suffering, in some lives, produces virtue that outweighs it — but it does not justify this suffering, in this life. The definition, it appears, evaluates the theodicy at a level of generality that permits it to succeed while leaving the most troubling cases untouched.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is contrary to the established regularities of nature as understood by the best available science, (ii) E serves a discernible purpose — it delivers, warns, or communicates in a way that is intelligible as the action of a purposive agent, and (iii) E cannot be explained by any natural cause, however remote or unusual.
conditions: E is contrary to established natural regularities
E serves a discernible purpose intelligible as the action of a purposive agent
E cannot be explained by any natural cause
counterexample: Consider a spontaneous remission of an advanced, metastatic cancer in a patient who has received no treatment. The remission is contrary to the established regularities of oncology. The patient, who had been praying for recovery, regards the remission as a purposive act of deliverance. And no natural cause — no treatment, no change of diet, no immune response that current medicine can identify — explains the remission. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet spontaneous remissions, though exceedingly rare, do occur in oncological practice, and the fact that no natural cause has been identified does not entail that none exists. The third condition, taken strictly, demands proof of a negative — that no natural explanation is possible — which is in practice unattainable.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the event cannot be explained by any natural cause, but this is a negative existential claim that can never be conclusively established. A condition based on the impossibility of natural explanation sets a standard that no event can in practice meet, since the absence of a known explanation is always compatible with the existence of an as-yet-undiscovered one.
passage: One might define a miracle as an event contrary to the established regularities of nature, serving a discernible purpose, and incapable of explanation by any natural cause. The first condition places the miracle outside the ordinary course of nature; the second gives it the character of an intentional act — a communication, a deliverance, a sign — that elevates it above the merely anomalous; and the third ensures that the event is not merely unusual but genuinely supernatural. Yet one might observe that the third condition, though essential to the definition's intent, is in practice impossible to satisfy. Consider a case of spontaneous remission of an advanced cancer in a patient who has received no medical treatment. The remission defies the established regularities of oncology; the patient, who had prayed fervently for recovery, regards it as a purposive act of divine mercy; and no natural cause that current medicine can identify accounts for the remission. All three conditions appear to be met. But the conclusion that no natural cause exists is unwarranted. Spontaneous remissions, though rare, are recognised phenomena, and the failure to identify a cause is not the same as the demonstration that no cause exists. The immune system is incompletely understood; the interactions between psychological states and physiological processes are only beginning to be mapped. The third condition requires proof that no natural explanation is possible, but this is a negative existential claim of a kind that empirical inquiry cannot conclusively establish. Any anomalous event may, at some future date, receive a natural explanation. The definition, it appears, sets a standard that no event can in practice satisfy.

definition: A person S exercises faith if and only if (i) S assents to a proposition P for which the available evidence is insufficient to compel rational assent, (ii) S's assent to P is voluntary — that is, S could have withheld assent, and (iii) S's assent is motivated by a desire for the truth of P rather than by the evidence for P.
conditions: S assents to a proposition for which the evidence is insufficient to compel rational assent
S's assent is voluntary
S's assent is motivated by a desire for the truth of P
counterexample: Consider a gambler who assents to the proposition that his horse will win the race. The evidence is insufficient to compel this belief — many horses are running, and the odds are against his. The assent is voluntary — he could have withheld it. And his assent is motivated by a desire for the truth of the proposition rather than by the evidence, since he desperately wants his horse to win. All three conditions are met, yet we should not say that the gambler exercises faith. What he exhibits is wishful thinking — the confusion of desire with reason.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish faith from wishful thinking. Both involve voluntary assent motivated by desire rather than evidence. A condition requiring that the object of faith possess a certain character — that it concern a matter of ultimate significance, or that the assent be embedded in a practice of commitment and trust — is needed to distinguish faith from mere desire-driven credulity.
passage: One might propose that faith is exercised when a person assents to a proposition for which the evidence is insufficient, does so voluntarily, and is motivated by a desire for the truth of the proposition rather than by the evidence. This definition captures something important about faith: it goes beyond what the evidence warrants, it is freely chosen, and it is animated by a yearning for a reality that the intellect alone cannot secure. The voluntariness condition distinguishes faith from involuntary credulity — the unthinking acceptance of whatever one is told — and the motivational condition connects faith to the will rather than to the understanding. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, describe a state of mind that is common and by no means always admirable. Consider a gambler who fervently believes that his horse will win the race. The evidence is insufficient — many horses are competing, and the form guide favours another. His belief is voluntary — he could have withheld it. And his assent is motivated not by the evidence but by his passionate desire for the proposition to be true. All three conditions are satisfied, and yet what the gambler exhibits is not faith but wishful thinking — the very vice against which the advocates of faith have always sought to distinguish their enterprise. The definition, it appears, fails to discriminate between faith and desire-driven self-deception. What is needed is a condition concerning the character of the object or the context of the commitment — something that distinguishes the existential gravity of religious faith from the psychological mechanism of wishing things were so.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is addressed to a being whom the agent regards as transcending the natural order, (ii) A expresses a petition — a request for something the agent desires, and (iii) the agent believes that the addressee is able to grant the petition.
conditions: A is addressed to a being regarded as transcending the natural order
A expresses a petition or request
The agent believes the addressee is able to grant the petition
counterexample: Consider a devout monk who spends hours in silent contemplation, directing his attention toward the divine without making any request whatsoever. His practice is addressed to a being he regards as transcending the natural order, and he believes that being is omnipotent. But his prayer contains no petition — it is an act of adoration, surrender, or attentiveness rather than a request. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. Yet the contemplative traditions of most major religions regard this kind of prayer as the highest form, surpassing petitionary prayer in spiritual significance.
missing_condition: The definition equates prayer with petition and thereby excludes contemplative, adorational, and meditative forms of prayer that involve no request. A broader condition encompassing the full range of communicative and attentive acts directed toward the transcendent is needed.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as an act addressed to a transcendent being, expressing a petition, where the agent believes the addressee can grant the petition. This definition captures the most familiar form of prayer — the petitionary prayer in which the supplicant asks for something: health, deliverance, guidance, forgiveness. The requirement that the addressee be regarded as transcending the natural order distinguishes prayer from ordinary requests addressed to other human beings. The requirement that the agent believe in the addressee's power to grant the petition ensures the sincerity and seriousness of the act. And the petitionary element gives the prayer its characteristic structure — it is an act of asking, not merely of speaking. Yet one might observe that this definition, though it describes one form of prayer, excludes others that the great religious traditions have regarded as more exalted. Consider the silent contemplation practised by monks and mystics across many traditions — the wordless attention directed toward the divine, the patient waiting upon a presence, the act of surrender in which the self is emptied of desire and petition. This contemplative prayer is addressed to a transcendent being; the practitioner believes in that being's power. But there is no petition. The monk asks for nothing; he offers himself. The definition, by insisting upon petition, excludes the very form of prayer that the contemplative traditions have most highly valued. What is wanted is a broader characterisation that encompasses not only asking but also adoring, attending, and surrendering — the full range of communicative acts that the life of prayer comprises.

definition: An object or place X is sacred if and only if (i) X is set apart from ordinary use by a community of believers, (ii) X is regarded by that community as possessing a special significance connected to the divine or the transcendent, and (iii) X is protected by rules or prohibitions that govern how it may be treated or approached.
conditions: X is set apart from ordinary use
X is regarded as possessing special significance connected to the divine or transcendent
X is protected by rules or prohibitions
counterexample: Consider the tomb of a national hero — say, the tomb of the Unknown Soldier. The tomb is set apart from ordinary use; no one would picnic upon it or use it as a bench. It is protected by elaborate rules and prohibitions — guards stand watch, visitors are expected to maintain silence and solemnity. And it is regarded as possessing a special significance that transcends the ordinary — it symbolises the sacrifice of an entire nation's war dead. All three conditions are met, yet the tomb's significance is patriotic rather than religious or transcendent. One would hesitate to call it sacred in the sense that a church altar or a holy relic is sacred, for its significance derives from national sentiment rather than from any connection to the divine.
missing_condition: The definition does not adequately distinguish the sacred from the merely venerated. A condition requiring that the special significance be connected to a specific metaphysical or theological claim about the transcendent — not merely to strong communal sentiment — is needed.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use, regarded as possessing a special significance connected to the divine or transcendent, and protected by rules governing its treatment. This definition captures the phenomenology of the sacred as it is encountered in religious life: the temple is set apart from the marketplace; the holy book is not treated as an ordinary text; the sacred grove is entered only with reverence. The tripartite structure — separation, significance, and protection — describes a coherent pattern that is recognisable across the world's religions. Yet one might observe that these conditions are satisfied by objects and places whose significance is not religious in any clear sense. Consider the tomb of a national hero or the memorial to the war dead. Such places are set apart from ordinary use; they are regarded as possessing a significance that transcends the quotidian; and they are protected by rules and prohibitions — guards, fences, codes of conduct. All three conditions are met. But the significance of these places is patriotic and commemorative rather than religious; it derives from national feeling, not from a conviction about the divine or the metaphysically transcendent. The definition, by specifying only that the significance be 'connected to the divine or the transcendent,' leaves sufficient ambiguity for the patriotic to masquerade as the sacred. What is needed, it appears, is a more precise specification of the kind of significance that renders an object or place genuinely sacred — a significance grounded in specific metaphysical or theological claims, not merely in the intensity of communal feeling.

definition: A communication C constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) C conveys information that could not have been obtained by the unaided exercise of human reason or observation, (ii) the recipient of C sincerely believes that the source of the communication is a divine being, and (iii) the content of C is consistent with the moral and theological teachings previously accepted by the recipient's religious community.
conditions: C conveys information unobtainable by unaided human reason or observation
The recipient sincerely believes the source is divine
The content is consistent with previously accepted teachings
counterexample: Consider the case of a prophet who receives a communication that contradicts the moral teachings of his community — a command, let us say, to overturn an established practice that the community regards as divinely ordained. The communication conveys information unobtainable by reason alone. The prophet sincerely believes the source is divine. But the content is inconsistent with the community's prior teachings. The third condition fails, and yet many of the most significant moments of revelation in the history of religion have involved precisely such disruptions — the overturning of established teachings by a new divine word. The definition, by requiring consistency with prior teachings, excludes the possibility of genuinely novel revelation.
missing_condition: The definition requires that revealed content be consistent with prior teachings, but this excludes the possibility that revelation might correct, supersede, or overturn what was previously accepted. A condition permitting novel or corrective revelation — while providing criteria for distinguishing genuine correction from error — is needed.
passage: One might define divine revelation as a communication that conveys information unobtainable by human reason, is sincerely believed by its recipient to come from a divine source, and is consistent with the moral and theological teachings previously accepted by the recipient's religious community. The first condition secures the distinctive character of revelation — it tells us something that reason, however diligently exercised, could not have discovered. The second ensures that the recipient's experience is genuine and not a merely theoretical possibility. And the third provides a check against delusion: if the supposed revelation contradicts the established teachings, one has reason to doubt its divine origin. Yet one might observe that the third condition, though prudentially sensible, is in tension with the very nature of revelation as it has been understood in the great religious traditions. Many of the most consequential moments of revelation have involved precisely the overturning of previously accepted teachings. The prophets of Israel did not merely confirm what their communities already believed; they challenged, corrected, and sometimes reversed established practices in the name of a divine word that was new and unwelcome. The definition, by requiring consistency, excludes the possibility that revelation might be corrective or disruptive — that the divine might speak not to confirm but to transform. What is needed is a condition that permits genuinely novel revelation while providing some criterion for distinguishing a genuine divine correction from a merely human error or delusion.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by a divine being, (ii) the command is communicated to human beings through a recognised channel — scripture, prophetic utterance, or direct personal experience, and (iii) the divine being who issues the command is perfectly good.
conditions: A is commanded by a divine being
The command is communicated through a recognised channel
The commanding divine being is perfectly good
counterexample: Consider a scriptural passage in which the divine being commands the wholesale destruction of a city and all its inhabitants, including women and children. The command is issued by a divine being; it is communicated through scripture — a recognised channel; and the divine being is, by the theology of the tradition, perfectly good. All three conditions are met. Yet the action commanded — the slaughter of innocents — is one that the moral sensibility of most persons would regard as deeply wrong. The definition provides no resources for evaluating the moral content of the command independently of its divine source; it makes moral obligation a function of divine fiat and thereby deprives the moral agent of any independent ground for assessing what is commanded.
missing_condition: The definition makes moral obligation entirely dependent on divine command and provides no independent moral criterion by which the content of a command may be assessed. A condition requiring that the commanded action be independently recognisable as good — or at least not manifestly wicked — is needed to avoid making morality a matter of arbitrary divine will.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory if and only if it is commanded by a divine being, communicated through a recognised channel, and issued by a being who is perfectly good. The appeal of this definition is considerable: it grounds morality in something higher than human convention, provides a clear criterion for identifying obligations, and ensures — through the goodness of the commanding being — that the commands will be good ones. The requirement of a recognised channel guards against the proliferation of private 'revelations' that might justify any behaviour whatsoever. Yet one might observe that the definition, even with its goodness condition, fails to provide the moral agent with any independent basis for evaluating the content of the command. Consider a passage of scripture in which the divine being commands the destruction of a city and all its inhabitants, including women, children, and animals. The command is issued by a divine being, communicated through scripture — a recognised channel — and the divine being is, by the theology of the tradition, perfectly good. All three conditions are met, and the action is therefore, on this definition, morally obligatory. Yet the action commanded is one that most persons, reflecting upon it, would judge to be deeply wrong. The definition provides no resources for this judgement; it has placed the entire weight of moral assessment upon the nature of the commander rather than upon the content of the command. What is wanted, it appears, is a condition allowing the moral agent to assess the content of a command independently — to recognise certain actions as wicked regardless of their purported source.

definition: A person S is immortal if and only if (i) there is some part or aspect of S — a soul, a mind, or a self — that continues to exist after the death of S's body, (ii) the continuing entity preserves the personal identity of S — that is, it is recognisably the same person, and (iii) the continuing entity is conscious — it has experiences, thoughts, or awareness of some kind.
conditions: Some part of S continues to exist after bodily death
The continuing entity preserves personal identity
The continuing entity is conscious
counterexample: Consider a view of immortality on which the soul survives the death of the body but, in its disembodied state, possesses none of the memories, character traits, or personality features by which S was known during life. The surviving entity is conscious — it has experiences of some kind. It is said to be the 'same' soul. But if it remembers nothing of S's life, possesses none of S's distinctive traits, and bears no psychological resemblance to S, one might well ask in what sense personal identity has been preserved. All three conditions may be formally met — something survives, it is called the same person, and it is conscious — yet the survival seems empty, for nothing recognisably personal about S has been carried over.
missing_condition: The definition requires the preservation of personal identity but does not specify what constitutes personal identity in the relevant sense. Without a condition specifying that personal identity requires continuity of memory, character, or psychological connectedness, the definition permits a notion of survival so attenuated that it is indistinguishable from the survival of a different person.
passage: One might define immortality as the continuation, after bodily death, of some part or aspect of the person — a soul, a mind, or a self — that preserves personal identity and remains conscious. This definition captures the essential features of what most people mean when they speak of life after death: not merely the persistence of some abstract entity, but the continuation of the person — the very same individual, with awareness and experience, surviving the destruction of the body. The identity condition ensures that what survives is not a generic soul but the particular person; the consciousness condition ensures that the survival is not a metaphor or an abstraction but a genuine continuation of experience. Yet one might observe that the identity condition, as stated, is insufficiently demanding. Consider a view on which the soul survives the body but, in its post-mortem state, retains none of the memories, character traits, or dispositions that constituted the personality of the living person. The surviving entity is conscious — it has experiences. It is called the 'same' soul. But if it remembers nothing of the person's earthly life, if it possesses none of the qualities by which the person was known, and if it bears no psychological resemblance to the person who died, then in what sense is personal identity preserved? The definition requires identity but does not specify what identity consists in. Without a condition requiring psychological continuity — continuity of memory, character, and disposition — the definition permits a survival so stripped of content that it is, for all practical purposes, the survival of someone else entirely.

definition: An experience E is a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid, immediate sense of contact with a reality that the subject takes to be divine or transcendent, (ii) E produces in the subject a lasting transformation of attitude, behaviour, or outlook, and (iii) E is not attributable to any known psychological or physiological cause such as illness, intoxication, or extreme deprivation.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of contact with a divine or transcendent reality
E produces a lasting transformation of attitude or behaviour
E is not attributable to any known psychological or physiological cause
counterexample: Consider the conversion of St Paul on the road to Damascus. Paul experienced a vivid and immediate encounter with what he took to be the risen Christ. The experience produced a lasting and dramatic transformation of his life — from persecutor of Christians to the foremost apostle of the new faith. But modern neurology might suggest that the experience — a blinding light, a voice heard by no one else, temporary blindness — is consistent with a temporal lobe seizure or similar neurological event. If the third condition is taken strictly, Paul's experience fails to qualify, since a physiological cause is at least possible. Yet Paul's conversion is one of the paradigmatic religious experiences in the history of Western religion.
missing_condition: The definition excludes experiences that have possible physiological or psychological explanations, but virtually any experience, including the most profound, has some physiological correlate. The condition sets an impossible standard by requiring the absence of any natural cause, when what may be needed is a condition distinguishing experiences whose significance is exhausted by their natural cause from those whose significance transcends it.
passage: One might propose that a genuine religious experience involves a vivid sense of contact with a transcendent reality, produces a lasting transformation in the subject, and is not attributable to any known psychological or physiological cause. The first condition captures the phenomenological core of religious experience — the overwhelming immediacy of the encounter, the sense that one is in the presence of something utterly beyond the ordinary. The transformative condition ensures that the experience is not a passing fancy but something that reshapes the person's life. And the causal condition is intended to exclude experiences that, however vivid, are the products of illness, intoxication, or deprivation. Yet one might observe that the third condition proves, upon reflection, excessively restrictive. Consider the case of Paul of Tarsus, whose experience on the road to Damascus is one of the paradigmatic religious experiences in the Western tradition. Paul experienced a blinding light, heard a voice, and was struck blind for three days. The experience transformed his life utterly. Yet a modern neurologist might observe that the symptoms — the sudden light, the auditory experience, the temporary blindness — are consistent with a temporal lobe seizure or a similar neurological episode. A physiological cause is at least possible. The third condition, taken strictly, excludes Paul's experience from the ranks of the genuinely religious, a conclusion that few students of religion would accept. The difficulty is that every experience, however profound, has some physiological correlate, and the possibility of a natural cause can never be conclusively ruled out. The definition, it appears, sets a standard that no experience can satisfy. What is needed is not the exclusion of natural causation but a criterion for distinguishing experiences whose significance is adequately explained by their natural cause from those whose significance transcends any such explanation.

definition: An argument A constitutes a sound proof of the existence of God if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted, (ii) A employs only valid logical inferences, and (iii) A reaches the conclusion that a being with the traditional divine attributes exists.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted
A employs only valid logical inferences
A concludes that a being with traditional divine attributes exists
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument in its classical form. The premise that God is that than which nothing greater can be conceived may be regarded as self-evident in the sense that it merely states a definition. The argument proceeds by valid logical steps. And the conclusion is that a being with maximal perfection — and hence the traditional divine attributes — exists.
