Yet the argument has been rejected by many philosophers, including Kant, on the ground that existence is not a predicate and cannot be included among the perfections whose absence would diminish a being's greatness. The premises are not universally accepted.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises be not merely formally self-evident or definitionally stipulated but substantively warranted — that is, that they be such as to compel assent on grounds independent of the conclusion they are meant to establish.
passage: One might propose that a proof of the existence of God is an argument proceeding from self-evident or universally accepted premises, employing valid inferences, to the conclusion that a being with the traditional divine attributes exists. This definition appears to set an appropriately high bar: self-evidence ensures that the starting points are beyond reasonable dispute, validity ensures that the reasoning is sound, and the conclusion must deliver the God of traditional theology. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the ontological argument. The argument begins with the premise that God is that than which nothing greater can be conceived — a premise that may be regarded as self-evident in the sense that it is merely a definition. It proceeds by valid logical steps to the conclusion that such a being must exist, for a being that exists in reality is greater than one that exists only in the understanding. The conclusion attributes to this being maximal perfection and hence the traditional divine attributes. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the argument has been rejected by philosophers from Gaunilo to Kant. Kant's objection — that existence is not a real predicate — challenges the substantive adequacy of the premise, not its formal self-evidence. The definition's requirement of self-evidence or universal acceptance does not distinguish between premises that are merely definitionally stipulated and premises that are substantively warranted.

definition: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of God with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T explains how evil can exist in a world created by an omnipotent, omniscient, and perfectly good being, (ii) T does not diminish any of the traditional divine attributes, and (iii) T accounts for the actual quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
conditions: T explains how evil can exist given an omnipotent, omniscient, perfectly good creator
T does not diminish any traditional divine attributes
T accounts for the actual quantity and distribution of evil observed
counterexample: Consider the free-will theodicy, which explains the existence of moral evil by the hypothesis that God granted human beings freedom of will, and that evil arises from the misuse of that freedom. This explanation does not diminish any divine attribute — God is still omnipotent but chose not to compel virtue. Yet the free-will theodicy does not account for the enormous quantity of natural evil — earthquakes, plagues, birth defects — which has nothing to do with human choices. The third condition is not satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between moral evil, which arises from the choices of free agents, and natural evil, which does not, and thus requires a theodicy to address both kinds under a single explanatory framework.
passage: A theodicy, in the traditional sense, seeks to reconcile divine perfection with the manifest presence of evil in the world. One might define a successful theodicy as one that explains how evil is compatible with an omnipotent, omniscient, and perfectly good creator, does not diminish any of the divine attributes, and accounts for the actual quantity and distribution of evil we observe. This definition sets a demanding standard, as it ought — the problem of evil is among the most formidable challenges in philosophy of religion. Consider, then, the free-will theodicy, which is among the most influential responses. It explains the existence of moral evil by positing that God endowed human beings with freedom of will, and that evil results from the misuse of that freedom. God is not thereby diminished — He remains omnipotent but chose freely to permit free creatures. Yet the free-will theodicy, for all its ingenuity, does not account for the vast quantity of natural evil in the world. Earthquakes devastate cities; plagues carry off thousands of innocents; children are born with agonising defects. None of this can be attributed to the misuse of human freedom. The third condition of the definition is not met, for the actual distribution of evil includes a great deal that the free-will theodicy leaves wholly unexplained.

definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by a divine agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious purpose.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is brought about by a divine agent
E serves a discernible religious purpose
counterexample: Consider the case of a patient whose terminal cancer goes into complete and unexplained remission after prayer. The remission serves an apparent religious purpose — it strengthens the faith of the community. The community attributes it to divine agency. But it is unclear that a law of nature has been violated: spontaneous remission, though extremely rare, is a recognised medical phenomenon. The first condition is not clearly met, yet the event is widely regarded as miraculous by those who witnessed it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for events that are extraordinary and religiously significant yet do not demonstrably violate any known law of nature, requiring a distinction between violation of natural law and mere extreme improbability.
passage: Hume's celebrated definition of a miracle as a violation of a law of nature by a divine volition has shaped much subsequent discussion. One might refine this into a three-part analysis: a miracle is an event that violates a law of nature, is brought about by a divine agent, and serves a discernible religious purpose. This definition has the virtue of precision: it distinguishes miracles from mere marvels by requiring divine agency, and from mere divine acts by requiring a violation of natural law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in the experience of many religious communities. Consider a patient diagnosed with terminal cancer who, following a period of earnest prayer, enters complete and unexplained remission. The community attributes the remission to divine intervention and finds in it a clear religious purpose — the strengthening of faith, the demonstration of providential care. But has a law of nature been violated? Spontaneous remission, though exceedingly rare, is a recognised medical phenomenon; it occurs without prayer as well as with it. The event is extraordinary but not demonstrably impossible under natural law. If we insist upon violation of natural law, this event does not qualify as a miracle — yet it is precisely the sort of event that is most commonly called miraculous in ordinary religious life. The definition is too narrow.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) the available evidence does not conclusively establish the truth of p, and (iii) S's belief in p is motivated by trust in an authority or a revelatory source.
conditions: S believes that p
The available evidence does not conclusively establish p
S's belief is motivated by trust in an authority or revelatory source
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who, having exhausted all her resources and finding herself in a desperate situation, believes that she will survive the descent. The evidence does not conclusively establish that she will survive — indeed, it rather suggests the contrary. Her belief is not motivated by trust in any authority or revelatory source but by a sheer act of will, a determination not to despair. One might naturally describe her as having faith that she will survive, yet the third condition is not met.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases of faith in which the motivating source is neither an authority nor a revelation but an act of volitional commitment in the face of adverse evidence.
passage: A common analysis of faith identifies it with belief in a proposition not conclusively supported by evidence, motivated by trust in an authority or revelation. This definition captures a wide range of religious cases: the believer who accepts the resurrection on the authority of scripture, or who trusts in the testimony of a saint. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is drawn from ordinary life rather than from religion. Consider a mountaineer trapped on an exposed ridge in deteriorating weather. She has exhausted her provisions; the path ahead is treacherous; the evidence, if coolly assessed, suggests that she is unlikely to reach safety. Yet she believes — with a conviction that shapes her every decision — that she will survive. This belief is not motivated by any authority or revelatory source. No scripture has promised her deliverance; no trusted guide has assured her of success. Her belief springs from a sheer act of will, a refusal to surrender to despair. One would naturally say that she has faith that she will survive. But the definition excludes her case, for the motivating source is volitional resolve rather than deference to authority. A more adequate analysis would allow faith to be grounded in acts of commitment as well as in trust.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a prayer if and only if (i) U is addressed to a divine being, (ii) U expresses a petition, entreaty, or act of thanksgiving, and (iii) S who utters U believes that the divine being is capable of hearing and responding to U.
conditions: U is addressed to a divine being
U expresses a petition, entreaty, or act of thanksgiving
S believes the divine being is capable of hearing and responding
counterexample: Consider the Buddhist practice of meditative recitation. A Theravada monk recites formulas expressing aspiration toward compassion and equanimity. The recitation has the form and function of prayer in many respects — it is solemn, regular, and spiritually purposive. Yet it is not addressed to a divine being; Theravada Buddhism does not posit a creator god. The first condition is not met, yet to deny that the monk's practice is a form of prayer would be to impose an unduly theistic conception on a broader phenomenon.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for prayer-like practices directed toward impersonal spiritual realities or toward the transformation of the practitioner's own mind, rather than toward a personal divine being.
passage: One might define prayer as an utterance addressed to a divine being, expressing a petition, entreaty, or thanksgiving, in the belief that the divine being can hear and respond. This definition fits the paradigmatic case of Western theistic prayer admirably: the Christian who kneels to petition God, the Jew who offers thanksgiving in the synagogue, the Muslim who supplicates Allah. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by certain Buddhist practices. A Theravada monk who engages in meditative recitation — expressing aspirations toward compassion, equanimity, and liberation — performs an activity that shares many features with what is ordinarily called prayer. The practice is solemn and ritualised; it is performed regularly; it serves a spiritual purpose. But Theravada Buddhism does not posit a personal creator god. The monk's recitation is not addressed to a divine being. It is directed, if anywhere, toward the transformation of his own mind and the cultivation of wholesome states. The first condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet to conclude that the monk's practice is simply not prayer would be to impose upon the concept an unduly narrow, specifically theistic form. A more comprehensive definition would need to allow for practices directed toward impersonal spiritual realities or toward the practitioner's own inner transformation.

definition: An object O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use by a community, (ii) O is regarded by that community as possessing a special connection to the divine, and (iii) O is treated with distinctive attitudes of reverence and awe.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use by a community
O is regarded as possessing a special connection to the divine
O is treated with distinctive attitudes of reverence and awe
counterexample: Consider the battlefield of Gettysburg as regarded in American civic life. The ground is set apart from ordinary use — it is preserved as a national park. It is treated with distinctive attitudes of reverence and awe — visitors speak in hushed tones and observe solemn rituals. Yet it is not regarded by the community as possessing a special connection to the divine. Its sacredness, if we may call it that, is civic and historical rather than theological. The second condition is not met, yet the term 'sacred' is commonly applied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the secular sacred — objects, places, or symbols that are set apart and treated with reverence on account of their historical, moral, or communal significance, without any connection to the divine.
passage: A natural analysis of the sacred identifies it with objects set apart from ordinary use, regarded as connected to the divine, and treated with reverence and awe. This definition captures many paradigmatic cases: the consecrated bread of the Eucharist, the Torah scrolls in a synagogue, the Black Stone in Mecca. Each is set apart, each is regarded as connected to the divine, and each is treated with profound reverence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by places of civic veneration. Consider the battlefield of Gettysburg. The ground has been set apart from ordinary use; it is preserved as a national memorial. Visitors approach it with attitudes of reverence and solemn awe; ceremonies are conducted there with ritual gravity. The word 'sacred' is commonly applied to it — Lincoln himself spoke of the ground as 'hallowed.' Yet the community does not regard Gettysburg as possessing a special connection to the divine. Its significance is historical and moral rather than theological. If we insist upon the divine connection, Gettysburg is not sacred, and the common usage of the term is simply mistaken. But it is at least as plausible that the common usage reveals a deficiency in the definition — that sacredness may attach to objects and places on account of their historical, moral, or communal weight, without any divine connection.

definition: A communication R constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) R originates from a divine source, (ii) R conveys propositional content that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason, and (iii) R is received by a human agent who recognises it as divine in origin.
conditions: R originates from a divine source
R conveys propositional content undiscoverable by unaided human reason
R is received by a human agent who recognises it as divine
counterexample: Consider the case of a mystic who reports an overwhelming experience of divine presence — a sense of unity with the ground of all being, a feeling of absolute dependence and love — but who is unable to articulate any propositional content whatever. She cannot state what was revealed; she can only report that she was in the presence of the divine. If we take her at her word regarding the divine source and her recognition of it, the first and third conditions are met. But no propositional content was conveyed. Yet many theologians would insist that such an experience constitutes a genuine revelation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for non-propositional revelation — the communication of divine presence, purpose, or character through experience rather than through articulable propositions.
passage: One might define divine revelation as a communication originating from a divine source, conveying propositional content that unaided reason could not discover, and received by a human agent who recognises its divine origin. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases familiar from Western theology: the giving of the Law at Sinai, the dictation of the Quran to Muhammad, the prophetic oracles of the Hebrew Bible. In each case, a divine source communicates specific propositions to a human recipient. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the mystical tradition. Consider a mystic who reports an experience of overwhelming divine presence — a sense of unity with the ground of being, an apprehension of absolute goodness and love — but who cannot articulate what was communicated in propositional form. She cannot say 'God told me that p.' She can only say that she was in the presence of the divine and that the experience transformed her understanding. If we grant the divine origin and the recognition, the first and third conditions are met. But the second condition requires propositional content, and none was conveyed. To exclude the mystic's experience from revelation seems to impose an unduly rationalistic conception of how the divine may communicate itself to human beings.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by God, (ii) God's command is communicated to moral agents through a recognised channel — scripture, prophecy, or the natural law — and (iii) the command is consistent with God's nature as perfectly good.
conditions: A is commanded by God
God's command is communicated through a recognised channel
The command is consistent with God's nature as perfectly good
counterexample: Consider the command recorded in Genesis 22, in which God instructs Abraham to sacrifice his son Isaac. The command is attributed to God and communicated through direct prophetic address — a recognised channel. The theological tradition holds that God's nature is perfectly good. Yet the command to kill an innocent child appears, on the face of it, inconsistent with perfect goodness. If we insist on the third condition, the command was not a genuine divine command; if we accept it as genuine, the third condition is not satisfied. Neither horn of the dilemma is comfortable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide a criterion for resolving cases in which the apparent content of a divine command conflicts with the requirement of consistency with divine goodness, leaving it unclear whether the command or the goodness condition should yield.
passage: The divine command theory, in one of its more refined formulations, holds that an action is morally obligatory if and only if it is commanded by God, communicated through a recognised channel, and consistent with God's perfectly good nature. The third condition represents an attempt to avoid the classical Euthyphro objection: if God's commands must be consistent with His goodness, then goodness is not arbitrary. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the binding of Isaac — the Akedah recorded in Genesis. God commands Abraham to sacrifice his son, his only son, whom he loves. The command is communicated through direct prophetic address, the most authoritative of channels. Yet the command appears to require the killing of an innocent child, which seems manifestly inconsistent with perfect goodness. If we apply the third condition rigorously, we must conclude that the command was not genuinely divine — but this contradicts the scriptural narrative. If we accept the narrative, we must concede that the third condition was not satisfied — but then the definition fails to secure the action as obligatory. The definition provides no guidance for resolving this tension, and it is precisely in such cases that guidance is most urgently needed.

definition: A person S possesses immortality if and only if (i) S survives the death of the body, (ii) S retains personal identity — that is, the surviving entity is the same person as the one who lived — and (iii) S's continued existence is unending.
conditions: S survives the death of the body
S retains personal identity after death
S's continued existence is unending
counterexample: Consider the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. According to this doctrine, something survives the death of the body — a stream of consciousness continues into a new life. And this stream continues without final end through countless rebirths. Yet Buddhist philosophy emphatically denies that there is a persisting self or personal identity that is transmitted. The doctrine of anatta (no-self) holds that what is reborn is not the same person. The second condition is not met, yet the tradition speaks of a form of continued existence beyond death.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate conceptions of post-mortem continuation in which survival occurs without the persistence of personal identity, as in traditions that affirm rebirth while denying a continuing self.
passage: A standard philosophical analysis of immortality holds that a person is immortal if and only if she survives bodily death, retains her personal identity, and continues to exist without end. This definition captures the Western theistic picture tolerably well: the Christian doctrine of the resurrection of the body, for instance, holds that the dead will rise as themselves and live forever. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. In the Buddhist teaching, something continues beyond the death of the body — a stream of consciousness, propelled by craving and conditioned by past actions, takes up residence in a new form. This continuation is, in principle, unending: the cycle of rebirth extends through countless lifetimes until the attainment of nirvana. The first and third conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied, at least on a broad reading. But the second condition is emphatically denied by the Buddhist tradition itself. The doctrine of anatta — no-self — holds that there is no persisting personal identity that passes from one life to the next. What is reborn is not the same person; it is, rather, a causally connected continuation without identity. If we insist upon the retention of personal identity, the Buddhist teaching does not qualify as a doctrine of immortality, which seems an unduly parochial conclusion.

definition: An experience E counts as a religious experience if and only if (i) E involves an awareness of a reality that transcends the ordinary empirical world, (ii) E is accompanied by characteristic affective states — awe, joy, dread, or a sense of absolute dependence — and (iii) E occurs within the context of a recognisable religious tradition.
conditions: E involves awareness of a reality transcending the ordinary empirical world
E is accompanied by characteristic affective states such as awe, joy, dread, or a sense of absolute dependence
E occurs within the context of a recognisable religious tradition
counterexample: Consider a solitary hiker who, upon reaching a mountain summit, is suddenly overwhelmed by a sense of the numinous — an apprehension of something vast and transcendent, accompanied by profound awe and a feeling of absolute dependence. She has no religious affiliation and the experience occurs outside any religious context. Yet the phenomenology of her experience is indistinguishable from what William James and Rudolf Otto described as paradigmatically religious. The third condition is not met, yet the experience shares every intrinsic feature with recognised religious experiences.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for spontaneous experiences that possess all the intrinsic phenomenological characteristics of religious experience but occur outside the framework of any established religious tradition.
passage: One might propose that a religious experience is an awareness of a transcendent reality, accompanied by characteristic affective states, occurring within the context of a recognisable religious tradition. This definition captures the typical cases: the Christian mystic's vision of the divine, the Sufi's ecstatic communion, the Buddhist monk's meditative insight. In each, the subject apprehends a transcendent reality, is moved by profound affect, and the experience takes place within a well-established religious framework. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by solitary experiences outside any religious context. Consider a hiker with no religious affiliation who reaches a remote summit and is suddenly seized by an overwhelming sense of the numinous. She apprehends something vast, transcendent, and wholly other. She is filled with awe and a sense of absolute dependence. The experience transforms her understanding of her place in the world. The phenomenology of her experience — its noetic quality, its affective intensity, its sense of contact with something beyond the empirical — matches what William James described as the hallmarks of religious experience. Yet she belongs to no tradition, and the experience occurs on a mountainside rather than in a church, a mosque, or a meditation hall. If we insist upon the third condition, her experience is not religious, a conclusion that seems to mistake the institutional setting for the essential character of the phenomenon.

definition: An argument A constitutes a proof of God's existence if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or necessarily true, (ii) A follows valid rules of deductive inference, and (iii) A's conclusion is that a being with the traditional divine attributes — omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness — exists.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or necessarily true
A follows valid deductive inference
A concludes that a being with omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness exists
counterexample: Consider a version of the cosmological argument that proceeds from the premise that contingent beings exist (which appears self-evident) and the premise that every contingent being requires a cause (which many regard as necessarily true), via valid deductive inference, to the conclusion that a necessary being exists. The argument is formally valid and the premises have strong claims to self-evidence. Yet the conclusion — that a necessary being exists — does not entail that this being possesses omnipotence, omniscience, or perfect goodness. The necessary being might be an impersonal first cause devoid of any of the traditional divine attributes. Condition (iii) is not met unless supplemented by further argumentation that the definition does not require.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the argument include a demonstration that the being whose existence is established possesses the specific attributes traditionally ascribed to God, rather than merely that some being of the requisite metaphysical status exists.
passage: One might propose that a proof of God's existence is an argument that proceeds from self-evident or necessarily true premises, follows valid deductive inference, and concludes that a being with the traditional divine attributes exists. This definition is demanding in a way that befits the gravity of the conclusion: it requires self-evidence or necessity in the premises, logical validity in the reasoning, and specificity in the conclusion. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the cosmological argument in one of its standard forms. The argument begins from the premise that contingent beings exist — a premise that is about as close to self-evidence as philosophical premises come — and adds the premise that every contingent being requires a cause, which many philosophers have regarded as necessarily true. By valid deductive reasoning, one concludes that there exists a being that is not contingent — a necessary being that is the ultimate cause of all contingent existence. The inference is valid and the premises are plausible. But the conclusion — that a necessary being exists — falls short of condition (iii). A necessary being is not, as such, omnipotent; it need not be omniscient; and there is nothing in the argument to show that it is perfectly good. The necessary being might be an impersonal metaphysical principle entirely devoid of the attributes that make the God of theism an object of worship. The definition requires that the conclusion affirm the existence of a being with the traditional divine attributes, but the most powerful deductive arguments for a first cause or necessary being characteristically establish the existence of something far more abstract than the God of religion.

definition: The existence of evil constitutes a decisive objection to theism if and only if (i) evil exists in significant quantity, (ii) a perfectly good, omnipotent, and omniscient being would prevent all evil that it could prevent, and (iii) such a being could prevent all evil.
conditions: Evil exists in significant quantity
A perfectly good, omnipotent, omniscient being would prevent all preventable evil
Such a being could prevent all evil
counterexample: Consider the suffering involved in an earthquake that destroys a village, killing many innocent people. Evil exists here in significant quantity. A perfectly good being, one might think, would prevent such suffering if it could. An omnipotent being, by definition, can do anything that is logically possible, and preventing an earthquake is certainly logically possible. Yet a theist might argue that the natural laws that produce earthquakes are necessary for the existence of a stable, law-governed universe in which rational agents can make predictions, exercise foresight, and develop science — and that such a universe is a greater good that could not be achieved without the possibility of natural disasters. If this is so, then condition (iii) is complicated: the being could prevent the earthquake but not without sacrificing a good — a law-governed universe — that outweighs the evil of the earthquake.
missing_condition: The definition fails to consider whether the prevention of particular evils might require the sacrifice of greater goods that are logically inseparable from the conditions that produce those evils.
passage: One might formulate the problem of evil as follows: evil exists in significant quantity; a perfectly good, omnipotent, and omniscient being would prevent all evil it could prevent; such a being could prevent all evil; therefore, no such being exists. The argument appears straightforward and powerful. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the third condition is natural evil — the suffering caused by earthquakes, floods, disease, and the like. The evil is real and significant; a perfectly good being would, one supposes, prevent it if it could. And an omnipotent being can certainly prevent an earthquake. But the theist may reply that the natural laws which produce earthquakes are the same laws that make the universe a stable, predictable environment in which rational agents can form expectations, develop science, and exercise genuine agency. A universe without the possibility of natural disaster might be a universe without the regularities that rational life requires. If the good of a law-governed universe cannot be achieved without the possibility of natural calamity, then the omnipotent being cannot prevent the earthquake without sacrificing that good — not because of a limitation in power, but because the goods are logically intertwined. The definition treats omnipotent prevention as unproblematic, but the relationship between particular evils and the broader goods of a law-governed universe complicates the third condition in ways that the definition does not anticipate.

definition: An event E qualifies as a miracle if and only if (i) E violates a well-established law of nature, (ii) E has religious significance for the community that reports it, and (iii) E is attested by multiple independent witnesses.
conditions: E violates a well-established law of nature
E has religious significance for the reporting community
E is attested by multiple independent witnesses
counterexample: Consider the stigmata reported to have appeared on the body of St Francis of Assisi. The appearance of wounds corresponding to the wounds of the crucifixion has been reported on numerous occasions throughout Christian history. The event has profound religious significance for the Catholic community. And in the case of St Francis, it was attested by multiple companions. Yet the appearance of wounds on the body does not clearly violate any well-established law of nature — psychosomatic phenomena, including the production of skin lesions under conditions of extreme psychological stress, are documented in the medical literature. Condition (i) is doubtful, yet the stigmata of St Francis are regarded by the Catholic tradition as among the most celebrated miracles.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate events that are regarded as miraculous not because they violate natural law but because their timing, their correspondence to religious expectation, and their spiritual significance are taken to reveal divine agency operating within the natural order.
passage: One might propose that a miracle is an event that violates a well-established law of nature, has religious significance for the community that reports it, and is attested by multiple independent witnesses. The requirement of a violation of natural law ensures that the event is genuinely extraordinary; the requirement of religious significance ensures that it is not merely a curiosity; and the requirement of multiple witnesses provides a measure of evidential support. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the stigmata of St Francis of Assisi. The appearance of wounds on the saint's body, corresponding to the five wounds of Christ, was reported by several of his companions and has been a central element of Franciscan devotion for centuries. The event has religious significance of the highest order. And it is multiply attested. But the appearance of wounds on the body does not unambiguously violate any law of nature. Medical literature documents cases of psychosomatic skin lesions appearing under conditions of intense emotional or psychological stress. The phenomenon is unusual but not clearly supernatural. Condition (i) — the violation of natural law — is at best uncertain. Yet the Catholic tradition regards the stigmata of St Francis as one of the most important miracles in the history of the Church. The definition, by insisting upon a violation of natural law, cannot accommodate events whose miraculous character is held to consist not in their physical impossibility but in their providential timing and their correspondence to a pattern of religious meaning.

definition: A person S has religious faith if and only if (i) S assents to a set of propositions concerning the divine, (ii) S's assent is voluntary rather than compelled by evidence, and (iii) S regards the propositions as of supreme importance for the conduct of life.
conditions: S assents to propositions concerning the divine
S's assent is voluntary rather than compelled by evidence
S regards the propositions as of supreme importance
counterexample: Consider a mystic who reports a direct and overwhelming experience of the divine — an experience so vivid and compelling that assent to the reality of God is not voluntary but felt as unavoidable, as natural and irresistible as the assent one gives to the existence of the physical world when one opens one's eyes. The mystic's assent concerns the divine and is regarded as of supreme importance. But it is not voluntary in the relevant sense — the mystic does not choose to believe but finds belief thrust upon her by the force of the experience. Condition (ii) is not met, yet the mystic's state is paradigmatically one of religious faith, and indeed of an unusually intense and certain variety.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that religious faith may be grounded in experiential encounter rather than voluntary assent, and that the most intense forms of faith are often characterised precisely by the felt impossibility of withholding assent.
passage: One might propose that religious faith consists in the voluntary assent to propositions concerning the divine that are regarded as of supreme importance for the conduct of life. The requirement of voluntariness captures the widespread intuition that faith is a choice — that it involves a commitment that goes beyond what the evidence compels. The requirement of supreme importance captures the seriousness and centrality of faith in the life of the believer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the faith of the mystic. The great mystics of the Christian, Islamic, and Hindu traditions — Teresa of Avila, al-Ghazali, Ramakrishna — report experiences of the divine that are not voluntary acts of the will but overwhelming encounters that compel assent as irresistibly as sensory experience compels assent to the existence of the material world. Teresa describes the experience of divine presence not as something she chose to believe but as something she could not help but recognise. Her assent to the reality of the divine was not an exercise of will in the face of insufficient evidence; it was, by her account, as immediate and involuntary as the perception of light. The propositions concern the divine, and she regards them as supremely important. But the assent is not voluntary — it is experientially compelled. The definition, by requiring voluntariness, excludes precisely those cases of faith that the religious traditions themselves regard as the most profound and authentic. One should hesitate to define faith in a way that excludes the mystics.

definition: An act A constitutes a prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, (ii) A expresses a request for some state of affairs to be brought about, and (iii) the person performing A believes that the divine being is capable of fulfilling the request.
conditions: A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being
A expresses a request for some state of affairs
The person believes the divine being is capable of fulfilling the request
counterexample: Consider the practice of contemplative prayer in the Christian monastic tradition — the prayer of silence, of simple presence before God, as practised by the Desert Fathers and later by the author of The Cloud of Unknowing. The practitioner addresses God, and believes in God's power. But the prayer does not express a request for any state of affairs to be brought about. The practitioner seeks nothing, asks for nothing, and desires nothing except to be present to the divine. The prayer is an act of attention, not of petition. Condition (ii) is not met, yet contemplative prayer is regarded by its practitioners as the highest form of prayer.
missing_condition: The definition reduces prayer to petition, failing to recognise that prayer encompasses acts of adoration, thanksgiving, confession, and contemplation that do not involve requesting any state of affairs.
passage: One might propose that a prayer is a verbal or mental address to a divine being expressing a request for some state of affairs, performed by one who believes the divine being capable of granting it. This definition captures the petitionary prayer that is perhaps the most familiar form — the prayer for health, for guidance, for the safety of loved ones. It is specific and testable: one can determine whether an act of prayer has occurred by asking whether a request was made and whether the person believed in the recipient's power. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the practice of contemplative prayer in the Christian monastic tradition. The Desert Fathers of the fourth century, the mediaeval mystics, and the author of The Cloud of Unknowing all describe a form of prayer that consists not in asking for anything but in the simple act of being present to God in silence. The practitioner empties the mind of all particular desires and requests, seeking only the awareness of the divine presence. There is a verbal or mental address — the practitioner directs attention towards God. There is a belief in God's power. But there is no request for any state of affairs to be brought about. The prayer of contemplation is not a petition; it is an act of attention, of self-surrender, of pure adoration. The definition, by making petition a necessary condition, excludes what the monastic tradition itself regards as the highest and most perfect form of prayer.

definition: An object, place, or time O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use by the community that regards it as sacred, (ii) O is associated with a divine being or transcendent reality, and (iii) contact with O is governed by specific ritual prescriptions.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use
O is associated with a divine being or transcendent reality
Contact with O is governed by specific ritual prescriptions
counterexample: Consider the reverence accorded to ancient trees in the Shinto tradition. A particular camphor tree, centuries old, may be encircled by a shimenawa — a sacred rope — and treated with deep reverence by the local community. The tree is set apart from ordinary use; no one would cut it for timber. Contact is governed by ritual prescriptions. Yet the tree is not necessarily associated with a specific divine being or transcendent reality in the way the definition requires. The reverence may be directed at the tree itself — at its age, its vitality, its rootedness in the landscape — rather than at any deity or transcendent entity. Condition (ii) is not met, yet the tree is sacred in the fullest Shinto sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires association with a deity or transcendent reality, but in certain religious traditions — notably animist and Shinto traditions — the sacred may inhere in natural objects themselves without reference to any being or reality beyond them.
passage: One might propose that something is sacred when it is set apart from ordinary use, associated with a divine being or transcendent reality, and surrounded by ritual prescriptions governing contact with it. This definition accommodates the paradigmatic cases of Western monotheism readily: the Sabbath is set apart from ordinary time, associated with God's rest on the seventh day, and governed by elaborate ritual rules. The Temple in Jerusalem was set apart from ordinary space, associated with the presence of God, and governed by detailed prescriptions about who might enter and when. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the sacred tree in the Shinto tradition. An ancient camphor tree, encircled by a shimenawa, is treated with the deepest reverence by the community. It is set apart from ordinary use — it would be unthinkable to fell it. Contact with it is governed by ritual prescriptions. But the tree is not associated with a specific divine being or transcendent reality in the sense the definition requires. The reverence may be directed at the tree itself, at its age and vitality, at its embodiment of the natural forces that sustain the landscape. There need be no deity behind the tree, no transcendent reality to which it points. The tree is sacred in itself. The definition, constructed from cases in which the sacred is always referential — pointing beyond itself to a deity — cannot accommodate traditions in which the sacred is immanent, inhering in the object itself.

definition: A communication R constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) R originates from a divine source, (ii) R conveys propositional content that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason, and (iii) R is received by a human agent who recognises it as divine in origin.
conditions: R originates from a divine source
R conveys propositional content undiscoverable by unaided reason
R is received by a human agent who recognises its divine origin
counterexample: Consider the experience of a prophet who is seized by an overwhelming vision — a torrent of images, emotions, and impressions that cannot be reduced to propositional form. The prophet feels certain that the experience is of divine origin. The experience transforms the prophet's apprehension of the world and of the divine will. Yet the revelation does not convey propositional content in any clear sense — it is not a set of statements that could be true or false but an experiential encounter that resists linguistic formulation. The prophet may later attempt to express the content in words, but the words are acknowledged to be inadequate to the experience. Condition (ii) is not met in its propositional requirement, yet the traditions of Israel, Islam, and Christianity all regard such visionary experiences as among the most important forms of revelation.
missing_condition: The definition restricts revelation to propositional communication, failing to recognise that many of the most important revelations reported in the major traditions are non-propositional in character — consisting in experiential encounters, visions, or transformations of understanding that resist formulation as a set of truth-bearing statements.
passage: One might propose that a divine revelation is a communication originating from a divine source, conveying propositional content undiscoverable by unaided reason, received by a human agent who recognises its divine origin. This definition captures one important model of revelation — the model in which God communicates truths to a prophet in a form that can be expressed in language and transmitted to others. The content of the Torah, the Quran, and the Christian creeds can all be understood, on one reading, as propositional revelation of this kind. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the prophetic vision. When Ezekiel describes his vision of the chariot of God, or when Muhammad describes the Night Journey, the revelation takes the form not of a set of propositions but of an overwhelming experiential encounter — a vision of unearthly beings, a sensation of being transported beyond the ordinary world, a torrent of imagery that resists reduction to statements. The prophet may subsequently attempt to render the vision in language, but the language is universally acknowledged to be inadequate. The revelation, in its primary form, is not propositional; it is experiential. It originates from a divine source, and the prophet recognises it as such. But it does not convey propositional content in the way the definition requires. The definition, by insisting upon propositional content, excludes some of the most celebrated and consequential revelations in the history of religious thought.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) God commands that A be performed, (ii) the command is clearly communicated to the agent through scripture, tradition, or direct revelation, and (iii) the agent is capable of performing A.
conditions: God commands that A be performed
The command is clearly communicated through scripture, tradition, or revelation
The agent is capable of performing A
counterexample: Consider a person living in a remote community that has never received any scriptural, traditional, or revelatory communication from the God of any major religion. This person performs acts of extraordinary generosity and compassion — feeding the hungry, caring for the sick, protecting the vulnerable — that are widely regarded as morally obligatory. No divine command has been communicated to her through any of the channels specified in condition (ii). Yet it would be perverse to say that her actions are not morally obligatory, or that she is under no moral obligation to perform them. The definition makes moral obligation dependent upon the receipt of a divine communication, but the person's obligations appear to obtain regardless of whether any such communication has been received.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the moral obligations of persons who have not received any divine communication through the specified channels, thereby making the existence of moral obligation contingent upon a communicative condition — the receipt of the command — rather than upon the command itself.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory if and only if God commands it, the command is clearly communicated through scripture, tradition, or direct revelation, and the agent is capable of performing it. This definition ties moral obligation firmly to divine authority and adds a condition of communicative clarity that ensures the agent can be held responsible — one cannot be obligated by a command one has never received. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis concerns the person who has received no divine communication whatsoever. Consider a woman living in a remote community that has had no contact with any of the major scriptural or revelatory traditions. She has received no communication from God through scripture, tradition, or revelation. She is, by the definition's lights, under no moral obligation at all, since condition (ii) is not satisfied for any action. Yet this woman may perform acts of extraordinary moral worth — sheltering refugees, tending the sick, sharing her meagre resources with the destitute — that every reflective person would recognise as morally obligatory. The definition implies that her actions, however admirable, are not the fulfilment of obligations, since no divine command has reached her. This consequence seems intolerable. It makes the existence of moral obligation depend not upon God's will but upon the accidents of geography and cultural transmission. One might observe that the definition confuses the metaphysical ground of obligation with its communicative transmission, and that a divine-command theory must account for the obligations of those to whom the commands have not been communicated.

definition: A person P possesses immortality if and only if (i) P survives the death of the body, (ii) P retains personal identity — that is, the surviving entity is the same person as the one who died, and (iii) the survival is permanent, admitting of no eventual cessation.
conditions: P survives the death of the body
P retains personal identity after death
The survival is permanent
counterexample: Consider the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. According to this doctrine, something survives the death of the body and continues into a new life. The process of rebirth is, in many Buddhist traditions, potentially unending — it continues until nirvana is achieved. Yet Buddhist philosophy explicitly denies that there is a persisting self or person that undergoes rebirth. What continues is a causal stream of mental events — the sankhara or formations — not a person. Condition (ii) is not met, since there is no personal identity that is retained. Yet the doctrine of rebirth provides a form of survival after death that is of profound religious significance in the Buddhist tradition and functions, within that tradition, as an analogue of immortality.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a conception of personal identity as a persisting substantial self, and thereby excludes conceptions of post-mortem survival — such as the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth — in which continuity is causal rather than substantial and no persisting self is posited.
passage: One might propose that immortality consists in the survival of a person beyond the death of the body, with retention of personal identity, in a permanent mode that admits of no cessation. This definition captures the conception of immortality that is characteristic of the Abrahamic religions: the person — the same person who lived and died — continues to exist after death, and continues forever. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. In the Buddhist understanding, something survives the death of the body: a causal stream of mental formations continues and gives rise to a new existence. The process may continue through innumerable lifetimes, potentially without end, until nirvana is achieved. But the Buddhist tradition explicitly rejects the notion of a persisting self or soul that undergoes this process. The doctrine of anatta — no-self — denies that there is a person, in the substantial sense, who is reborn. What continues is a causal sequence, not an enduring substance. The definition requires that the surviving entity be the same person as the one who died — that personal identity be retained — and the Buddhist tradition denies precisely this. Yet the doctrine of rebirth functions within Buddhism as a form of survival after death of the deepest religious significance, motivating ethical conduct, sustaining hope, and structuring the entire Buddhist understanding of human existence. The definition, by requiring personal identity in the substantial sense, cannot accommodate a conception of post-mortem survival that is of central importance to one of the world's great religions.

definition: An experience E qualifies as a religious experience if and only if (i) the subject of E reports a vivid sense of contact with a reality that transcends the ordinary world, (ii) E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's attitudes, values, or behaviour, and (iii) E occurs within the context of an established religious tradition.
conditions: The subject reports vivid contact with a transcendent reality
E produces lasting transformation in attitudes, values, or behaviour
E occurs within an established religious tradition
counterexample: Consider the experience reported by the French mathematician Blaise Pascal on the night of 23 November 1654 — the 'night of fire' in which he experienced an overwhelming sense of the presence of God, recording his experience on a scrap of paper sewn into the lining of his coat. The experience was vivid; it transformed his life, turning him from mathematics to religion. But Pascal was not, at the time, a practising member of any religious order or community in the relevant sense; he was a layman and a scientist who was undergoing a crisis of belief. The experience did not occur within the context of an established religious tradition in any straightforward sense — it occurred in solitude, outside any liturgical or institutional setting. Condition (iii) is at best tenuously met, yet the experience is universally regarded as one of the most important religious experiences in the history of Christianity.
missing_condition: The definition ties religious experience to institutional context, failing to recognise that many of the most celebrated and consequential religious experiences occur outside the framework of established traditions, in moments of solitary crisis or spontaneous encounter.
passage: One might propose that a religious experience is one in which the subject reports vivid contact with a transcendent reality, the experience produces lasting transformation in the subject's life, and it occurs within the context of an established religious tradition. The requirement of institutional context is intended to distinguish genuine religious experiences from merely unusual psychological episodes: the experience is authenticated, as it were, by its embeddedness in a tradition that provides the interpretive framework and the vocabulary for understanding it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement is the experience of Pascal on the night of 23 November 1654. Pascal recorded on a scrap of paper, later found sewn into the lining of his coat, an overwhelming encounter with the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — an encounter characterised by fire, certainty, and joy. The experience was vivid beyond question. It transformed his life permanently, turning him from mathematics and natural philosophy to the religious and apologetic writings that occupied the rest of his days. But the experience did not occur within the context of an established religious tradition in any clear sense. Pascal was alone in his room. He was not attending a service, following a liturgy, or practising a recognised form of devotion. He was a layman in the midst of a spiritual crisis. The experience came to him unbidden, outside any institutional framework. To exclude it from the category of religious experience on the ground that it lacked an institutional context would be to exclude one of the most celebrated and consequential religious experiences in the history of Christianity. The definition mistakes a frequent accompaniment of religious experience for a necessary condition.

definition: An argument A constitutes a valid proof of the existence of God if and only if (i) the premises of A are self-evident or knowable a priori, (ii) the conclusion of A asserts the existence of a being possessing the traditional divine attributes, and (iii) the inference from premises to conclusion is deductively valid.
conditions: The premises of A are self-evident or knowable a priori
The conclusion asserts the existence of a being possessing the traditional divine attributes
The inference from premises to conclusion is deductively valid
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument in its classical form. The premise — that God is that than which nothing greater can be conceived — is presented as self-evident or at least knowable by reflection alone. The conclusion asserts the existence of a being possessing maximal perfection. The inference is deductively valid given the premise that existence is a perfection. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to accept the argument as a proof, for the premise that existence is a perfection — that a being which exists is, other things being equal, greater than one which does not — is precisely what is at issue. The argument's validity depends upon a premise that is not self-evident but philosophically contentious.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises be not merely self-evident in appearance but genuinely uncontroversial, so that the proof does not beg the question by embedding the very point at issue within a premise presented as self-evident.
passage: One might propose that a valid proof of the existence of God consists in an argument whose premises are self-evident or knowable a priori, whose conclusion asserts the existence of a being with the traditional divine attributes, and whose inference is deductively valid. This proposal captures the aspirations of natural theology in its most ambitious form. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the ontological argument. In its classical formulation, the argument begins from the premise that God is that than which nothing greater can be conceived, and proceeds to the conclusion that such a being must exist, on the ground that a being which exists is greater, other things being equal, than one which does not. The premise is presented as self-evident — who could deny that God, if He exists, is that than which nothing greater can be conceived? The conclusion asserts the existence of a being with maximal perfection. The inference is deductively valid if one grants the intermediate premise that existence is a perfection. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that the crucial premise — that existence is a perfection — is not self-evident but is itself the point at issue. To treat it as self-evident is to assume what the argument purports to prove. The definition, by requiring only that the premises be self-evident, does not guard against the possibility that a premise may appear self-evident whilst in fact smuggling in the very conclusion it is meant to establish.

definition: The existence of suffering constitutes a decisive objection to theism if and only if (i) the suffering in question is genuinely gratuitous — that is, it serves no greater good, (ii) an omnipotent being could have prevented the suffering, and (iii) an omnibenevolent being would have prevented any suffering it could have prevented.
conditions: The suffering is genuinely gratuitous — serving no greater good
An omnipotent being could have prevented the suffering
An omnibenevolent being would have prevented any suffering it could have prevented
counterexample: Consider the suffering of an animal caught in a forest fire ignited by lightning in a remote wilderness, where no human being is present to witness the event or learn from it. The suffering appears gratuitous — it serves no moral education, builds no character, tests no faith. An omnipotent being could have prevented the lightning strike or redirected the fire. Yet whether an omnibenevolent being would have prevented it depends upon assumptions about the nature of benevolence that the definition does not examine. If benevolence requires respect for the regular operation of natural laws, then the omnibenevolent being might refrain from intervention not from indifference but from a commitment to the integrity of the natural order.
missing_condition: The definition assumes without argument that omnibenevolence entails the prevention of all preventable suffering, when it is at least conceivable that a benevolent being might have reasons — such as the preservation of a law-governed natural order — for permitting suffering that serves no good discernible to us.
passage: One might hold that the existence of suffering constitutes a decisive objection to theism if the suffering is genuinely gratuitous, if an omnipotent being could have prevented it, and if an omnibenevolent being would have prevented any suffering within its power to prevent. This formulation has considerable force, and it captures the intuitive pull of the argument from suffering against the existence of God. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Consider the suffering of a wild animal caught in a forest fire ignited by a lightning strike in a wilderness where no human being is present. The suffering appears to serve no greater good: no moral lesson is learned, no character is built, no soul is tried and strengthened. An omnipotent being could easily have prevented the lightning strike or diverted the fire. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet condition (iii) rests upon an assumption about the nature of omnibenevolence that is less obvious than it first appears. One might observe that benevolence, even omnibenevolence, need not entail the prevention of every preventable suffering. If the regular operation of natural laws is itself a good — if a world governed by reliable regularities is preferable to one subject to constant miraculous intervention — then a benevolent being might have reason to permit the fire and its attendant suffering. The definition, by treating condition (iii) as unproblematic, forecloses the possibility that omnibenevolence is compatible with the permission of natural suffering.

definition: An event M is a miracle if and only if (i) M is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) M is brought about by the direct intervention of God, and (iii) M serves a discernible religious or moral purpose.
conditions: M is a violation of a law of nature
M is brought about by the direct intervention of God
M serves a discernible religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Consider a remarkable coincidence in which a drought-stricken community prays for rain and, on the very day of their collective prayer, an unforeseen weather system produces abundant rainfall. No law of nature is violated — the rain is produced by ordinary meteorological processes. Yet the timing is so extraordinary and the correspondence with the community's petition so precise that the event is regarded by the community, with apparent reasonableness, as a miraculous answer to prayer. The event satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) on the community's understanding but not condition (i).
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring a violation of natural law, thereby excluding events that are extraordinary in their timing or circumstances without involving any physical impossibility, and that are reasonably regarded as miraculous by those who experience them.
passage: A familiar definition holds that a miracle is a violation of a law of nature brought about by God's direct intervention for a discernible religious or moral purpose. This definition, which owes much to the discussion of miracles in the eighteenth century, has the merit of distinguishing miracles from merely unusual events. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers events that are extraordinary not in their physical character but in their timing and circumstances. Suppose a community suffering from severe drought gathers to pray for rain. On the very day of their prayer, an entirely unforeseen weather system develops and produces abundant rainfall. No law of nature is violated — the rain is produced by the ordinary processes of atmospheric circulation and condensation. A meteorologist, examining the event after the fact, could identify the physical causes of the rain. Yet the temporal coincidence between the prayer and the rainfall is so striking, and the correspondence between the community's petition and the event so precise, that the community regards the rainfall as a miraculous answer to prayer. One might observe that to exclude such events from the category of the miraculous merely because no physical law was violated is to adopt too narrow a conception of what constitutes a miracle. It appears that the definition fails to accommodate the category of providential timing — events that are physically ordinary but circumstantially extraordinary.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) S lacks conclusive evidence for p, and (iii) S is disposed to act on the basis of p despite the absence of conclusive evidence.
conditions: S believes that p
S lacks conclusive evidence for p
S is disposed to act on the basis of p despite the absence of conclusive evidence
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who has faith that his rope will hold, in circumstances where he possesses extensive evidence for its reliability — he has tested it, inspected it, and used it many times before. He believes the rope will hold, he acts on that belief, and yet his faith in the rope does not seem to require the absence of conclusive evidence. Indeed, it appears that his faith is made more rational, not less, by the abundance of his evidence. He has faith in the rope precisely because the evidence supports his trust.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires the absence of conclusive evidence as a necessary condition of faith, thereby excluding cases in which faith is exercised in the presence of strong evidence and is better understood as a disposition of trust or commitment that may be supported, rather than undermined, by evidence.
passage: One might propose that a person has faith in a proposition if and only if she believes it, lacks conclusive evidence for it, and is disposed to act on it despite the absence of such evidence. This proposal captures a common understanding of faith as belief that goes beyond the evidence. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers ordinary cases of trust. A mountaineer who has faith that his rope will hold need not lack evidence for his belief. He may have tested the rope, inspected it for fraying, and used it successfully on many previous occasions. His evidence may be abundant and, for practical purposes, conclusive. Yet it would be natural to say that he has faith in the rope — that he trusts it with his life. His faith does not consist in believing something for which he lacks evidence; it consists in committing himself to act upon a belief that is, as it happens, well supported. One might observe that the definition, by requiring the absence of conclusive evidence, conflates faith with credulity and fails to recognise that faith may be a species of trust that is entirely compatible with, and indeed strengthened by, evidential support. It appears that the essential element of faith is not evidential deficiency but personal commitment.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) God commands that A be performed, (ii) the command is issued with the intention that A promote the good, and (iii) the command is accessible to human beings through revelation or conscience.
conditions: God commands that A be performed
The command is issued with the intention that A promote the good
The command is accessible to human beings through revelation or conscience
counterexample: Consider the case of Abraham, who is commanded by God to sacrifice his son Isaac. The command is divine, and it is accessible to Abraham through direct revelation. If we assume that God issues all commands with the intention that they promote the good, then condition (ii) is satisfied. All three conditions are met, and the action is therefore morally obligatory on the definition's account. Yet one should hesitate to say that the killing of an innocent child is morally obligatory, and the case reveals that the definition provides no independent standard by which the content of divine commands may be assessed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide a criterion for evaluating the moral content of divine commands independently of their divine origin, so that a command whose content is prima facie morally abhorrent cannot be distinguished from a genuinely obligatory command.
passage: One might hold that an action is morally obligatory if and only if God commands it, the command is issued with the intention of promoting the good, and the command is accessible to human beings through revelation or conscience. This account ties moral obligation directly to divine authority and has been influential in the history of moral philosophy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the binding of Isaac. Abraham is commanded by God to sacrifice his son. The command is manifestly divine — it comes directly from God. It is accessible to Abraham through revelation. And if we assume, as the definition invites us to do, that God's commands are always intended to promote the good, then condition (ii) is met. On the definition's account, Abraham is morally obligated to kill his innocent son. Yet one might observe that this conclusion is deeply troubling. The willingness to call the killing of an innocent child morally obligatory, simply because it is divinely commanded, suggests that the definition lacks any independent criterion for assessing the moral content of commands. It appears that a satisfactory account of moral obligation must provide some standard by which the content of a command — even a divine command — may be evaluated on its own merits, independently of its source.

definition: An experience E is a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves an apparent awareness of a transcendent reality, (ii) E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's character or conduct, and (iii) E is reported by the subject as qualitatively distinct from ordinary experience.
conditions: E involves an apparent awareness of a transcendent reality
E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's character or conduct
E is reported as qualitatively distinct from ordinary experience
counterexample: Consider a person who ingests a psychoactive substance and reports an overwhelming sense of unity with a transcendent reality, qualitatively unlike any ordinary experience. In the following months, the person undergoes a lasting transformation of character — becoming more compassionate, less anxious, and more oriented toward contemplative practice. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one should hesitate to classify the experience as a genuine religious experience, for the apparent awareness of transcendent reality was induced by a chemical agent rather than arising spontaneously or through spiritual discipline.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to impose any requirement concerning the cause or occasion of the experience, thereby failing to distinguish experiences induced by pharmacological or pathological means from those that arise through spiritual practice, prayer, or the spontaneous operation of the subject's faculties.
passage: One might define a genuine religious experience as one that involves an apparent awareness of a transcendent reality, produces a lasting transformation in the subject's character, and is reported as qualitatively distinct from ordinary experience. This definition captures the features most commonly cited by those who report such experiences: the sense of contact with something beyond the mundane, the transformative effect upon one's life, and the radical novelty of the experience itself. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers experiences induced by psychoactive substances. A person who ingests psilocybin may report an overwhelming sense of unity with a transcendent reality, an experience qualitatively unlike anything encountered in ordinary life. In the months that follow, she may undergo a lasting transformation of character — becoming more compassionate, more generous, and more attentive to the needs of others. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the experience was produced by a chemical agent, not by prayer, meditation, or the spontaneous operation of the subject's spiritual faculties. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenological character and the effects of the experience, cannot distinguish between experiences whose cause gives them a claim to religious significance and those whose cause does not.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a prayer if and only if (i) U is addressed to God or a divine being, (ii) U expresses a request, petition, or supplication, and (iii) the speaker sincerely believes that the addressee is capable of granting the request.
conditions: U is addressed to God or a divine being
U expresses a request, petition, or supplication
The speaker sincerely believes the addressee is capable of granting the request
counterexample: Consider a monk who spends his days in contemplative prayer, sitting in silence and attending to the presence of God without formulating any request or petition. His practice is universally recognised as prayer — indeed, as a particularly elevated form of prayer. He addresses himself to God, and he believes in God's power. Yet he expresses no request, petition, or supplication. His prayer is one of pure attention, not of asking.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring a request or petition, thereby excluding contemplative, meditative, and adorational forms of prayer in which the essential act is one of attention, praise, or silent communion rather than supplication.
passage: One might define prayer as an utterance addressed to God or a divine being that expresses a request or supplication and is spoken by one who sincerely believes the addressee capable of granting the request. This definition captures the petitionary prayers that form the most familiar type of religious address. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the contemplative prayer practised in monastic traditions. A Carthusian monk who sits in silence for hours, attending to the presence of God, makes no request and formulates no petition. He does not ask for anything — not for health, not for wisdom, not for the well-being of others. His prayer consists entirely in a silent, sustained attention to the divine. It would be recognised by any competent theologian as prayer, and indeed as a form of prayer more advanced than mere petition. Yet condition (ii) is not satisfied, for no request or supplication is expressed. One might observe that the definition, by restricting prayer to the petitionary mode, fails to accommodate the rich variety of forms that prayer takes in the great religious traditions — contemplation, adoration, thanksgiving, and simple attendance upon the divine presence.

definition: A communication C constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) C originates from God, (ii) C discloses truths that could not be discovered by human reason alone, and (iii) C is received by a specific individual or community at a determinate time and place.
conditions: C originates from God
C discloses truths that could not be discovered by human reason alone
C is received by a specific individual or community at a determinate time and place
counterexample: Consider the view, held by many theologians, that God reveals Himself through the natural world — through the beauty and order of creation, through the moral law written upon the human heart. This general revelation is held to originate from God and to disclose truths about God's nature — His power, His wisdom, His goodness. Yet it is not received by a specific individual or community at a determinate time and place; it is available to all persons at all times through the exercise of their natural faculties. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, yet theologians have traditionally regarded this as a genuine form of revelation.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring reception at a determinate time and place, thereby excluding general or natural revelation — the disclosure of divine truth through the created order — which is available universally and continuously rather than at a specific historical moment.
passage: A familiar definition holds that divine revelation is a communication originating from God that discloses truths beyond the reach of human reason and is received by a specific individual or community at a determinate time and place. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases of special revelation — the giving of the Torah at Sinai, the visions of the prophets, the Incarnation. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers what theologians have called general or natural revelation. On this view, God reveals Himself not only through specific historical communications but through the order and beauty of the created world, through the moral law discernible by conscience, and through the rational structure of the universe. Such revelation originates from God and discloses truths about His nature — His power, wisdom, and goodness — that are, on this account, genuinely communicated by the divine. Yet it is not received by a specific individual at a determinate time. It is available to all persons at all times through the exercise of their natural capacities. One might observe that the definition, by restricting revelation to discrete historical events, fails to accommodate this widespread theological tradition and thereby narrows the concept of revelation beyond what the major religious traditions would accept.
