definition: An object or place O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use, (ii) O is regarded by a community as having a special connection to the divine, and (iii) O is treated with distinctive forms of reverence or ritual behaviour.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use
O is regarded by a community as having a special connection to the divine
O is treated with distinctive forms of reverence or ritual behaviour
counterexample: Consider a battlefield where a great and terrible slaughter took place — Gettysburg, Thermopylae, the Somme. The ground is set apart from ordinary use; it is treated with reverence and ritual behaviour — ceremonies of remembrance, the laying of wreaths, the maintenance of memorials. Yet the community does not regard the battlefield as having a special connection to the divine. Its sacredness, if we may call it that, derives from the sacrifice of those who died there, not from any association with God or the transcendent. The site satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but not (ii).
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring a connection to the divine, thereby excluding objects and places that are experienced as sacred in virtue of their association with profound human experiences — sacrifice, suffering, moral commitment — without any reference to transcendent or divine reality.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use, regarded by a community as having a special connection to the divine, and treated with distinctive forms of reverence or ritual behaviour. This definition captures the sacred as it appears in the major theistic traditions — the temple, the altar, the consecrated host. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers sites of profound human sacrifice. A battlefield such as the Somme, where hundreds of thousands of young men perished in circumstances of almost inconceivable horror, is set apart from ordinary use and is treated with elaborate forms of reverence — annual ceremonies of remembrance, the laying of wreaths, the maintenance of cemeteries and memorials. Visitors to the site report a sense of awe and solemnity that is, phenomenologically, difficult to distinguish from the experience of the sacred. Yet the community that maintains the site does not typically regard it as having a special connection to the divine. Its sacredness — if the word is appropriate — derives from the enormity of the sacrifice and the depth of the obligation felt toward the dead. One might observe that the definition, by tying the sacred exclusively to the divine, fails to accommodate a category of experience that is widespread in secular societies and that bears all the marks of the sacred except the theological.

definition: A person P survives death if and only if (i) there exists after P's bodily death an entity that possesses P's memories, (ii) this entity possesses P's character traits and dispositions, and (iii) this entity is conscious and capable of experience.
conditions: There exists after bodily death an entity possessing P's memories
The entity possesses P's character traits and dispositions
The entity is conscious and capable of experience
counterexample: Consider a case in which, at the moment of a person's death, God creates a perfect duplicate of that person — possessing all of the original's memories, character traits, and dispositions, and fully conscious and capable of experience. The duplicate satisfies all three conditions. Yet one should hesitate to say that the original person has survived death, for the duplicate, however perfect, is a different individual — a copy, not a continuation. The original person perished; a replica came into being. Survival, it appears, requires more than the preservation of psychological characteristics.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the post-mortem entity stand in a relation of genuine continuity — whether physical, psychological, or metaphysical — with the ante-mortem person, rather than merely replicating the person's psychological profile.
passage: One might define survival after death as the existence, after bodily death, of an entity that possesses the person's memories, character traits, and dispositions, and that is conscious and capable of experience. This definition captures the psychological criteria that many philosophers have regarded as central to personal identity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Suppose that at the moment of a man's death, a divine being creates, in another realm, a perfect duplicate of the man — an entity that possesses all of his memories, all of his character traits and dispositions, and that is fully conscious and capable of experience. This duplicate remembers the man's childhood, shares his loves and fears, and would, in all psychological respects, be indistinguishable from the original. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the original man has not survived; he has perished, and a copy has come into being. The duplicate is a new individual who happens to share the psychological profile of the deceased. Survival, it appears, requires not merely the replication of psychological characteristics but some form of genuine continuity between the ante-mortem and post-mortem individual. The definition, by attending only to the presence of memories, traits, and consciousness, cannot distinguish survival from duplication.

definition: An argument A constitutes a sound demonstration of the existence of God if and only if (i) the premises of A are self-evidently true or established by rigorous proof, (ii) the conclusion that God exists follows deductively from the premises, and (iii) no premise of A presupposes the very conclusion it is meant to establish.
conditions: The premises of A are self-evidently true or established by rigorous proof
The conclusion that God exists follows deductively from the premises
No premise of A presupposes the very conclusion it is meant to establish
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument, which begins from the premise that God is by definition the greatest conceivable being and proceeds to argue that existence in reality is greater than existence in the understanding alone, concluding that God must exist. The conclusion appears to follow deductively, and no single premise overtly presupposes the conclusion. Yet one might observe that the initial premise — that God is the greatest conceivable being — smuggles existence into the definition itself, so that the argument's apparently innocent starting point already contains its conclusion in disguised form.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises not implicitly encode the conclusion through definitional stipulation, so that arguments which derive existence from a definition tailor-made to guarantee it may satisfy all three conditions.
passage: One might propose that a sound demonstration of the existence of God must proceed from self-evident or rigorously established premises to the conclusion that God exists by valid deduction, without any premise presupposing that very conclusion. This characterisation is intended to guard against circular reasoning and to ensure that the demonstration rests upon a secure foundation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of these requirements may be found in the ontological argument. The argument begins with the premise that God is, by definition, the greatest conceivable being. It then maintains that a being which exists in reality is greater than one which exists in the understanding alone. From these premises it concludes that God must exist in reality. The deduction appears valid, and no single premise overtly states that God exists. Yet one might observe that the initial definition — God as the greatest conceivable being — is so constructed as to make the conclusion virtually inescapable. Existence is, as it were, built into the very concept from which the argument departs. It appears that the definition of a sound demonstration, by prohibiting only the overt presupposition of the conclusion, cannot exclude arguments in which the conclusion is covertly encoded within an apparently innocent definitional premise.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by the direct intervention of a divine agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is brought about by the direct intervention of a divine agent
E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person suffering from an incurable illness experiences a sudden and complete recovery following a period of intense prayer. The recovery serves a discernible religious purpose — it strengthens the faith of the community — and the faithful attribute it to divine intervention. Yet medical science, while unable to explain the recovery at present, does not regard it as a violation of any law of nature; rather, it is classified as a spontaneous remission, an event that is extremely rare but not physically impossible.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate putative miracles that do not violate any law of nature but consist in the extraordinary timing or conjunction of natural events, so that events widely experienced as miraculous may fail to qualify.
passage: The classical definition of a miracle, associated with Hume, holds that a miracle is a violation of a law of nature brought about by divine intervention and serving a discernible religious or moral purpose. This definition has the virtue of precision: it distinguishes miracles from merely unusual events and ties them to the action of a divine agent. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be readily constructed. Suppose a person suffering from an illness that medical science regards as incurable experiences a sudden and complete recovery following a period of intense communal prayer. The faithful regard the recovery as the work of God, and it manifestly serves a religious purpose — it strengthens the faith of the community and is commemorated in their devotional life. Yet medical science does not regard the recovery as a violation of any natural law. It is classified as a spontaneous remission — an event that is extremely rare but not physically impossible, not in conflict with any known law. One might observe that the definition, by requiring a violation of natural law, excludes events that are widely and sincerely experienced as miraculous. It appears that many of the events that function as miracles in the religious life of communities are remarkable not because they violate natural laws but because of their extraordinary timing or their conjunction with prayer and faith.

definition: The existence of suffering in the world is logically incompatible with the existence of God if and only if (i) God is defined as omnipotent, (ii) God is defined as perfectly benevolent, and (iii) an omnipotent and perfectly benevolent being would eliminate all suffering.
conditions: God is defined as omnipotent
God is defined as perfectly benevolent
An omnipotent and perfectly benevolent being would eliminate all suffering
counterexample: Consider a parent who has the power to prevent all discomfort in a child's life but who deliberately permits the child to experience the frustration of learning to walk, the pain of a scraped knee, and the disappointment of failure, because these experiences are necessary for the child's development into a mature and capable person. The parent is both powerful and benevolent, yet does not eliminate all suffering. By analogy, an omnipotent and perfectly benevolent being might permit suffering that serves a developmental or moral purpose.
missing_condition: The third condition is too strong: it assumes that a perfectly benevolent being would eliminate all suffering without exception, failing to consider the possibility that some suffering may be a necessary condition for the realisation of greater goods that a benevolent being would wish to secure.
passage: It has been argued that the existence of suffering in the world is logically incompatible with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly benevolent God, on the ground that such a being would eliminate all suffering. The argument has considerable force, for it appears to derive a contradiction from the conjunction of divine attributes and the manifest reality of pain. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the third condition may be constructed by analogy with human benevolence. Consider a parent who possesses the power to shield a child from every form of discomfort — the frustration of learning to walk, the pain of a scraped knee, the sting of failure. Such a parent might nonetheless choose to permit these experiences, judging that they are necessary for the child's development into a capable and resilient person. The parent is both powerful and benevolent, yet does not eliminate all suffering. One might observe that the third condition — that an omnipotent and perfectly benevolent being would eliminate all suffering — is too strong. It assumes that every instance of suffering is pointless, but it appears conceivable that some suffering serves as a necessary condition for the realisation of greater goods, such as moral growth, compassion, or the development of character. The definition, by treating the elimination of all suffering as a requirement of perfect benevolence, forecloses this possibility.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition p if and only if (i) S assents to p, (ii) S lacks conclusive evidence for p, and (iii) S's assent to p is not based on rational argument.
conditions: S assents to p
S lacks conclusive evidence for p
S's assent to p is not based on rational argument
counterexample: Consider a mediaeval theologian who assents to the proposition that God exists on the basis of an elaborate rational argument — the cosmological argument from contingency, say — while fully acknowledging that the argument falls short of conclusive demonstration. All three conditions appear to be met: the theologian assents, lacks conclusive evidence, and yet it seems wrong to say that the assent is not based on rational argument, for the argument is precisely what motivates the theologian's assent. The case reveals an ambiguity: the theologian has faith, employs rational argument, and yet lacks conclusive evidence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that faith may be partly or wholly sustained by rational argument even when the argument does not amount to conclusive proof, so that reason-based faith is excluded from the category.
passage: A familiar account of faith holds that to have faith in a proposition is to assent to it in the absence of conclusive evidence and without the support of rational argument. This account captures a certain picture of faith as a leap beyond the reach of reason. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the tradition of natural theology. Consider a mediaeval theologian — one in the tradition of Anselm or Aquinas — who assents to the proposition that God exists on the basis of an elaborate rational argument from contingency. The theologian concedes that the argument does not amount to conclusive demonstration; reasonable minds may differ about the soundness of its premises. Yet the argument is what motivates and sustains the theologian's assent. One might observe that this theologian has faith — the assent goes beyond what conclusive evidence warrants — and yet the assent is based upon rational argument. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the assent not be based on rational argument, excludes a long and distinguished tradition of reason-based faith. The definition captures only fideistic faith and cannot accommodate the possibility that rational argument may support, without conclusively establishing, the propositions to which the faithful assent.

definition: An experience E is a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a divine or transcendent being, (ii) E produces lasting positive transformation in the character or conduct of the person who undergoes it, and (iii) E is accompanied by a feeling of absolute certainty regarding the reality of the being encountered.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a divine or transcendent being
E produces lasting positive transformation in the character or conduct of the person who undergoes it
E is accompanied by a feeling of absolute certainty regarding the reality of the being encountered
counterexample: Consider the case of a contemplative nun who, over many years of disciplined prayer, experiences a profound sense of the presence of the divine. This experience transforms her character — she becomes notably more compassionate and self-sacrificing. Yet she reports no feeling of absolute certainty; rather, she describes her experience as suffused with what the mystical tradition calls 'the dark night of the soul,' a persistent doubt that coexists with the sense of presence. Her experience satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that genuine religious experience may be accompanied by doubt, uncertainty, or a sense of mystery rather than absolute certainty, so that the experiences of many serious contemplatives are excluded.
passage: One might define a genuine religious experience as one that involves a vivid sense of the presence of a divine or transcendent being, produces lasting positive transformation in character, and is accompanied by a feeling of absolute certainty regarding the reality of the being encountered. This account draws upon the classical descriptions of religious experience found in the writings of James and Otto. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the contemplative traditions of the Christian mystics. Consider a nun who, through many years of disciplined prayer, experiences a profound and recurrent sense of the divine presence. The experience transforms her character in lasting ways — she becomes markedly more compassionate, more patient, and more devoted to the service of others. Yet she reports no feeling of absolute certainty. On the contrary, she describes her experience as pervaded by what the mystical tradition calls 'the dark night of the soul' — a persistent doubt and sense of absence that coexists with the sense of presence. One might observe that this contemplative's experience is, by any reasonable standard, a genuine religious experience. It appears that the definition, by requiring absolute certainty, excludes the experiences of many of the most serious and devoted practitioners of the religious life. The mystical traditions of Christianity, Islam, and Hinduism all contain accounts of encounters with the divine that are characterised not by certainty but by a profound ambiguity.

definition: A communication C constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) C originates from God, (ii) C conveys propositional content that was previously unavailable to the human recipient, and (iii) C is accompanied by a sign or miracle that authenticates its divine origin.
conditions: C originates from God
C conveys propositional content that was previously unavailable to the human recipient
C is accompanied by a sign or miracle that authenticates its divine origin
counterexample: Consider the experience of a prophet who receives a divine communication not in the form of propositional content but as an overwhelming vision — a vision of the holiness of God, say — that does not convey any specific new information but transforms the prophet's understanding of what was already implicitly present in the received tradition. The experience is accompanied by no sign or miracle. Yet the prophetic tradition has always regarded such experiences as among the most important forms of divine revelation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate non-propositional forms of revelation — such as visions, encounters, or transformative experiences — that do not convey new propositional content and may not be accompanied by authenticating signs.
passage: A traditional account of divine revelation holds that it consists in a communication originating from God, conveying previously unavailable propositional content, and authenticated by an accompanying sign or miracle. This account has the merit of providing clear criteria by which purported revelations might be assessed. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of these criteria may be drawn from the prophetic tradition. Consider a prophet who, in the course of worship, is overwhelmed by a vision of the holiness of God. The vision does not convey any specific new proposition; it does not inform the prophet of any fact previously unavailable. Rather, it transforms the prophet's understanding of what was already implicitly present in the received tradition — a deepening of apprehension rather than an addition of information. Moreover, the experience is accompanied by no miracle or sign; the prophet is simply overwhelmed in the silence of prayer. Yet the prophetic traditions of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam have consistently regarded such experiences as among the most profound forms of divine revelation. One might observe that the definition, by requiring propositional content and authenticating signs, excludes the very experiences that the traditions themselves regard as most central to the revelatory process.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal or mental address directed to God, (ii) A expresses a petition or request, and (iii) the person performing A believes that God is capable of granting the petition.
conditions: A is a verbal or mental address directed to God
A expresses a petition or request
The person performing A believes that God is capable of granting the petition
counterexample: Consider a monk who spends hours each day in contemplative silence, directing his attention toward God without formulating any petition or request. His practice consists not in asking for anything but in a disciplined attentiveness to the divine presence — what the contemplative tradition calls 'the prayer of simple regard.' He addresses God, and he believes in God's power, but he expresses no petition. By the definition, this does not constitute genuine prayer, though it is widely regarded as one of the highest forms of the practice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate non-petitionary forms of prayer — such as contemplation, adoration, and thanksgiving — that do not express any request but are nonetheless central to the devotional life.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as a verbal or mental address directed to God that expresses a petition and is made in the belief that God is capable of granting it. This account captures the most familiar form of prayer — the plea for divine assistance — and it accords with the common understanding of prayer as essentially petitionary. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be found in the contemplative traditions. Consider a monk who devotes several hours each day to what the tradition calls 'the prayer of simple regard': a disciplined attentiveness to the divine presence, conducted in silence, without the formulation of any petition or request. The monk addresses God — his attention is directed toward the divine — and he believes in God's power. But he asks for nothing. His practice is one of receptive attentiveness rather than petition. By the definition, this does not count as genuine prayer. Yet the contemplative traditions of both Eastern and Western Christianity, and analogous traditions in Sufism and Hindu bhakti, have consistently regarded such practices as among the most exalted forms of prayer. It appears that the definition, by requiring the expression of a petition, is too narrow to encompass the full range of what the religious traditions recognise as prayer.

definition: An action X is morally obligatory if and only if (i) God commands X, (ii) God's command is communicated to human beings through a recognised channel of revelation, and (iii) the command is consistent with God's nature as perfectly good.
conditions: God commands X
God's command is communicated to human beings through a recognised channel of revelation
The command is consistent with God's nature as perfectly good
counterexample: Consider the case of Abraham, who, according to the scriptural narrative, is commanded by God to sacrifice his son Isaac. The command is communicated through a recognised channel — direct divine address — and if God is perfectly good, then the command must be consistent with His nature. Yet the command appears to require an action — the killing of an innocent child — that is, by any ordinary moral standard, deeply wrong. The conditions of the definition are formally satisfied, yet one might observe that the conclusion — that killing Isaac is morally obligatory — strikes us as monstrous.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide any independent criterion by which the goodness of a divine command can be assessed, so that apparently abhorrent commands must be accepted as morally obligatory simply because they issue from a being stipulated to be good.
passage: The divine command theory holds, in one of its forms, that an action is morally obligatory if and only if God commands it, the command is communicated through a recognised channel of revelation, and the command is consistent with God's perfectly good nature. This account has the virtue of grounding moral obligation in the will of an authoritative and good being. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition may be drawn from the Abrahamic tradition. According to the scriptural narrative, Abraham is commanded by God to sacrifice his son Isaac. The command is communicated through direct divine address — a recognised channel if ever there was one. And if God is by nature perfectly good, then the command is, by stipulation, consistent with that nature. All three conditions are formally met. Yet one might observe that the action commanded — the killing of an innocent child — is, by any ordinary moral reckoning, deeply wrong. We should hesitate to say that the killing of Isaac is morally obligatory merely because it is divinely commanded. It appears that the definition, by making consistency with God's good nature a condition but providing no independent standard by which that goodness can be assessed, leaves us without resources to evaluate apparently abhorrent commands. The goodness of God's nature is invoked to guarantee the goodness of the command, but the goodness of the command is the very thing in question.

definition: A person S survives bodily death if and only if (i) there exists after S's bodily death an entity that possesses S's memories, (ii) that entity possesses S's character traits and personality, and (iii) that entity is conscious.
conditions: There exists after S's bodily death an entity that possesses S's memories
That entity possesses S's character traits and personality
That entity is conscious
counterexample: Consider the possibility that, at the moment of a person's death, God creates a perfect replica of that person — a being with all of the original's memories, character traits, and personality, and which is fully conscious. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the replica is a numerically distinct entity from the original person — it was created at the moment of death and is not identical with the person who died. It is a copy, not a continuation. We should hesitate to say that the original person has survived death merely because a perfect duplicate has been produced.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the post-mortem entity be numerically identical with the ante-mortem person rather than merely a qualitative duplicate, so that the creation of a perfect copy would count as survival.
passage: A natural account of survival after bodily death holds that a person survives if there exists, after the body's demise, a conscious entity possessing the person's memories, character traits, and personality. This account draws upon the psychological criteria of personal identity that have been much discussed in modern philosophy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. Suppose that, at the moment of a person's death, God — or, if one prefers, some sufficiently powerful being — creates a perfect replica: a new entity endowed with all of the deceased's memories, character traits, and personality, and which is fully conscious. All three conditions of the definition are met. The replica remembers the deceased's childhood, shares the deceased's temperament, and is aware of its own existence. Yet one might observe that this entity is not the original person but a copy. It came into existence at the moment of the original's death and is numerically distinct from the person who lived and died. It appears that we should hesitate to say that the original person has survived. The definition, by requiring only the preservation of psychological attributes, cannot distinguish genuine survival from the mere production of a qualitative duplicate.

definition: An object or place O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use by a community, (ii) O is the focus of rituals, ceremonies, or devotional practices, and (iii) the community regards O with a distinctive attitude of reverence.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use by a community
O is the focus of rituals, ceremonies, or devotional practices
The community regards O with a distinctive attitude of reverence
counterexample: Consider the grave of an unknown soldier in a national war memorial. It is set apart from ordinary use by the community, it is the focus of elaborate ceremonies and rituals of remembrance, and the community regards it with an attitude of deep reverence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the grave of the unknown soldier is a civic rather than a religious site, and the reverence it inspires is patriotic rather than religious in character. It is not sacred in the sense that a church or a temple is sacred.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the setting-apart, the rituals, and the reverence be directed toward or connected with a divine or transcendent reality, so that purely civic objects of communal reverence may qualify as sacred.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use by a community, made the focus of ritual and ceremony, and regarded with a distinctive attitude of reverence. This definition draws upon the influential analyses of Durkheim and Otto and captures much of what we associate with sacred objects and places — temples, holy books, consecrated ground. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the civic sphere. Consider the grave of an unknown soldier in a national war memorial. The grave is set apart from ordinary use; no one may walk upon it or use its vicinity for mundane purposes. It is the focus of elaborate ceremonies — wreath-laying, military salutes, minutes of silence — performed annually on days of national remembrance. And the community regards it with a distinctive attitude of reverence that closely resembles the attitude directed toward religious objects. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the grave of the unknown soldier is a civic monument, not a sacred site in the religious sense. The reverence it inspires is patriotic, not directed toward the divine. It appears that the definition, by requiring only communal setting-apart, ritual focus, and reverence, cannot distinguish the religiously sacred from the civically venerated.

definition: An argument A constitutes a successful proof of the existence of a supreme being if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A's conclusion is that a supreme being exists.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true
A employs only valid deductive inferences
A's conclusion is that a supreme being exists
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument in its classical form. It proceeds from the premise that we possess the concept of a being than which nothing greater can be conceived — a premise many would regard as self-evident, since we do indeed possess such a concept. It employs valid deductive inferences. And its conclusion is that such a being exists. All three conditions appear met. Yet Kant famously objected that existence is not a predicate — that the concept of a hundred real thalers contains not a penny more than the concept of a hundred possible thalers — and that the argument therefore smuggles existence into the concept rather than establishing it by legitimate deduction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises of A do not tacitly presuppose the truth of the conclusion, so that an argument which is circular — defining the subject in a way that already entails its existence — may satisfy all three conditions.
passage: One might propose that a successful proof of the existence of a supreme being is an argument proceeding from self-evidently true premises, employing only valid deductive inferences, and concluding that a supreme being exists. This characterisation captures the formal desiderata of a deductive proof. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the ontological argument. In its classical form, the argument begins with the premise that we possess the concept of a being than which nothing greater can be conceived. This premise has been regarded by many philosophers as self-evident, for we plainly do possess such a concept. The argument proceeds by valid deductive inferences to the conclusion that such a being must exist, for a being that existed would be greater than one that did not, and a being than which nothing greater can be conceived must therefore exist. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet as Kant argued, the conclusion is already smuggled into the premises. The premise defines the subject in a way that makes non-existence a contradiction, but this is a feature of the definition, not of the world. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only self-evidence, validity, and the appropriate conclusion, cannot distinguish a genuinely probative argument from one that achieves its conclusion by definitional fiat. A more adequate account would require that the premises be independent of the conclusion they are intended to establish.

definition: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
conditions: T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil
The reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence
T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world
counterexample: Consider the free-will theodicy, which holds that a benevolent deity permits evil because the possession of genuine free will requires the possibility of its misuse, and a world with free agents is more valuable than one without. This provides a morally sufficient reason consistent with omnipotence and benevolence. Yet it does not obviously account for natural evils — earthquakes, plagues, childhood cancers — which are not the result of any agent's free choice. The theodicy addresses moral evil but leaves natural evil unexplained.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T account for every category of evil — both moral and natural — so that a theodicy which addresses one source of evil while leaving another entirely unexplained may count as successful.
passage: A natural account of a successful theodicy holds that it provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, that this reason is consistent with omnipotence and benevolence, and that it accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil in the world. This characterisation captures the logical and moral demands that any reconciliation must satisfy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises with the free-will theodicy. This theodicy maintains that a benevolent deity permits evil because genuine free will — which is a great good — requires the possibility of its misuse, and a world containing free agents is more valuable than a world of mere automata. The reason provided is morally substantial and consistent with the attributes of the deity. But one might observe that the free-will theodicy addresses only moral evil — evil that results from the deliberate choices of agents. It does not account for natural evil: earthquakes that destroy cities, plagues that decimate populations, cancers that afflict children who have exercised no choice whatever. The quantity and distribution of natural evil remain entirely unaddressed. The definition, by requiring that the theodicy account for the observed evil without specifying that it must address every category of evil, permits a theodicy that explains one class of evil while leaving another wholly mysterious.

definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by the direct intervention of a supernatural agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is brought about by the direct intervention of a supernatural agent
E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Consider the spontaneous remission of a terminal illness in a patient who has received no medical treatment and has not engaged in prayer or any religious practice. The remission appears to violate the natural course of the disease. If one supposes, for the sake of argument, that it was brought about by supernatural intervention, and that it serves the purpose of preserving a life, all three conditions are met. Yet many theologians would hesitate to classify this as a miracle, for the event bears no relation to any religious context — no prayer was offered, no faith was exercised, no religious community witnessed the event. The absence of religious context renders the classification arbitrary.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that E occur within a recognisable religious context — in response to prayer, within a community of faith, or in connection with a specific religious narrative — so that any unexplained beneficial event may be classified as miraculous.
passage: One might define a miracle as an event that violates a law of nature, is brought about by the direct intervention of a supernatural agent, and serves a discernible religious or moral purpose. This characterisation captures the traditional theological understanding of miracles. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers unexplained medical recoveries. Suppose a patient with a terminal illness experiences complete and spontaneous remission without medical treatment. The remission appears to violate the natural course of the disease. If one supposes — for the sake of the philosophical exercise — that the remission was brought about by supernatural intervention, and that the preservation of a human life serves a moral purpose, then all three conditions are met. Yet the patient was not a person of any religious conviction, had offered no prayer, and the remission occurred in the absence of any religious context whatsoever. Many theologians would hesitate to classify this event as a miracle, for the concept of miracle, as historically understood, is bound up with the life of religious communities — with prayer, with prophetic activity, with the narrative of divine-human encounter. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to natural law, supernatural agency, and moral purpose, detaches the concept of miracle from its religious context and permits the classification of any beneficial anomaly as miraculous.

definition: A person S possesses genuine faith in a proposition P if and only if (i) S accepts P as true, (ii) S lacks conclusive evidence for the truth of P, and (iii) S's acceptance of P is not shaken by the absence of such evidence.
conditions: S accepts P as true
S lacks conclusive evidence for the truth of P
S's acceptance of P is not shaken by the absence of such evidence
counterexample: Consider a committed conspiracy theorist who accepts as true the proposition that the moon landings were staged, lacks conclusive evidence for this claim, and whose acceptance is not shaken by the absence of evidence — indeed, the absence of evidence is taken as confirmation of the conspiracy's effectiveness. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that the conspiracy theorist possesses genuine faith in any religiously or philosophically significant sense. The acceptance is born of suspicion and paranoia rather than trust or commitment.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's acceptance of P involve an element of trust, commitment, or existential orientation toward the object of faith, so that mere stubborn adherence to an unsupported claim may count as genuine faith.
passage: One might characterise genuine faith as the acceptance of a proposition as true in the absence of conclusive evidence, where the acceptance is not shaken by this absence. This characterisation captures a feature often attributed to faith: its independence from demonstrative proof. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed. Consider a person who is firmly convinced that the moon landings of the 1960s were staged by a government agency. This person accepts the proposition as true. They lack conclusive evidence for it — indeed, the overwhelming evidence points the other way. And their acceptance is entirely unshaken by the absence of supporting evidence; they regard this very absence as proof of the conspiracy's sophistication. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to describe this person as possessing genuine faith. The conspiracy theorist's acceptance is born of suspicion, hostility, and a disposition to distrust, whereas faith — in its religious and philosophical employment — typically involves an element of trust, personal commitment, and existential orientation toward the object of belief. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the acceptance of an unsupported proposition and the resilience of that acceptance, cannot distinguish faith from mere credulity or paranoid conviction.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal or mental address directed to a deity or transcendent being, (ii) A expresses a request, a thanksgiving, or an act of worship, and (iii) A is performed with the sincere intention of communicating with the being so addressed.
conditions: A is a verbal or mental address directed to a deity or transcendent being
A expresses a request, a thanksgiving, or an act of worship
A is performed with the sincere intention of communicating with the being so addressed
counterexample: Consider a Theravada Buddhist monk who practises silent meditation directed not to any deity but toward the attainment of insight into the nature of suffering. The monk's practice is not addressed to any transcendent being, yet within the Theravada tradition, such meditation is regarded as a form of devotional practice closely allied to prayer. The monk's practice fails condition (i), and the definition excludes it from the category of genuine prayer, even though it functions within its tradition in ways that are functionally analogous to prayer.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate non-theistic forms of devotional or contemplative practice that function within their religious traditions as prayer functions within theistic ones, so that the contemplative disciplines of non-theistic traditions are excluded by definitional fiat.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as a verbal or mental address directed to a deity or transcendent being, expressing a request, thanksgiving, or worship, and performed with the sincere intention of communication. This characterisation captures the essential features of prayer as understood within the theistic traditions of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the context of non-theistic religious practice. Consider a Theravada Buddhist monk engaged in silent meditation. The monk's practice is not directed to any deity or transcendent being — indeed, classical Theravada Buddhism does not posit such a being. The meditation does not express a request or thanksgiving, and it is not performed with the intention of communicating with a supernatural addressee. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet within the Theravada tradition, such meditation occupies a position functionally analogous to prayer in the theistic traditions: it is the central devotional practice, it structures the monk's spiritual life, and it is regarded as the primary means of spiritual progress. One might observe that the definition, by requiring an address to a transcendent being, is implicitly theistic and cannot accommodate the contemplative practices of traditions in which no such being is posited. The definition mistakes a feature of theistic prayer for an essential feature of prayer as such.

definition: An object or place O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use by a community, (ii) O is associated with the presence or activity of a deity, and (iii) O is treated with special reverence by the members of the community.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use by a community
O is associated with the presence or activity of a deity
O is treated with special reverence by the members of the community
counterexample: Consider the tomb of Lenin in Red Square, Moscow, during the Soviet period. The tomb was set apart from ordinary use — it served as a site of pilgrimage and state ceremony. It was treated with extraordinary reverence — visitors were required to remove their hats, maintain silence, and observe strict decorum. Yet it was not associated with the presence or activity of any deity, for the Soviet state was officially atheist. The tomb functioned as a sacred site in every sociological sense, yet the definition excludes it because condition (ii) is not met.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for secular or non-theistic forms of the sacred — sites, objects, or symbols that are set apart and treated with reverence in virtue of their association with foundational political, national, or ideological narratives rather than with any deity.
passage: A standard characterisation of the sacred holds that an object or place is sacred if it is set apart from ordinary use, associated with the presence or activity of a deity, and treated with special reverence. This characterisation captures the notion of the sacred as it operates within theistic traditions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the domain of political life. Consider the tomb of Lenin in Moscow's Red Square during the Soviet period. The tomb was emphatically set apart from ordinary use — it functioned as a site of state pilgrimage, political ceremony, and ideological devotion. Visitors were required to remove their hats, maintain absolute silence, and observe an elaborate protocol of decorum. The tomb was, in every phenomenological sense, a sacred site. Yet the Soviet state was officially and vehemently atheist, and the tomb was associated with no deity whatever. It was sacred — if the word may be employed — in virtue of its association with the founding narrative of the Soviet state and the quasi-mythological status of Lenin within that narrative. One might observe that the definition, by requiring association with a deity, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of secular sacredness — the setting apart and reverence of objects and places that derive their special status from political, national, or ideological narratives rather than from any transcendent being.

definition: A communication R constitutes a genuine divine revelation if and only if (i) R originates from a deity, (ii) R conveys truths that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason, and (iii) R is received by a human intermediary who transmits it to a wider community.
conditions: R originates from a deity
R conveys truths that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason
R is received by a human intermediary who transmits it to a wider community
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the universe had a temporal beginning. Some theologians have claimed this as a revealed truth, yet modern cosmology has independently arrived at the same conclusion through empirical investigation and mathematical reasoning. If this truth can be discovered by unaided human reason — and it appears that it can — then condition (ii) is not satisfied, and the communication of this truth would not count as genuine revelation even if it did originate from a deity. The definition would have the paradoxical consequence that a truth ceases to be revealed once human reason catches up with it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a genuine revelation may convey truths which, though discoverable in principle by human reason, were in fact first communicated by divine agency and received their authority from that origin, so that the advance of human inquiry can retrospectively strip a communication of its revelatory status.
passage: One might characterise genuine divine revelation as a communication originating from a deity, conveying truths undiscoverable by unaided human reason, and received by a human intermediary for transmission to a wider community. This characterisation captures the structure of revelation as understood in many theistic traditions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers truths that were once thought to require revelation but have since been established by independent inquiry. Consider the proposition that the universe had a temporal beginning. In certain theological traditions, this proposition was held to be a revealed truth — one that human reason could not establish on its own. Yet modern cosmology, through the general theory of relativity and the observation of the cosmic microwave background radiation, has arrived at the same conclusion by entirely natural means. If this truth can be discovered by unaided human reason, then condition (ii) is not satisfied, and the original communication — even if it did originate from a deity — would no longer count as genuine revelation. One might observe that the definition has the paradoxical consequence that the advance of human understanding can retroactively strip a communication of its revelatory status. The definition, by tying revelation to the cognitive limitations of a particular epoch, makes the concept hostage to the progress of science.

definition: An action X is morally obligatory if and only if (i) X is commanded by a deity, (ii) the deity's command is communicated to moral agents through scripture or prophetic revelation, and (iii) the deity possesses perfect moral goodness.
conditions: X is commanded by a deity
The deity's command is communicated to moral agents through scripture or prophetic revelation
The deity possesses perfect moral goodness
counterexample: Consider the biblical command to Abraham to sacrifice his son Isaac. The command originates from the deity. It is communicated through direct prophetic revelation. And the deity is held to possess perfect moral goodness. All three conditions are met. Yet the action commanded — the killing of an innocent child — appears to be morally monstrous by any standard of moral reasoning independent of the command itself. If the command makes the action obligatory, then the theory has the consequence that an apparently abhorrent act becomes morally required solely in virtue of being commanded.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the commanded action be independently recognisable as morally permissible or good, so that a command to perform an action that would otherwise be morally abhorrent becomes obligatory merely because it is divinely commanded.
passage: A classical theory of moral obligation holds that an action is morally obligatory if and only if it is commanded by a deity of perfect moral goodness, and the command is communicated through scripture or prophetic revelation. This characterisation anchors moral obligation in divine authority. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the Genesis narrative. According to the text, the deity commands Abraham to take his son Isaac to the land of Moriah and offer him there as a burnt offering. The command originates from the deity. It is communicated through direct prophetic revelation. And the deity is, by hypothesis, perfectly good. All three conditions are met, and the action is therefore morally obligatory on this account. Yet the action commanded is the killing of an innocent child. One might observe that this consequence is deeply troubling. If the theory entails that the slaughter of an innocent child becomes morally required whenever a perfectly good deity commands it, then the theory either renders moral obligation wholly opaque — we can never anticipate what might be required of us — or it empties the concept of divine goodness of independent content, for the deity's goodness consists in nothing more than the deity's being the source of commands. The definition, by making obligation consist entirely in the fact of divine command, cannot secure the moral intelligibility that obligation seems to require.

definition: A person S survives death if and only if (i) there exists, after S's bodily death, an entity E that is conscious, (ii) E possesses the memories and personality traits that characterised S during life, and (iii) E is numerically identical with S.
conditions: There exists, after S's bodily death, an entity E that is conscious
E possesses the memories and personality traits that characterised S during life
E is numerically identical with S
counterexample: Consider a case in which, at the moment of a person's death, a deity creates a perfect replica of that person — an entity possessing the same memories, personality traits, dispositions, and physical constitution. The replica is conscious. It possesses all of S's memories and traits. Yet is it numerically identical with S? It appears to be a distinct entity — a copy rather than a continuation. The definition requires numerical identity but provides no criterion for establishing it, and in this case the most natural verdict is that the replica, however perfect, is not S but a duplicate.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify a criterion for numerical identity across the gap of bodily death, so that it cannot distinguish genuine survival from the creation of a perfect replica, leaving the most fundamental question — what makes the post-mortem entity the same person — entirely unanswered.
passage: One might characterise survival of death as the post-mortem existence of a conscious entity possessing the memories and personality traits of the deceased person and numerically identical with that person. This characterisation captures the intuitions that survival requires continuity of consciousness, psychological similarity, and genuine identity rather than mere resemblance. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the possibility of divine replication. Suppose that at the moment of a person's bodily death, a deity creates a perfect replica — an entity possessing the same memories, the same personality traits, the same dispositions, and even the same physical constitution as the deceased. The replica is conscious. It possesses all of the relevant memories and traits. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But is the replica numerically identical with the deceased? It appears that it is not. The replica was created at the moment of death; it is a new entity that happens to resemble the deceased in every respect. It is a copy, not a continuation. One might observe that the definition, by requiring numerical identity without providing any criterion for establishing it across the gap of bodily death, leaves the most fundamental question unanswered. The definition tells us that survival requires identity, but it does not tell us what identity consists in when the body has perished and a new entity — however similar — has come into existence.

definition: An experience E constitutes a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a transcendent reality, (ii) E produces in the subject a lasting transformation of character or outlook, and (iii) the subject sincerely reports that E was qualitatively unlike any ordinary perceptual experience.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a transcendent reality
E produces in the subject a lasting transformation of character or outlook
The subject sincerely reports that E was qualitatively unlike any ordinary perceptual experience
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who, upon reaching a summit after an arduous climb, experiences a vivid sense of the presence of something vast and transcendent — what Rudolf Otto might call the numinous. The experience produces a lasting transformation of character: the mountaineer becomes more contemplative, more attentive to the natural world, more attuned to what they describe as the sacred. And the mountaineer sincerely reports that the experience was qualitatively unlike any ordinary perception. All three conditions are met. Yet the experience was induced by physical exhaustion, altitude, and the grandeur of the natural landscape rather than by any encounter with a transcendent reality. Many would hesitate to classify it as a genuine religious experience in the theologically significant sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require any independent criterion for distinguishing experiences that are genuinely caused by the presence of a transcendent reality from those that are induced by natural psychological or physiological factors, so that altitude-induced euphoria and genuine mystical encounter are rendered indistinguishable.
passage: One might define a genuine religious experience as one involving a vivid sense of the presence of a transcendent reality, producing a lasting transformation of character, and sincerely reported by the subject as qualitatively unlike ordinary perception. This characterisation captures features commonly attributed to mystical and religious experiences. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the domain of extreme physical experience. Consider a mountaineer who, upon reaching a summit after hours of strenuous climbing at high altitude, is overwhelmed by a vivid sense of the presence of something vast, mysterious, and transcendent. The experience is transformative: the mountaineer's character is lastingly altered — they become more reflective, more sensitive to the grandeur of nature, more inclined to speak of the sacred. The experience is sincerely reported as qualitatively unlike any ordinary perception. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the experience is readily explicable in terms of physical exhaustion, hypoxia, and the overwhelming sensory impact of an alpine panorama. No transcendent reality need be invoked. One might observe that the definition, by relying on the subject's phenomenology — the sense of transcendent presence, the transformative effect, the qualitative uniqueness — cannot distinguish experiences that are veridical perceptions of a transcendent reality from those that are natural psychological responses to extreme physical or environmental conditions. The definition attends to how the experience feels but not to what causes it.

definition: An argument A constitutes a sound proof of the existence of a divine being if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true or universally accepted, (ii) A's logical form is valid, and (iii) the conclusion asserts the existence of a being possessing the traditional divine attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true or universally accepted
A's logical form is valid
The conclusion asserts the existence of a being possessing omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness
counterexample: Consider the ontological argument in Anselm's formulation. Its premise — that a being than which none greater can be conceived must exist in reality as well as in the understanding, for otherwise a greater being could be conceived — purports to be self-evidently true. The logical form is valid. And the conclusion asserts the existence of a being with traditional divine attributes. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the same argumentative structure could be used to prove the existence of a perfect island — an island than which none greater can be conceived must exist, by parity of reasoning. The argument proves too much; its form generates absurd conclusions when applied to other subject matters.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the argumentative structure not generate absurd conclusions when applied to subject matters other than the divine, so that a logically valid argument from apparently self-evident premises may prove too much by establishing the existence of entities that manifestly do not exist.
passage: One might define a sound proof of the existence of a divine being as an argument from self-evident or universally accepted premises, in valid logical form, concluding with the existence of a being possessing omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness. This definition captures the logical and theological features such a proof would need. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed by attending to the ontological argument. In Anselm's formulation, the premise appears self-evident: a being than which nothing greater can be conceived must exist in reality, for if it existed only in the understanding, a greater being — one existing in reality as well — could be conceived. The logical form is valid. The conclusion asserts the existence of a maximally great being. All three conditions are met. Yet, as Gaunilo observed in the eleventh century, the same form of argument could establish the existence of a perfect island — an island than which no greater island can be conceived. If the argument works for a divine being, it works equally for islands, mountains, and any other kind of thing that admits of degrees of perfection. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only self-evident premises, valid form, and the right conclusion, cannot distinguish a proof that establishes something genuine from one whose form generates an infinity of absurd existential claims.

definition: A person S has genuine faith in a proposition P if and only if (i) S believes that P is true, (ii) S's belief in P is not based on conclusive evidence, and (iii) S is willing to act on the basis of P despite the absence of such evidence.
conditions: S believes that P is true
S's belief in P is not based on conclusive evidence
S is willing to act on the basis of P despite the absence of such evidence
counterexample: Consider a gambler who believes, without conclusive evidence, that his next wager will succeed, and who is willing to stake his savings on this belief. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that the gambler has faith in any sense that resembles religious faith. His belief is a matter of wishful optimism, not of the kind of trust and commitment that the concept of faith characteristically involves.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the belief involve a relationship of trust or commitment to that in which faith is placed, so that any belief held without evidence and acted upon — including mere wishful thinking or reckless optimism — may qualify as faith.
passage: One might characterise genuine faith as believing a proposition to be true without conclusive evidence and being willing to act upon it. This definition aims to distinguish faith from ordinary belief by emphasising the absence of evidential compulsion. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from everyday life. Consider a gambler who believes, without any conclusive evidence, that his next wager at the racing track will succeed. He is willing to act on this belief, staking his savings upon it. All three conditions are met: he believes the proposition, the belief is not grounded in conclusive evidence, and he acts upon it. Yet one might observe that describing the gambler's attitude as faith distorts the concept beyond recognition. The gambler's belief is a species of wishful optimism or reckless hope; it lacks the dimension of trust, commitment, and existential seriousness that characterises what we ordinarily mean by faith. The definition, by requiring only unsupported belief and willingness to act, cannot distinguish faith from gambling, from superstition, or from any other case in which someone acts upon a belief they cannot prove.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E violates a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by a supernatural agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose.
conditions: E violates a law of nature
E is brought about by a supernatural agent
E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Suppose a spontaneous quantum fluctuation causes a terminally ill patient to recover instantaneously. The event violates the statistical expectations of medical science — one might argue it violates a law of nature in the relevant sense. Suppose further that the recovery inspires the patient's family to lead more charitable lives, so that it serves a discernible moral purpose. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met. Yet there is no supernatural agent involved; the recovery is the result of an extraordinarily improbable but physically possible quantum event. If we insist on condition (ii), we must deny that this is a miracle. But many religious believers would regard such an event as miraculous precisely because of its effects, regardless of its mechanism.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the mechanism of the event involve a supernatural agent, but fails to recognise that the concept of miracle as commonly employed may depend upon the significance and effects of the event rather than upon the nature of the causal mechanism producing it.
passage: A traditional definition holds that a miracle is an event that violates a law of nature, is brought about by a supernatural agent, and serves a discernible religious or moral purpose. This characterisation captures the theological and nomological features commonly ascribed to miracles. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be constructed. Suppose a patient in the terminal stages of an illness recovers instantaneously. Medical science can offer no explanation; the recovery violates every expectation grounded in natural law. The event inspires the patient's family to transform their lives, devoting themselves to charitable work. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet suppose the cause of the recovery is an extraordinarily improbable quantum fluctuation — a physical event, however fantastically unlikely, that falls within the scope of natural law broadly conceived. There is no supernatural agent. Condition (ii) fails. The definition would classify this event as not miraculous. Yet one might observe that many who witnessed it would regard it as a miracle, and their judgement would rest not upon an analysis of the causal mechanism but upon the extraordinary character and moral significance of the event itself. The definition, by requiring supernatural causation, privileges a particular metaphysical commitment that may not be essential to the concept of miracle as it is actually employed.

definition: The existence of evil constitutes a decisive objection to theism if and only if (i) a perfectly good being would prevent all evil that it could prevent, (ii) an omnipotent being could prevent all evil, and (iii) evil exists.
conditions: A perfectly good being would prevent all evil that it could prevent
An omnipotent being could prevent all evil
Evil exists
counterexample: Consider a parent who allows a child to experience the pain of a minor burn from touching a hot stove. The parent could have prevented the suffering — condition (ii) is met analogously. The suffering exists — condition (iii) holds. Yet the parent is not thereby shown to be less than perfectly good, for the experience teaches the child a lesson that prevents far greater harm. It appears that a perfectly good agent might permit evil when doing so is necessary for a greater good, undermining condition (i).
missing_condition: The definition fails to qualify condition (i) to allow that a perfectly good being might permit evil when its prevention would preclude the realisation of a greater good that could not be achieved by other means.
passage: One might formulate the argument from evil as follows: a perfectly good being would prevent all evil it could prevent; an omnipotent being could prevent all evil; evil exists; therefore no perfectly good and omnipotent being exists. The argument is logically valid, and its third premise is undeniable. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the first premise may be drawn from ordinary moral life. Consider a parent who, seeing a child reach toward a hot stove, deliberately refrains from intervening. The child touches the stove and suffers a minor burn. The parent could have prevented this suffering. The suffering exists. Yet we should hesitate to say that the parent has acted in a manner incompatible with perfect goodness. The experience teaches the child a vital lesson — the danger of heat — that the parent's verbal warnings had failed to convey, and which prevents far graver injuries in future. One might observe that the first premise of the argument, taken without qualification, is too strong. A perfectly good being might permit evil when its prevention would preclude the realisation of a greater good that could not otherwise be attained. The definition, by asserting that a good being would prevent all preventable evil, leaves no room for the possibility that some evils are instrumentally necessary for goods that outweigh them.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by God, (ii) the command is clearly communicated through revelation or scripture, and (iii) the command is consistent with God's nature as perfectly good.
conditions: A is commanded by God
The command is clearly communicated through revelation or scripture
The command is consistent with God's nature as perfectly good
counterexample: Consider the command in the Hebrew scriptures that Abraham sacrifice his son Isaac. The command is issued by God. It is clearly communicated through direct revelation. And if one maintains that all of God's commands are consistent with His perfectly good nature, then condition (iii) is satisfied by definition. All three conditions are met. Yet many morally serious persons would resist the conclusion that the killing of an innocent child is morally obligatory, even when commanded by God. The case suggests that the definition cannot accommodate the possibility that a divine command might conflict with moral requirements that hold independently of any command.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide an independent criterion of moral goodness against which divine commands might be assessed, so that any command attributed to God automatically satisfies the consistency requirement and no divine command — however morally troubling — can be classified as non-obligatory.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory if and only if it is commanded by God, the command is clearly communicated through revelation or scripture, and the command is consistent with God's perfectly good nature. This definition attempts to ground moral obligation in divine authority while safeguarding it against arbitrary divine decrees. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the Hebrew scriptures. God commands Abraham to sacrifice his son Isaac. The command is issued directly by God. It is communicated through unambiguous revelation. And if one holds that all of God's commands are by definition consistent with His perfectly good nature — for God, being perfectly good, could not command anything inconsistent with goodness — then condition (iii) is satisfied automatically. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that many thoughtful persons would resist the conclusion that the killing of an innocent child is morally obligatory. The difficulty is that condition (iii) provides no independent criterion of goodness; it is satisfied whenever (i) is satisfied, rendering it vacuous. The definition, by failing to specify an independent standard against which divine commands might be evaluated, makes it impossible to distinguish a genuinely good command from one that is merely attributed to a good commander.

definition: An experience E qualifies as a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a reality transcending the ordinary empirical world, (ii) E is accompanied by feelings of awe, reverence, or profound peace, and (iii) E produces lasting positive transformation in the subject's character or conduct.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a reality transcending the ordinary empirical world
E is accompanied by feelings of awe, reverence, or profound peace
E produces lasting positive transformation in the subject's character or conduct
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who, upon reaching the summit of a great peak, experiences a vivid sense of the presence of something vast and transcendent, accompanied by intense feelings of awe and profound peace. The experience transforms her character — she becomes more contemplative, more generous, more attentive to the needs of others. All three conditions are met. Yet the mountaineer attributes her experience entirely to the grandeur of the natural landscape and the physiological effects of altitude. She regards it as a purely natural experience, devoid of any religious content. The definition would classify it as a genuine religious experience against her own understanding.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the subject interpret the experience as having religious significance — that the transcendent reality be understood as divine, sacred, or connected to a religious framework — so that experiences of natural sublimity satisfying all three conditions are indistinguishable from religious experiences.
passage: One might define a genuine religious experience as one involving a vivid sense of transcendent presence, accompanied by awe or profound peace, and producing lasting positive transformation in the subject's character. This definition captures the phenomenological and transformative features commonly associated with religious experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the experience of mountaineering. Consider a climber who, upon reaching the summit of a great Himalayan peak, is overwhelmed by a vivid sense of the presence of something vast and transcendent — the immensity of the landscape, the silence, the nearness of the sky. She feels profound awe and a peace she has never before experienced. The experience transforms her character: she becomes more contemplative, more generous, more attentive to others. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the mountaineer herself attributes the experience entirely to the natural grandeur of the mountains and the physiological effects of extreme altitude. She has no religious framework and does not interpret the experience as involving anything divine or sacred. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenological quality and transformative effects of the experience, cannot distinguish a genuinely religious experience from an experience of natural sublimity that happens to share the same features.

definition: A communication C constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) C originates from a divine source, (ii) C conveys truths that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason, and (iii) C is preserved in a text or tradition that has been accepted by a religious community.
conditions: C originates from a divine source
C conveys truths that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason
C is preserved in a text or tradition that has been accepted by a religious community
counterexample: Consider two mutually contradictory scriptures, each accepted by its respective religious community as containing truths originating from a divine source and inaccessible to unaided reason. Both satisfy all three conditions. Yet they cannot both be genuine divine revelations insofar as they contradict one another on matters of substance. The definition provides no means of adjudicating between them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a criterion for distinguishing genuine divine communications from those merely believed to be divine by their recipients, so that any communication accepted by a community as divinely originated automatically qualifies, even when rival and contradictory communications satisfy the same conditions.
passage: One might characterise a divine revelation as a communication originating from a divine source, conveying truths inaccessible to unaided human reason, and preserved in a text or tradition accepted by a religious community. This characterisation captures the supernatural origin, cognitive content, and communal reception of revelation. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed by attending to the plurality of religious traditions. Consider the scriptures of two distinct religious communities — let us say the Vedas and the Quran — each of which satisfies all three conditions. Each is held by its community to originate from a divine source. Each conveys doctrines that its community regards as inaccessible to unaided reason. Each is preserved in a tradition accepted by millions. Yet they contradict one another on fundamental matters: the nature of the divine, the structure of the cosmos, the destiny of the human soul. They cannot both be genuine divine revelations insofar as they assert incompatible propositions.
