One might observe that the definition, by requiring only divine origin, rational inaccessibility, and communal acceptance, provides no criterion for distinguishing communications that genuinely originate from a divine source from those that are merely believed to do so. The definition classifies all accepted scriptures as divine revelations and offers no means of adjudicating the conflicts among them.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is directed toward a divine being, (ii) A expresses a request, thanksgiving, or adoration, and (iii) the person performing A sincerely believes that the divine being is capable of receiving the communication.
conditions: A is directed toward a divine being
A expresses a request, thanksgiving, or adoration
The person performing A sincerely believes that the divine being is capable of receiving the communication
counterexample: Consider a monk who practises contemplative prayer consisting entirely in silent attentiveness — an emptying of the mind of all content, including requests, thanksgiving, and adoration. The monk directs his attention toward the divine and sincerely believes his prayer is received. Yet his prayer expresses no request, no thanksgiving, and no adoration. Condition (ii) is not met. The definition would classify his contemplative practice as not genuine prayer, yet the contemplative tradition is among the oldest and most venerated forms of prayer in the history of religion.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the content of prayer to request, thanksgiving, or adoration, failing to recognise that genuine prayer may consist in silent contemplation, attentive presence, or receptive stillness that expresses no propositional content whatsoever.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as an act directed toward a divine being, expressing a request, thanksgiving, or adoration, performed by someone who sincerely believes the divine being can receive the communication. This definition captures the intentional, communicative, and faith-involving aspects of prayer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the contemplative traditions. Consider a monk who practises what is known in his tradition as the prayer of quiet — a form of prayer consisting entirely in silent attentiveness to the divine, in which the practitioner empties the mind of all discursive content. The monk directs his attention toward God. He sincerely believes that God receives his prayer. But the prayer expresses no request, no thanksgiving, and no adoration. It is an act of pure receptive stillness. Condition (ii) is not met. The definition would therefore classify this practice as something other than prayer. Yet one might observe that the contemplative tradition is among the most ancient and revered forms of prayer in the history of Christianity, Islam, and Judaism alike. The definition, by restricting the content of prayer to propositional attitudes, cannot accommodate the silent, non-discursive form of prayer that many traditions regard as the highest.

definition: A person S possesses immortality if and only if (i) S continues to exist after the death of the physical body, (ii) S retains personal identity — memories, character, and self-awareness — in the post-mortem state, and (iii) S's continued existence is without temporal limit.
conditions: S continues to exist after the death of the physical body
S retains personal identity — memories, character, and self-awareness — in the post-mortem state
S's continued existence is without temporal limit
counterexample: Consider the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth, in which a stream of consciousness continues after death without retaining the memories, character, or self-awareness of the previous life. There is continuity — a causal connection between one life and the next — but not the persistence of personal identity as the definition requires. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. The definition would therefore deny that Buddhist rebirth constitutes a form of immortality. Yet millions of persons regard rebirth as their tradition's account of how existence persists beyond death, and to deny it the name of immortality is to impose one tradition's understanding upon a concept that has been construed very differently across cultures.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a particular conception of personal identity — involving the retention of memories, character, and self-awareness — that is specific to certain Western philosophical and religious traditions, failing to recognise that other traditions construe post-mortem continuity in terms of causal or qualitative continuity rather than strict identity.
passage: One might define immortality as continued existence after bodily death, with retention of personal identity and without temporal limit. This definition captures the conception of immortality prominent in much Western philosophical and religious thought. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from Buddhist philosophy. In the Buddhist account, a stream of consciousness continues after the death of the body: one life gives rise to the next through a chain of causal dependence. Yet the memories, character traits, and self-awareness of the previous life are not retained in the next. There is continuity without identity in the Western sense. Condition (ii) is not met, and the definition would therefore deny that Buddhist rebirth constitutes a form of immortality. One might observe that this denial imposes a parochial standard upon a concept that has been understood very differently across the world's philosophical and religious traditions. The Buddhist does not claim that the same person is reborn; the claim is rather that existence continues, that the consequences of action persist, and that the stream of consciousness is not extinguished by bodily death. The definition, by requiring the retention of personal identity as understood in Western thought, cannot accommodate this alternative and cannot serve as a general characterisation of immortality across traditions.

definition: An object, place, or practice X is sacred if and only if (i) X is set apart from ordinary use and treated with special reverence, (ii) X is associated with a divine being or a transcendent reality, and (iii) contact with or violation of X is believed to produce extraordinary consequences — either beneficial or harmful.
conditions: X is set apart from ordinary use and treated with special reverence
X is associated with a divine being or a transcendent reality
Contact with or violation of X is believed to produce extraordinary consequences
counterexample: Consider the tomb of the unknown soldier in a modern secular republic. It is set apart from ordinary use and treated with the highest reverence. Contact with it — touching the wreath, stepping on the monument — is believed to produce extraordinary social consequences: public outrage, legal sanction, and dishonour. All the conditions appear to be met in a formal sense, yet the tomb is not associated with any divine being or transcendent reality. If one broadens 'transcendent reality' to include the nation or the idea of sacrifice, then condition (ii) is met, but only by stretching the notion of transcendence beyond its religious content. The definition cannot clearly distinguish the sacred from the merely solemn and ceremonially protected.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify what distinguishes the transcendent reality involved in sacredness from the secular ideals — nation, sacrifice, honour — that may also inspire reverence and ceremonial protection, so that the boundary between the sacred and the solemnly secular remains indeterminate.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use, associated with a divine being or transcendent reality, and believed to produce extraordinary consequences upon contact or violation. This tripartite definition captures the segregation, association, and potency that are commonly ascribed to sacred things. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from modern civic life. Consider the tomb of the unknown soldier in a secular republic. It is emphatically set apart from ordinary use and treated with the highest ceremonial reverence. Violation of it — walking upon the monument, defacing the inscription — is believed to produce extraordinary consequences: public outrage, legal punishment, lasting dishonour. These consequences are experienced as genuinely powerful, not merely conventional. Conditions (i) and (iii) appear to be satisfied. As for condition (ii), one might argue that the tomb is associated with a transcendent reality — the idea of the nation, the concept of ultimate sacrifice. But to extend the notion of transcendence to encompass secular ideals is to stretch it beyond any distinctively religious meaning. One might observe that the definition, by failing to specify what distinguishes religious transcendence from secular ideals that inspire comparable reverence and protection, cannot draw a clear boundary between the sacred and the solemnly ceremonial.

definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is contrary to the established laws of nature, (ii) E is brought about by a divine agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious purpose.
conditions: E is contrary to the established laws of nature
E is brought about by a divine agent
E serves a discernible religious purpose
counterexample: Consider a spontaneous remission of an advanced cancer in a patient who has been praying for recovery. Suppose for the sake of argument that a divine agent has indeed brought about the remission and that it serves the religious purpose of strengthening the faith of the patient's community. Two conditions are met. Yet the remission, though exceedingly rare, is not contrary to the established laws of nature — spontaneous remissions are documented in the medical literature and, however improbable, fall within the range of outcomes that natural processes can produce. The first condition is not satisfied. The definition therefore denies that this event is a miracle, even though both the divine agency and the religious purpose are present.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the possibility of miracles that operate through natural processes — events that, while not violating natural laws, are directed or timed by divine agency in a manner that gives them religious significance.
passage: One might propose that a miracle is an event contrary to the established laws of nature, brought about by a divine agent, and serving a discernible religious purpose. This characterisation captures the supernatural, purposive, and theologically significant features traditionally associated with miracles. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the theology of healing. Consider a patient suffering from an advanced malignancy who prays fervently for recovery, and suppose — granting for the sake of the argument a theistic framework — that a divine agent brings about a spontaneous remission that strengthens the faith of the patient's entire congregation. The event is brought about by a divine agent and serves a religious purpose. Two conditions are plainly met. Yet spontaneous remissions of advanced cancers, while exceedingly rare, are documented in the medical literature. They are not contrary to the established laws of nature — they fall, however improbably, within the range of outcomes that natural processes can produce. The first condition is not satisfied. The definition therefore rules that the event is not a miracle. One might observe that this verdict is unsatisfactory within the very theistic framework the definition presupposes. If a divine agent has deliberately timed and directed a natural process so as to produce a religiously significant outcome, it appears that the event possesses every feature we should wish to attribute to a miracle save the violation of natural law.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) God commands A, (ii) the agent is aware of God's command regarding A, and (iii) A is within the agent's power to perform.
conditions: God commands A
The agent is aware of God's command regarding A
A is within the agent's power to perform
counterexample: Consider the case of Abraham commanded to sacrifice Isaac. God commands the action. Abraham is aware of the command. The action is within Abraham's power. All three conditions are met, and the definition yields the verdict that the sacrifice is morally obligatory. Yet the action consists in the killing of an innocent child. If we accept that the action is morally obligatory simply because God commands it, we must accept that the killing of innocents can be morally obligatory — a conclusion that many moral intuitions resist with great force.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the commanded action be independently consistent with moral constraints — it provides no check against commands whose content appears to be morally abhorrent by any standard apart from the bare fact of divine decree.
passage: One might hold that an action is morally obligatory if and only if God commands it, the agent is aware of the command, and the action lies within the agent's power. This characterisation aims to ground moral obligation in divine authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the biblical narrative of Abraham. According to the narrative, God commands Abraham to sacrifice his son Isaac. Abraham is aware of the command. The action is within his power. All three conditions are met, and the definition yields the verdict that the sacrifice is morally obligatory. Yet the commanded action consists in the killing of an innocent child. If one accepts the definition, one must accept that the slaying of an innocent person can be morally required simply because a deity has commanded it. One might observe that this consequence reveals a deep difficulty. The definition provides no constraint upon the content of divine commands — it entails that any action whatsoever, however apparently abhorrent, becomes morally obligatory the moment it is divinely commanded. It appears that the characterisation, by grounding obligation entirely in the fact of divine command without any independent moral constraint upon the content of what may be commanded, cannot distinguish genuine moral obligations from directives whose content renders them morally repugnant by any standard apart from the bare authority of the commander.

definition: The existence of a supreme being is established if and only if (i) there is an argument from premises known to be true to the conclusion that a supreme being exists, (ii) the argument is deductively valid, and (iii) the argument has been accepted by a consensus of competent thinkers.
conditions: There is an argument from premises known to be true to the conclusion that a supreme being exists
The argument is deductively valid
The argument has been accepted by a consensus of competent thinkers
counterexample: Consider Anselm's ontological argument, which proceeds from the premise that we can conceive of a being than which nothing greater can be conceived, together with the premise that existence in reality is greater than existence in the understanding alone, to the conclusion that such a being must exist in reality. The argument is deductively valid. Suppose, for the sake of the example, that the premises are accepted as true by a consensus of competent thinkers. All three conditions are met. Yet one may construct a precisely parallel argument for the existence of a perfect island — an island than which no greater island can be conceived must exist in reality if existence is greater than mere conceivability. The logical structure is identical, yet the conclusion is absurd.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the argument's premises be immune to parodic replication — that the same logical structure cannot be used to establish the existence of any arbitrarily specified perfect entity, which would render the argument a general-purpose existence proof rather than a demonstration of any particular being's existence.
passage: One might maintain that the existence of a supreme being is established when there is a deductively valid argument from premises known to be true to that conclusion, and the argument has been accepted by a consensus of competent thinkers. This account appeals to the highest standards of logical rigour and collective rational endorsement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed through parody. Consider an argument modelled on the ontological argument: we can conceive of an island than which no more perfect island can be conceived; an island that exists in reality is more perfect than one that exists merely in the understanding; therefore the most perfect conceivable island must exist in reality. The argument is deductively valid — its conclusion follows from its premises by the same logical steps as the original. If one grants the premises of the original, consistency requires granting the parallel premises here. And if the original argument commands consensus, the parody should command equal consensus, since its logical structure is identical. All three conditions appear met. Yet the conclusion — that a supremely perfect island must exist — is manifestly absurd. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only validity, true premises, and consensus, cannot distinguish an argument that establishes something genuine from one whose logical structure can be replicated to prove the existence of any arbitrarily specified perfect entity.

definition: The existence of suffering is compatible with the existence of an omnipotent and benevolent deity if and only if (i) the suffering serves a greater good that could not be achieved without it, (ii) the suffering is the minimum necessary to achieve that good, and (iii) the beings who suffer are compensated for their suffering in due course.
conditions: The suffering serves a greater good that could not be achieved without it
The suffering is the minimum necessary to achieve that good
The beings who suffer are compensated for their suffering in due course
counterexample: Consider the suffering of a young child who dies of a painful disease before reaching an age at which moral development, character formation, or any other commonly cited greater good could plausibly be served. The suffering does not appear to serve any greater good that could not be achieved without it, since the child's death prevents the realisation of whatever good the suffering might have served. The definition would conclude that this suffering is incompatible with an omnipotent and benevolent deity — yet defenders of theism have long maintained that such cases are compatible with divine benevolence by appealing to inscrutable purposes that lie beyond human comprehension. The definition leaves no room for this appeal.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the possibility that the purposes served by suffering may be beyond human comprehension — it requires that the greater good be identifiable, when the theistic position may depend upon trusting that a sufficient reason exists without our being able to specify what it is.
passage: One might argue that suffering is compatible with divine omnipotence and benevolence if the suffering serves a greater good unattainable without it, if the suffering is the minimum necessary, and if the sufferers are duly compensated. This defence is familiar and has considerable intuitive appeal. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the suffering of those who die before any good could plausibly be served. Consider a child of two years who contracts a painful wasting disease and dies after months of agony. The child has not reached an age at which moral development, the formation of character through adversity, or any other commonly cited greater good could plausibly be achieved. The suffering cannot serve the good of moral growth because the child's death prevents that growth from occurring. The first condition — that the suffering serve a greater good — appears unsatisfied. The definition would conclude that this suffering is incompatible with divine benevolence. Yet theologians have long maintained that such cases do not refute theism, on the ground that divine purposes may lie beyond the reach of human comprehension. One might observe that the definition, by requiring an identifiable greater good, forecloses the appeal to inscrutability that has been a central element of theistic responses to suffering. It appears that the characterisation, by demanding that we specify the good served, cannot accommodate a theistic position that rests upon trust in purposes we cannot fathom.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition P if and only if (i) S accepts P as true, (ii) S lacks conclusive evidence for the truth of P, and (iii) S's acceptance of P is voluntary rather than compelled by the evidence.
conditions: S accepts P as true
S lacks conclusive evidence for the truth of P
S's acceptance of P is voluntary rather than compelled by the evidence
counterexample: Consider a confidence trickster who accepts the proposition that his fraudulent scheme will succeed. He lacks conclusive evidence — indeed, the outcome is uncertain. His acceptance is voluntary; nothing compels him to believe it. All three conditions are met. Yet the trickster's confident optimism about his swindle is not an instance of faith in any recognisable religious or philosophical sense; it is mere wishful thinking or calculated bluster.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the acceptance be oriented toward something of transcendent or ultimate significance and grounded in a relationship of trust, rather than being a merely voluntary acceptance of any uncertain proposition whatsoever.
passage: One might define faith as the voluntary acceptance of a proposition as true in the absence of conclusive evidence. This characterisation aims to distinguish faith from both ordinary belief, which is typically compelled by the evidence, and mere supposition, which does not involve acceptance as true. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from ordinary life. Consider a confidence trickster who devises an elaborate fraudulent scheme. He accepts the proposition that the scheme will succeed. He lacks conclusive evidence of its success — the outcome depends upon the gullibility of his victims, the inattention of the authorities, and sundry contingencies. His acceptance is voluntary; the evidence does not compel him to believe it, and indeed a sober assessment might counsel doubt. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the trickster's optimism about his swindle is not faith in any philosophically interesting sense. It is wishful thinking, or calculated bluster, or the occupational self-deception of the habitual criminal. It appears that the definition, by requiring only voluntary acceptance without conclusive evidence, cannot distinguish the religious or philosophical attitude of faith — which involves a certain orientation toward what is taken to be of ultimate significance — from any instance of confident acceptance in the face of uncertainty.

definition: An utterance U counts as a genuine prayer if and only if (i) U is addressed to a divine being, (ii) the speaker of U believes that the divine being can hear and respond, and (iii) U expresses a petition or request.
conditions: U is addressed to a divine being
The speaker of U believes that the divine being can hear and respond
U expresses a petition or request
counterexample: Consider a devout contemplative who sits in silence before the altar, addressing no petition, making no request, but simply dwelling in the presence of the divine with an attitude of receptive attentiveness. The contemplative addresses no utterance to a divine being — there is no utterance at all. No petition is expressed. At most one condition is met: the contemplative believes the divine being is present and attentive. Yet this practice of contemplative silence is widely regarded within the Christian mystical tradition as a form of prayer — indeed, as a higher form than petitionary prayer.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise non-petitionary and non-verbal forms of prayer, such as contemplation, adoration, and silent presence, which involve no utterance and no request but are nonetheless regarded as genuine prayer within major religious traditions.
passage: One might define a genuine prayer as an utterance addressed to a divine being by a speaker who believes the being can hear and respond, expressing a petition or request. This characterisation captures the communicative, theistic, and petitionary features most commonly associated with prayer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the contemplative traditions of Christianity. Consider a contemplative — a practitioner of what the tradition calls the prayer of quiet or the prayer of simple regard — who sits in silence before the altar, making no utterance, addressing no petition, expressing no request. The contemplative simply dwells in the presence of the divine with an attitude of receptive attentiveness, waiting upon a presence rather than seeking a favour. There is no utterance, so the first condition cannot be met. There is no petition, so the third condition fails. Yet this practice of contemplative silence is regarded within the mystical tradition — by figures from the author of The Cloud of Unknowing to the Carmelite writers of the sixteenth century — as a form of prayer, and indeed as a more advanced form than petitionary prayer. One might observe that the definition, by requiring an utterance expressing a petition, cannot accommodate the forms of prayer that consist precisely in the relinquishment of petition and the embrace of silence.

definition: An experience E counts as a genuinely religious experience if and only if (i) the subject of E reports a vivid awareness of a reality beyond the ordinary empirical world, (ii) E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's attitudes or conduct, and (iii) E is spontaneous rather than deliberately induced.
conditions: The subject of E reports a vivid awareness of a reality beyond the ordinary empirical world
E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's attitudes or conduct
E is spontaneous rather than deliberately induced
counterexample: Consider the meditative experiences cultivated by practitioners within the Buddhist and Christian contemplative traditions through years of disciplined practice — prolonged sitting, regulated breathing, systematic attention to the contents of consciousness. These experiences are not spontaneous; they are the product of deliberate and sustained effort. Yet many such practitioners report vivid awareness of a reality beyond ordinary experience, and these experiences produce lasting transformations in attitude and conduct. Two conditions are met. But the third — spontaneity — is not. The definition therefore excludes experiences that arise from disciplined contemplative practice, which constitute a central category of religious experience across traditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate religious experiences that arise through disciplined practice rather than spontaneously — it wrongly excludes the vast category of cultivated contemplative experiences that are deliberately sought through ascetic or meditative disciplines.
passage: One might characterise a genuinely religious experience as one in which the subject reports vivid awareness of a reality beyond the empirical world, undergoes a lasting transformation in attitude or conduct, and experiences the event spontaneously rather than through deliberate induction. This account aims to separate authentic religious experience from artificially produced altered states. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the contemplative traditions of both East and West. Consider a Buddhist monk who, after years of disciplined meditation — regulated breathing, sustained attention, systematic observation of mental processes — attains an experience of profound clarity that transforms his understanding and conduct for the remainder of his life. Or consider a Christian contemplative who, after decades of the disciplined practice of interior prayer, experiences what the tradition describes as union with the divine. In both cases, the subject reports vivid awareness of a reality beyond ordinary experience, and the experience produces lasting transformation. Two conditions are met. But the experiences are not spontaneous — they are the fruit of sustained, deliberate effort. The third condition fails. One might observe that the definition, by requiring spontaneity, excludes precisely those experiences that the contemplative traditions regard as most authoritative: those achieved through years of disciplined practice.

definition: A communication R from a divine source to a human recipient counts as a genuine revelation if and only if (i) R conveys propositional content that was previously inaccessible to the recipient, (ii) the recipient has adequate grounds for believing that R originates from a divine source, and (iii) the content of R is internally consistent.
conditions: R conveys propositional content that was previously inaccessible to the recipient
The recipient has adequate grounds for believing that R originates from a divine source
The content of R is internally consistent
counterexample: Consider an elaborate hoax in which a skilled illusionist, using hidden acoustics and lighting effects, leads a devout hermit to believe that a divine voice has communicated a set of novel and internally consistent theological propositions. The propositions were previously inaccessible to the hermit. The hermit has what appear to him to be adequate grounds — the extraordinary sensory circumstances — for believing the communication is divine. The content is internally consistent. All three conditions are met. Yet the communication is not a genuine revelation; it originates from a human deceiver, not from a divine source.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the communication actually originate from a divine source — it requires only that the recipient have adequate grounds for believing it does, which leaves room for cases in which the grounds are adequate by any reasonable standard yet the belief in divine origin is false.
passage: One might define a genuine revelation as a communication from a divine source that conveys previously inaccessible propositional content, is believed by the recipient on adequate grounds to originate from such a source, and is internally consistent. This characterisation aims to combine the novelty of the content, the rationality of the recipient's acceptance, and the coherence of the message. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed through the device of an elaborate hoax. Suppose a skilled illusionist, wishing to deceive a devout hermit, employs hidden acoustics, carefully contrived lighting, and an intimate awareness of the hermit's theological commitments to simulate a divine communication. The simulated voice conveys a set of novel theological propositions that are internally consistent and that the hermit has never encountered. The sensory circumstances — the apparent voice from an empty sky, the unearthly radiance — provide the hermit with what appear to be adequate grounds for accepting the divine origin of the message. All three conditions are met. Yet the communication is not a genuine revelation. It originates from a human deceiver, however skilled. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only adequate grounds for belief in divine origin rather than actual divine origin, cannot distinguish a genuine revelation from a sufficiently convincing forgery.

definition: A person S possesses immortality if and only if (i) S's conscious experience continues without interruption after the death of S's body, (ii) S retains personal identity through the transition from bodily to post-bodily existence, and (iii) the continued existence is of unlimited temporal duration.
conditions: S's conscious experience continues without interruption after the death of S's body
S retains personal identity through the transition from bodily to post-bodily existence
The continued existence is of unlimited temporal duration
counterexample: Consider a person whose body dies but whose conscious experience continues without interruption, with personal identity retained, for an unlimited duration — yet in a state of unrelieved suffering. The person is conscious, is identifiably the same person, and will exist forever. All three conditions are met. Yet many religious and philosophical traditions would regard this as a form of damnation rather than the desirable immortality the concept is meant to capture. The definition cannot distinguish immortality as a good from immortality as a curse.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the continued existence be of a character that makes it desirable or worth having — it treats mere perpetual continuation of personal conscious existence as sufficient, without any constraint upon the quality or nature of that existence.
passage: One might characterise immortality as the uninterrupted continuation of conscious experience after bodily death, with retention of personal identity, for an unlimited duration. This formulation captures the persistence, continuity, and endlessness that are commonly associated with the concept. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by considering the quality of the continued existence. Suppose a person dies and her conscious experience continues without interruption. She retains her personal identity — her memories, her character, her sense of self. And her continued existence has no temporal limit; it will persist without end. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose this unlimited continued existence consists entirely of unrelieved suffering — an eternity of conscious torment from which there is no escape and no respite. One might observe that this satisfies the definition of immortality yet corresponds more closely to what many traditions call damnation. The religious and philosophical interest in immortality has typically been an interest in a desirable form of continued existence — one that is worth having. It appears that the definition, by requiring only persistence, identity, and unlimited duration, cannot distinguish between immortality as a blessing and immortality as a curse. The concept, as defined, is silent upon the quality of the existence it guarantees.

definition: An object, place, or practice X is sacred if and only if (i) X is set apart from ordinary use, (ii) X is regarded with an attitude of reverence or awe by a community, and (iii) X is associated with a specific religious tradition or narrative.
conditions: X is set apart from ordinary use
X is regarded with an attitude of reverence or awe by a community
X is associated with a specific religious tradition or narrative
counterexample: Consider the tomb of Lenin in Red Square, Moscow, during the Soviet period. The tomb was set apart from ordinary use — it was a specially constructed mausoleum with restricted access and ceremonial guards. It was regarded with reverence and awe by millions of Soviet citizens. Yet it was not associated with a specific religious tradition or narrative; it was associated with a secular political ideology that was explicitly atheistic. The definition excludes objects that possess the phenomenological character of the sacred — the setting apart, the reverence, the awe — but are embedded in secular rather than religious frameworks.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the sacred may arise outside the boundaries of specific religious traditions — that the phenomenological features of sacredness (setting apart, reverence, awe) may attach to objects, places, or practices embedded in secular ideological contexts.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use, regarded with reverence or awe by a community, and associated with a specific religious tradition or narrative. This characterisation captures the separation, the affective response, and the religious embeddedness that are commonly taken to constitute sacredness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the political history of the twentieth century. Consider the tomb of Lenin in Red Square during the height of Soviet power. The tomb was set apart from ordinary use — it was a purpose-built mausoleum with restricted access, ceremonial guards, and elaborate rituals of visitation. It was regarded with an attitude of deep reverence by millions of Soviet citizens, who queued for hours to pass through its interior in a silence that observers frequently compared to the atmosphere of a cathedral. Two conditions are plainly met. Yet the tomb was not associated with any religious tradition or narrative. The Soviet state was explicitly and programmatically atheistic; the reverence accorded to Lenin's remains was embedded in a secular revolutionary ideology. One might observe that the definition, by requiring association with a specific religious tradition, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of secular sacredness — cases in which objects or places possess every phenomenological feature of the sacred yet belong to no religious framework.

definition: An argument A constitutes a sound proof of the existence of a divine being if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted, (ii) the inference from premises to conclusion is logically valid, and (iii) the conclusion of A asserts the existence of a being possessing the traditional divine attributes.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or universally accepted
The inference from premises to conclusion is logically valid
The conclusion of A asserts the existence of a being possessing the traditional divine attributes
counterexample: Suppose one constructs the following argument: let the premises be that every concept entails the existence of its object, and that we possess the concept of a divine being. The first premise, though controversial, has been accepted by certain philosophical schools as self-evident. The inference is valid: if every concept entails the existence of its object, and we possess the concept of a divine being, then a divine being exists. The conclusion asserts the existence of a being with traditional divine attributes. All three conditions are met. Yet the argument is not a sound proof, for the first premise is false — possessing a concept does not entail the existence of its object, as the concept of a golden mountain demonstrates.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises of A be true — that the self-evidence or universal acceptance demanded by condition (i) be a reliable indicator of truth, or that truth be required independently of acceptance or apparent self-evidence.
passage: One might define a sound proof of the existence of a divine being as an argument proceeding from self-evident or universally accepted premises, through logically valid inference, to a conclusion asserting the existence of a being with the traditional divine attributes. This characterisation captures the demonstrative structure and theological content that such a proof is expected to possess. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the gap between acceptance and truth. Suppose one frames the following argument: every concept entails the existence of its object; we possess the concept of a divine being; therefore a divine being exists. The first premise has been accepted by certain philosophical schools as self-evident — indeed, there have been periods in the history of thought when it was widely endorsed. The inference is plainly valid. And the conclusion attributes to the divine being the traditional attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the argument is not a sound proof. The first premise is false, as the concept of a golden mountain, or of a winged horse, amply demonstrates. Possessing a concept does not entail the existence of its object. The definition, by requiring that premises be self-evident or universally accepted without independently requiring that they be true, cannot distinguish arguments whose premises happen to enjoy acceptance from those whose premises are actually the case. It appears that acceptance and self-evidence, however widespread, are not sufficient guarantees of truth.

definition: A theodicy T constitutes a satisfactory response to the problem of evil if and only if (i) T provides a reason why a perfectly good and omnipotent being would permit suffering, (ii) the reason cited in T is logically consistent with the existence of such a being, and (iii) T does not deny the reality of suffering.
conditions: T provides a reason why a perfectly good and omnipotent being would permit suffering
The reason cited in T is logically consistent with the existence of such a being
T does not deny the reality of suffering
counterexample: Consider a theodicy that holds that all suffering is permitted because it builds moral character. The reason — character-building — is logically consistent with the existence of a good and omnipotent being. The theodicy does not deny the reality of suffering. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the theodicy fails to account for the suffering of infants and animals, who are incapable of moral development; their suffering cannot build their character, for they have no character to build. The reason provided, though applicable to some cases, is plainly inadequate to cover the full range of suffering that the problem of evil encompasses.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the reason provided by T be adequate to account for the full range of suffering actually observed — that it explain not merely some cases but all significant categories of suffering, including those that most resist the proposed justification.
passage: One might define a satisfactory theodicy as one that provides a reason, logically consistent with the existence of a perfectly good and omnipotent being, for the permission of suffering, without denying the reality of that suffering. This formulation captures the logical and substantive demands that the problem of evil imposes upon any would-be solution. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the familiar soul-making theodicy. Suppose one holds that all suffering is permitted because it serves to build moral character — that the tribulations of life are the means by which souls are shaped and refined. This reason is logically consistent with the existence of a good and omnipotent being; there is no formal contradiction in supposing that such a being permits suffering for the sake of character development. The theodicy does not deny the reality of suffering; on the contrary, it affirms that suffering is genuine and purposeful. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that this theodicy is manifestly inadequate to cover the full range of suffering actually observed. The suffering of infants who die before attaining any capacity for moral development, the suffering of animals who possess no moral character to be built, the suffering caused by natural disasters that strike indiscriminately — none of these can be explained by the character-building reason. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that a reason be provided, be logically consistent, and not deny suffering, cannot distinguish theodicies that account for all significant categories of suffering from those that address only a convenient subset.

definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E is an occurrence that violates a known law of nature, (ii) E is witnessed by one or more persons, and (iii) E is attributed by those witnesses to a divine cause.
conditions: E is an occurrence that violates a known law of nature
E is witnessed by one or more persons
E is attributed by those witnesses to a divine cause
counterexample: Suppose a conjuror performs a stage illusion in which a person appears to float unsupported in the air. Several audience members, unfamiliar with the technique employed, sincerely believe they have witnessed a violation of gravitational law. They attribute the event to divine intervention. The event appears to violate a known law of nature. It is witnessed by multiple persons. The witnesses attribute it to a divine cause. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard this as a miracle; the event has a perfectly mundane explanation in the conjuror's apparatus, and the witnesses' attribution to divine agency rests upon their ignorance of the mechanism employed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the violation of natural law be genuine rather than merely apparent — that the event actually contravene the laws of nature rather than merely appearing to do so owing to the observers' ignorance of the operative mechanism.
passage: One might characterise a miracle as an event that violates a known law of nature, is witnessed by one or more persons, and is attributed by those witnesses to a divine cause. This formulation aims to capture the supernatural, empirical, and theological dimensions of the miraculous. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the practice of stage conjuring. Suppose a skilled conjuror performs an illusion in which a member of the audience appears to float unsupported in the air, in apparent violation of the law of gravitation. Several spectators, wholly unfamiliar with the technique of suspension employed, sincerely believe they have witnessed a genuine violation of natural law. Moved by this belief, they attribute the event to divine intervention, supposing that no natural cause could produce such a spectacle. The event appears to violate a known law of nature. It is witnessed by multiple persons. The witnesses attribute it to a divine cause. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a miracle. The floating person is suspended by a concealed mechanical apparatus; the apparent violation of gravitational law is an illusion. The witnesses' attribution to divine agency rests entirely upon their ignorance of the operative mechanism. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the event violate a known law in the eyes of the witnesses and be attributed to divinity, cannot distinguish genuine violations of natural law from events that merely appear to violate such laws because the observers lack knowledge of the mundane causes at work.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition P if and only if (i) S accepts P as true, (ii) S cannot produce conclusive evidence for P, and (iii) S is committed to acting in accordance with P despite the absence of conclusive evidence.
conditions: S accepts P as true
S cannot produce conclusive evidence for P
S is committed to acting in accordance with P despite the absence of conclusive evidence
counterexample: Consider a gambler who accepts as true the proposition that his next wager will be profitable. He cannot produce conclusive evidence for this — the outcome is uncertain. He is committed to acting in accordance with this acceptance, staking his money on the wager. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not say the gambler has faith in any recognisable sense; he has merely a reckless confidence born of wishful thinking and habit, directed at a trivial and self-serving end.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object of faith possess a significance or seriousness that distinguishes faith from mere reckless confidence — that P pertain to matters of ultimate concern, moral gravity, or existential import, rather than to trivial or self-interested ends.
passage: One might define faith as the acceptance of a proposition as true, in the absence of conclusive evidence, together with a commitment to act in accordance with that proposition. This characterisation captures the volitional and evidentially precarious character that has often been attributed to faith. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the behaviour of a habitual gambler. Suppose a man who frequents the racecourse accepts as true the proposition that his next wager will be profitable. He has no conclusive evidence for this — indeed, the balance of evidence, given his past losses, tells rather against it. Nevertheless, he is firmly committed to acting in accordance with this acceptance, and he stakes a considerable sum upon the next race. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: acceptance, absence of conclusive evidence, and commitment to action. Yet one might observe that we should not describe this gambler as having faith. His attitude is better characterised as reckless confidence, born of wishful thinking and sustained by habit. It lacks the seriousness, the moral gravity, and the existential depth that we associate with faith in its proper employment. It appears that the definition, by requiring only acceptance without evidence and commitment to action, cannot distinguish faith from any instance of groundless confidence directed at a trivial or self-serving end.

definition: An object O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use, (ii) O is treated with special reverence by a community, and (iii) the desecration of O would provoke moral outrage within that community.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use
O is treated with special reverence by a community
The desecration of O would provoke moral outrage within that community
counterexample: Consider a national flag in a fervently patriotic society. The flag is set apart from ordinary use — it is displayed only on official occasions, handled according to strict protocols, and never employed as a common textile. It is treated with special reverence by the community. And its desecration — burning or defacing — would provoke intense moral outrage. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call the flag sacred in the religious sense; its special status derives from political sentiment rather than from any connection to the divine, the transcendent, or the numinous.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the reverence directed towards O be connected to the divine, the transcendent, or the numinous — that O be set apart not merely by social convention or political sentiment but by its perceived relation to a reality beyond the ordinary empirical world.
passage: One might propose that an object is sacred when it is set apart from ordinary use, treated with special reverence by a community, and would provoke moral outrage if desecrated. This characterisation captures the segregation, veneration, and inviolability that we associate with the sacred. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the treatment of national symbols. Consider the flag of a fervently patriotic nation. It is set apart from ordinary use: strict protocols govern its display, it is never employed as a common textile, and it is stored and transported with ceremonial care. The community treats it with the deepest reverence; citizens stand in its presence, salute it, and speak of it in tones of solemn devotion. And its desecration — whether by burning, defacing, or dragging upon the ground — would provoke intense moral outrage throughout the society. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to call the national flag sacred in the sense in which that term is employed in the philosophy of religion. Its special status derives from political sentiment and patriotic feeling, not from any perceived connection to the divine, the transcendent, or the numinous. It appears that the definition, by requiring only segregation, reverence, and the provocation of outrage, cannot distinguish objects whose special status arises from a relationship to the transcendent from those whose status is rooted in secular loyalties and political passions.

definition: A communication C constitutes a genuine divine revelation if and only if (i) C conveys information that could not have been arrived at by unaided human reasoning, (ii) C is received by a person who sincerely believes it to originate from a divine source, and (iii) the content of C is consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine.
conditions: C conveys information that could not have been arrived at by unaided human reasoning
C is received by a person who sincerely believes it to originate from a divine source
The content of C is consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine
counterexample: Suppose a person suffering from a high fever experiences a vivid hallucination in which a luminous figure communicates a moral precept of remarkable profundity — one that, let us grant, could not plausibly have been arrived at by the person's unaided reasoning. The person sincerely believes the communication originates from a divine source. The content of the communication — a precept of universal compassion — is consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine. All three conditions are met. Yet the communication is the product of a fever-induced hallucination and does not originate from any divine source.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C actually originate from a divine source — that the communication be genuinely produced by the divine rather than merely believed by the recipient to have such an origin, since the sincerity of the recipient's belief is no guarantee of its truth.
passage: One might define a genuine divine revelation as a communication conveying information beyond the reach of unaided human reasoning, received by a person who sincerely believes its divine origin, and whose content is consistent with the moral attributes of the divine. This characterisation aims to distinguish revelatory communications from ordinary human insights by their extraordinary content, the sincerity of their reception, and their moral coherence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomena of illness. Suppose a person suffering from a severe fever experiences a vivid hallucination in which a luminous figure communicates a moral precept of remarkable depth — let us say, a precept commanding universal compassion towards all sentient creatures. The precept is of such profundity that we may grant it could not plausibly have been arrived at by the person's unaided reasoning in ordinary circumstances. The person sincerely believes the communication to originate from a divine source. And the content — universal compassion — is entirely consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this communication is the product of a fever-induced hallucination. It originates from the disordered physiology of the patient's brain, not from any divine being. The sincerity of the recipient's belief is beyond question, but sincerity is no guarantee of truth. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the recipient's sincere belief in divine origin rather than actual divine origin, cannot distinguish genuine revelations from hallucinations, however sincere and morally edifying.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A has been commanded by a divine being, (ii) the command is addressed to all moral agents, and (iii) the command is promulgated through a recognised authoritative channel.
conditions: A has been commanded by a divine being
The command is addressed to all moral agents
The command is promulgated through a recognised authoritative channel
counterexample: Suppose a divine being commands, through a recognised prophet, that all moral agents shall refrain from showing kindness to strangers. The command is issued by a divine being. It is addressed to all moral agents. It is promulgated through a recognised authoritative channel. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to regard the avoidance of kindness as morally obligatory, for the content of the command appears to be morally repugnant; compliance would require the suppression of a widely recognised virtue.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the content of the divine command be independently morally defensible — that the commanded action accord with standards of goodness that are not themselves derived solely from the fact of having been commanded, so that moral obligation is not reduced to mere obedience irrespective of the nature of what is commanded.
passage: One might define moral obligation as the property of actions commanded by a divine being, addressed to all moral agents, and promulgated through a recognised authoritative channel. This characterisation grounds morality in divine authority and ensures that obligations are universal and publicly accessible. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by considering a morally repugnant command. Suppose a divine being issues, through a recognised prophet, the command that all moral agents shall refrain from showing kindness to strangers. The command satisfies all three conditions: it is issued by a divine being, addressed to all moral agents, and promulgated through a recognised authoritative channel. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to regard the avoidance of kindness to strangers as morally obligatory. The content of the command is repugnant; its observance would require the systematic suppression of a virtue that is widely and, it seems, rightly recognised as central to the moral life. If we are prepared to say that an action is morally obligatory simply because it has been divinely commanded, irrespective of the nature of the action, then we are committed to the position that any action whatever could become obligatory merely by being commanded — including actions that are manifestly cruel, unjust, or degrading. It appears that the definition, by locating the source of obligation entirely in the fact of divine command without reference to the independent moral character of the commanded action, cannot distinguish genuine moral obligations from demands that happen to issue from a divine source but whose content we have every reason to reject.

definition: A person S survives death if and only if (i) there exists, after the destruction of S's body, a being B who possesses all of S's memories, (ii) B is conscious and capable of experience, and (iii) B identifies itself as S.
conditions: There exists, after the destruction of S's body, a being B who possesses all of S's memories
B is conscious and capable of experience
B identifies itself as S
counterexample: Suppose that at the moment of a person's death, an angel creates two distinct beings, each of whom possesses all of the deceased person's memories, is conscious and capable of experience, and identifies itself as the deceased. Both satisfy all three conditions. Yet they cannot both be the deceased person, for they are numerically distinct from each other; if both were identical to the original person, they would be identical to each other, which they manifestly are not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the being B be uniquely related to S — that there be at most one such being satisfying the conditions — since the possibility of multiple candidates, each satisfying every stated condition, reveals that the conditions are insufficient to secure the identity of B with S.
passage: One might propose that a person survives death when there exists, after the destruction of the body, a conscious being who possesses all of the deceased person's memories and identifies itself as that person. This characterisation captures the psychological continuity and self-awareness that many philosophers have regarded as essential to personal identity across time. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed through a thought-experiment involving duplication. Suppose that at the moment of a person's death, a supernatural being creates not one but two beings, each endowed with the complete set of the deceased person's memories, each conscious and capable of experience, and each sincerely identifying itself as the deceased. Both beings satisfy all three conditions of the proposed definition. Yet they cannot both be the deceased person, for they are numerically distinct from each other. If the first were identical to the original person and the second were also identical to the original person, then the first and the second would be identical to each other — which they manifestly are not, since they are two separate beings with separate streams of consciousness. One might observe that the definition, by specifying the qualitative conditions that a surviving being must satisfy without requiring that at most one being satisfy them, cannot secure the identity of the survivor with the deceased. The mere possession of memories and self-identification does not suffice for identity when duplication is possible.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a genuine prayer if and only if (i) U is addressed to a divine being, (ii) U expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or praise, and (iii) U is produced voluntarily by the person who utters it.
conditions: U is addressed to a divine being
U expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or praise
U is produced voluntarily by the person who utters it
counterexample: Suppose an actor, performing a role in a theatrical production, voluntarily addresses a speech to a divine being, expressing a petition for the deliverance of the character's family. The utterance is addressed to a divine being. It expresses a petition. It is produced voluntarily. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard the actor's speech as a genuine prayer; it is a performance, and the actor may have no intention whatever of communicating with the divine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person who utters U intend the utterance as a genuine communication with the divine — that U be produced with the sincere purpose of addressing the divine being, rather than as a performance, recitation, or dramatic exercise.
passage: One might characterise a genuine prayer as a voluntary utterance addressed to a divine being and expressing a petition, thanksgiving, or praise. This formulation captures the intentional, communicative, and devotional character that we associate with prayer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the theatre. Suppose an actor, in the course of a dramatic performance, delivers a speech addressed to a divine being, petitioning for the deliverance of the character's family from affliction. The actor produces the utterance voluntarily — no one compels him to take the role. The utterance is addressed to a divine being. It expresses a petition. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should not describe this as a genuine prayer. The actor has no intention of communicating with the divine. His utterance is a performance, produced for the benefit of an audience, and its devotional content belongs to the character he portrays rather than to himself. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the utterance be voluntary, addressed to the divine, and expressive of petition or praise, cannot distinguish genuine prayers — which are produced with the sincere intention of communicating with the divine — from performances that satisfy all the stated conditions while lacking any devotional purpose.

definition: An experience E constitutes a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a reality beyond the empirical world, (ii) E produces in the subject a lasting transformation of attitudes or conduct, and (iii) the subject is sincere in reporting E.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of the presence of a reality beyond the empirical world
E produces in the subject a lasting transformation of attitudes or conduct
The subject is sincere in reporting E
counterexample: Suppose a person ingests a powerful psychoactive substance and experiences a vivid sense of the presence of a transcendent reality — a sense of union with something infinite and beyond the ordinary world. The experience produces a lasting transformation: the person becomes more compassionate, more reflective, and less attached to material possessions. The person sincerely reports the experience. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call this a genuine religious experience, for its proximate cause is a chemical alteration of brain function, and the vivid sense of transcendence may be entirely an artefact of that alteration.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the experience have an appropriate causal origin — that the sense of transcendent presence be produced by a reality beyond the empirical world rather than by a purely natural cause such as chemical stimulation, neurological disorder, or extreme fatigue.
passage: One might define a genuine religious experience as one involving a vivid sense of the presence of a reality beyond the empirical world, producing a lasting transformation in the subject, and sincerely reported. This characterisation captures the phenomenological, transformative, and testimonial dimensions that have been thought essential to religious experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the pharmacological sciences. Suppose a person ingests a powerful psychoactive substance and thereupon experiences a vivid and overwhelming sense of the presence of a transcendent reality — a sense of union with something infinite, eternal, and beyond the boundaries of the ordinary world. The experience is so profound that it produces a lasting transformation: the person becomes markedly more compassionate, adopts a reflective and contemplative manner of life, and relinquishes former attachments to material possessions. The person sincerely reports the experience and its effects. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should hesitate to call this a genuine religious experience. Its proximate cause is a chemical alteration of brain function induced by the ingestion of a substance. The vivid sense of transcendent presence may be entirely an artefact of that chemical alteration, bearing no relation to the actual presence of any reality beyond the empirical world. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenological character of the experience, its transformative effects, and the sincerity of the report, cannot distinguish experiences that are genuinely produced by a transcendent reality from those that are produced by natural causes that merely simulate the phenomenology of transcendence.

definition: An argument A establishes the existence of a divine being if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true or universally accepted, (ii) A follows valid deductive rules of inference, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being of supreme perfection exists.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evidently true or universally accepted
A follows valid deductive rules of inference
A arrives at the conclusion that a being of supreme perfection exists
counterexample: Consider an argument that begins with the self-evident premise that some things are more perfect than others, adds the universally accepted premise that a finite chain of increasing perfection must terminate in a most perfect member, and validly deduces the conclusion that a being of supreme perfection exists. All three conditions are met. Yet the conclusion follows only if the chain of increasing perfection that exists among observable things must terminate in a being that exists independently of our ordering of observable things. The argument has shown that the concept of supreme perfection is coherent and that a logical chain can be constructed, but it has not shown that any actual being corresponds to this concept.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the argument establish the real, mind-independent existence of the divine being, as opposed to merely demonstrating the logical coherence of the concept or the formal validity of a deductive chain that terminates in a conceptual conclusion.
passage: One might propose that an argument establishes the existence of a divine being when it proceeds from self-evident or universally accepted premises, follows valid deductive inference, and arrives at the conclusion that a being of supreme perfection exists. This characterisation captures the logical rigour and foundational certainty that we should expect of so momentous a demonstration. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed with some care. Suppose one begins with the self-evident premise that among the things we observe, some are more perfect than others: a healthy organism is more perfect than a diseased one, a harmonious arrangement more perfect than a disordered one. One adds the premise, which enjoys wide acceptance, that a finite chain of things ordered by increasing perfection must terminate in a most perfect member. From these premises, one validly deduces that a being of supreme perfection exists. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. The premises are self-evident or universally accepted; the deductive rules are valid; and the conclusion affirms the existence of a supremely perfect being. Nevertheless, one might observe that the argument has accomplished less than it appears. It has shown that the concept of supreme perfection may be coherently placed at the terminus of a logical ordering, but it has not shown that any actual being, existing independently of our conceptual arrangements, corresponds to this terminus. The chain of increasing perfection is a chain among observed things and concepts; the leap to the real existence of a being at its apex requires a further premise — that conceptual orderings of this kind must terminate in something real — which is neither self-evident nor universally accepted. It appears that the definition, by requiring only self-evident premises, valid inference, and a conclusion about supreme perfection, fails to demand that the argument bridge the gap between logical coherence and real, mind-independent existence.

definition: A theodicy T is successful if and only if (i) T identifies a morally sufficient reason for the permission of suffering, (ii) the reason cited in T is compatible with the attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine — omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness, and (iii) T does not deny or minimise the reality of the suffering it seeks to explain.
conditions: T identifies a morally sufficient reason for the permission of suffering
The reason cited in T is compatible with the attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine
T does not deny or minimise the reality of the suffering it seeks to explain
counterexample: Consider a theodicy that argues suffering is permitted because it provides opportunities for the development of virtuous character: compassion is impossible without the witness of pain, courage without the presence of danger, generosity without the existence of need. The reason — character development — is morally significant. It is compatible with divine omnipotence, omniscience, and goodness. And the theodicy fully acknowledges the reality of suffering. All three conditions are met. Yet this theodicy cannot account for the suffering of infants or of animals, who are incapable of developing virtuous character. Their suffering serves none of the purposes the theodicy cites, yet it is among the most grievous and abundant forms of suffering in the world.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the morally sufficient reason cited in T apply to all instances of suffering it is invoked to explain, rather than to only a subset — that the theodicy account for the full range and distribution of suffering, including cases where the cited reason is manifestly inapplicable.
passage: One might define a successful theodicy as one that identifies a morally sufficient reason for the permission of suffering, one compatible with the traditional divine attributes and that does not deny the reality of suffering. This characterisation captures the moral, theological, and phenomenological requirements of a serious response to the problem of evil. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the so-called soul-making theodicy. Suppose one argues that suffering is permitted because it furnishes the necessary conditions for the development of virtuous character. Compassion cannot arise except in the presence of pain; courage requires the reality of danger; generosity presupposes genuine need. The reason is morally significant: the development of virtue is a good of the highest order. It is compatible with divine omnipotence, omniscience, and goodness, for a perfectly good being might reasonably permit suffering in the service of so great a purpose. And the theodicy fully acknowledges the reality and severity of the suffering in question. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the theodicy fails utterly to account for the suffering of infants, who die before they can develop any character whatsoever, or of non-human animals, whose cognitive capacities do not extend to the cultivation of moral virtue. These beings suffer — often grievously — yet their suffering serves none of the purposes the theodicy cites. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the identification of a morally sufficient reason compatible with divine attributes, fails to demand that the reason apply to all instances of suffering the theodicy is invoked to address. A theodicy that accounts for the suffering of some while leaving the suffering of others entirely unexplained can hardly be called successful.

definition: An event M constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) M is an event that contravenes a well-established law of nature, (ii) M occurs in a context in which it is attributed to divine agency, and (iii) the testimony reporting M comes from witnesses who are sincere and not known to be unreliable.
conditions: M is an event that contravenes a well-established law of nature
M occurs in a context in which it is attributed to divine agency
The testimony reporting M comes from witnesses who are sincere and not known to be unreliable
counterexample: Suppose a group of sincere and normally reliable witnesses in a rural village reports that a statue wept tears of blood. The event, if it occurred, would contravene the established laws governing the behaviour of inert matter. It takes place in a religious context and is attributed to divine agency. The witnesses are sincere and have no history of unreliability. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the weeping is subsequently explained by the discovery that the statue is made of a porous material that absorbed rainwater mixed with iron-rich clay, and that temperature fluctuations caused the reddish liquid to seep from the eyes. The event did not actually contravene any law of nature; it merely appeared to do so because the witnesses were ignorant of the material mechanism.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the apparent contravention of natural law not be the product of ignorance of the actual causal mechanism — that all naturalistic explanations of M have been excluded, rather than merely that the event appear to the witnesses to be inexplicable by natural means.
passage: One might define a miracle as an event that contravenes a well-established law of nature, occurs in a context of divine attribution, and is reported by sincere and reliable witnesses. This characterisation captures the supernatural, religious, and testimonial dimensions that the concept of miracle traditionally involves. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of religious phenomena. Suppose a group of villagers, all of them sincere and with no prior record of unreliability, reports that a statue in the local church has wept tears of blood. The event, if genuine, would contravene established physical laws: inert stone does not produce biological fluids. The event occurs in a manifestly religious context and is universally attributed to divine agency. The witnesses are numerous, sincere, and not known to be unreliable. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, suppose that a subsequent investigation reveals that the statue is composed of a porous stone that had absorbed rainwater mixed with iron-rich clay from the surrounding soil, and that a change in temperature caused the reddish liquid to seep from small apertures near the eyes. The event did not in fact contravene any law of nature; it was an entirely natural process whose mechanism was unknown to the witnesses. The appearance of miraculous contravention was the product of ignorance of the operative cause, not of any genuine departure from natural regularity. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the event contravene a law of nature and be reported by sincere witnesses, fails to demand that the apparent contravention not be the result of ignorance of the natural mechanism. Without such a requirement, the definition cannot distinguish genuine miracles from events whose natural explanation merely eludes the observers.

definition: A person P has faith that a proposition Q is true if and only if (i) P believes that Q is true, (ii) P lacks conclusive evidence for Q, and (iii) P's belief in Q is accompanied by a subjective feeling of confidence or trust.
conditions: P believes that Q is true
P lacks conclusive evidence for Q
P's belief in Q is accompanied by a subjective feeling of confidence or trust
counterexample: Suppose a compulsive gambler believes that his next bet will win, despite lacking any conclusive evidence for this outcome, and suppose his belief is accompanied by a powerful subjective feeling of confidence.
