He believes, he lacks evidence, and he feels confident. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not describe the gambler as having faith in the relevant sense; his confidence is the product of addiction and cognitive distortion rather than the kind of trust or commitment that characterises genuine faith.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's belief be oriented towards an object or commitment that transcends mere self-interest or compulsion — that faith involve a volitional dimension of commitment or loyalty to something regarded as worthy of trust, rather than mere confident belief in the absence of evidence.
passage: One might propose that a person has faith when she believes a proposition to be true, lacks conclusive evidence for it, and feels confident or trusting in that belief. This characterisation captures the cognitive, evidential, and affective dimensions that we commonly associate with faith. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the psychology of compulsive gambling. Suppose a gambler, seated at a card table, believes with great conviction that his next bet will win. He lacks any conclusive evidence for this — the odds are against him, and he has lost repeatedly. Nevertheless, his belief is accompanied by a powerful subjective feeling of confidence, a sensation that this time will be different. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met: he believes, he lacks evidence, and he feels confident. Yet one might observe that we should not describe the gambler as having faith in the sense that the definition means to capture. His confidence is not the expression of a thoughtful commitment to something he regards as worthy of trust; it is the product of compulsion, of cognitive distortion bred by addiction. It lacks the volitional, self-transcending character that distinguishes genuine faith from mere wishful thinking or pathological confidence. It appears that the definition, by requiring only belief, evidential deficiency, and subjective confidence, fails to demand that faith involve a dimension of voluntary commitment or loyalty directed towards an object regarded as worthy — a dimension that separates the trust of the devout from the delusion of the addicted.

definition: An object or place X is sacred if and only if (i) X is set apart from ordinary use by a community, (ii) X is treated with special reverence or ritual observance by members of the community, and (iii) contact with X or behaviour in its presence is governed by prescriptive norms that differ from those governing ordinary objects or places.
conditions: X is set apart from ordinary use by a community
X is treated with special reverence or ritual observance by members of the community
Contact with X or behaviour in its presence is governed by prescriptive norms that differ from those governing ordinary objects or places
counterexample: Consider the original manuscript of a nation's constitution, housed in a climate-controlled vault, removed from ordinary use, and treated with extraordinary reverence by citizens who queue for hours merely to view it. Behaviour in its presence is governed by strict norms: silence is required, photography is forbidden, and visitors must pass through elaborate security procedures. All three conditions are met. Yet the manuscript is not sacred in the religious sense; the reverence it commands is civic and patriotic, grounded in political history rather than in any relation to the transcendent or the divine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the setting-apart and the reverence directed towards X be grounded in a relation to the transcendent, the numinous, or the divine — that the norms governing X derive from a sense of contact with a reality beyond the merely human and historical.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use, treated with special reverence, and governed by prescriptive norms distinct from those applying to ordinary things. This characterisation captures the practical dimensions of sacredness: the separation, the reverence, and the normative regulation that typically attend sacred objects and places. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed from civic life. Consider the original manuscript of a nation's founding constitution, preserved in a climate-controlled vault in the capital. The manuscript is set apart from ordinary use: it is never handled, never moved, never employed for any practical purpose. It is treated with extraordinary reverence: citizens queue for hours to view it through its protective glass, and public ceremonies are conducted in its presence on national anniversaries. And behaviour in its vicinity is governed by prescriptive norms that differ markedly from those governing ordinary documents: silence is maintained, photography is forbidden, visitors must pass through elaborate security protocols, and guards enforce a strict code of conduct. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the manuscript is not sacred in the sense the definition means to capture. The reverence it commands is civic and patriotic, grounded in political history and national identity rather than in any relation to the transcendent or the divine. It appears that the definition, by requiring only separation, reverence, and special norms, fails to demand that these attitudes and practices be grounded in a perceived relation to something that transcends the merely human and historical — a dimension of the numinous or the divine without which sacredness collapses into mere civic solemnity.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by a divine being, (ii) the divine being issuing the command is perfectly good, and (iii) the command is communicated to moral agents through a recognised channel of revelation.
conditions: A is commanded by a divine being
The divine being issuing the command is perfectly good
The command is communicated to moral agents through a recognised channel of revelation
counterexample: Suppose two recognised channels of revelation — say, two scriptural traditions both accepted by a community as authentic — communicate conflicting commands from the same perfectly good divine being: one commands that a particular form of commerce be practised, and the other commands that it be forbidden. Both commands are issued by the same divine being. The being is perfectly good. Both are communicated through recognised channels of revelation. All three conditions are met for each command, yet A and not-A cannot both be morally obligatory. The definition yields a contradiction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require a principle for adjudicating between conflicting commands received through different recognised channels of revelation — or, more fundamentally, a criterion for determining which communications genuinely represent the will of the divine being when the channels of revelation disagree.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory when it is commanded by a perfectly good divine being through a recognised channel of revelation. This characterisation aims to ground moral obligation in divine authority while safeguarding against the attribution of obligations to malevolent or imagined sources. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the familiar problem of conflicting revelations. Suppose a religious community recognises two scriptural traditions as authentic channels of divine communication. Both traditions are regarded as genuine transmissions of the will of the same perfectly good divine being. Now suppose the two traditions issue conflicting commands on a specific matter: one text commands that a particular form of commerce be practised as a matter of duty, while the other forbids the same commerce as morally impermissible. Each command is issued by the same divine being. The being is perfectly good. Each command is communicated through a channel of revelation that the community recognises as authentic. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied in both cases. Yet the resulting moral obligations are contradictory: the same action is both obligatory and forbidden. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only divine origin, perfect goodness, and a recognised revelatory channel, provides no resource for resolving such conflicts. It lacks any principle for determining which communication genuinely represents the divine will when the recognised channels disagree. Without such a principle, the definition is liable to generate contradictory obligations whenever the channels of revelation produce divergent commands — a situation that the history of religious thought suggests is far from uncommon.

definition: A communication C constitutes a genuine divine revelation if and only if (i) C conveys propositions that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason, (ii) C is received by a human agent who sincerely reports its content, and (iii) C is consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine.
conditions: C conveys propositions that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason
C is received by a human agent who sincerely reports its content
C is consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine
counterexample: Suppose a mystic sincerely reports receiving a communication that conveys a previously unknown but subsequently verified astronomical fact — the existence of a distant planet — which could not plausibly have been discovered by unaided reason. The content is consistent with divine moral attributes, containing no morally objectionable elements. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the mystic's knowledge was actually acquired through an unconscious memory of a conversation overheard at a lecture by a visiting astronomer whose preliminary findings had not yet been published. The communication is not genuinely divine; it originates in a mundane source whose contribution was forgotten.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the source of C be verified as genuinely divine rather than as a natural cognitive process — that the origin of the communication be established independently of the mere fact that the propositions it conveys exceed what the recipient could have discovered by conscious reasoning.
passage: One might propose that a genuine divine revelation is a communication conveying propositions beyond the reach of unaided human reason, received by a sincere human agent, and consistent with the moral attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine. This characterisation captures the cognitive, testimonial, and moral dimensions that we associate with authentic revelatory experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of cryptomnesia — the unconscious retrieval of forgotten memories. Suppose a mystic sincerely reports receiving a divine communication that conveys the existence of a hitherto unknown planet in a distant region of the solar system. The proposition could not have been discovered by the mystic's unaided reasoning; she has no astronomical training. The content is morally unobjectionable and fully consistent with divine goodness. And suppose the planet's existence is subsequently confirmed by telescopic observation. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, suppose that the mystic had, months earlier, briefly overheard a visiting astronomer at a public lecture mention preliminary evidence for exactly such a planet — evidence not yet published. The mystic has no conscious memory of this encounter, but the information lodged in her memory and resurfaced during a period of contemplative absorption, presenting itself with the phenomenological character of divine communication. The content is genuine and verified, but its source is entirely mundane. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the content exceed unaided reason, be sincerely reported, and be morally consistent, fails to demand any independent verification that the source of the communication is divine rather than a natural cognitive process operating below the threshold of conscious awareness.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, (ii) A expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or act of praise, and (iii) the person performing A believes that the divine being to whom the address is directed exists and is capable of receiving the communication.
conditions: A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being
A expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or act of praise
The person performing A believes that the divine being to whom the address is directed exists and is capable of receiving the communication
counterexample: Suppose an actor, performing the role of a devout character in a play, delivers a passionate prayer on stage: he addresses a divine being, expresses a petition for mercy, and while performing he so immerses himself in the role that he genuinely believes, for the duration of the scene, that the divine being exists and hears him. All three conditions are met. Yet the actor is not genuinely praying; his address is a performance, undertaken for the sake of theatrical art rather than as a sincere communication with the divine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person performing A intend the address as a genuine act of communication with the divine being, undertaken for its own sake and not as a performance, exercise, or instrumental means to some non-religious end.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, expressing petition, thanksgiving, or praise, and performed by someone who believes in the existence and receptivity of that being. This characterisation captures the communicative, expressive, and cognitive dimensions of prayer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the theatre. Suppose an actor, performing the role of a devout penitent in a dramatic production, delivers a passionate prayer upon the stage. He addresses a divine being by name; he expresses a heartfelt petition for mercy and forgiveness; and suppose that, being a method actor of extraordinary commitment, he has so thoroughly immersed himself in the character that he genuinely believes, for the duration of the scene, that the divine being exists and hears his words. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. The address is directed towards a divine being. It expresses a petition. The performer believes the being exists and is capable of receiving the communication. Nevertheless, one might observe that the actor is not genuinely praying. His address is a performance, undertaken for the sake of art and the entertainment of an audience, not as an authentic act of communication with the divine. When the curtain falls, the belief will evaporate with the role. It appears that the definition, by requiring only address, expression, and belief, fails to demand that the act be intended as a genuine, non-instrumental communication with the divine — that it be undertaken for its own sake as a spiritual act and not merely as a component of some extrinsic enterprise.

definition: A being B is immortal if and only if (i) B possesses a continuous personal identity over time, (ii) B's existence has no temporal end, and (iii) B retains the capacities — consciousness, memory, volition — that constitute personal life.
conditions: B possesses a continuous personal identity over time
B's existence has no temporal end
B retains the capacities that constitute personal life: consciousness, memory, and volition
counterexample: Suppose a person's memories, personality, and patterns of thought are perfectly copied into a succession of biological bodies, each replacing the last before its death, so that at every moment there exists exactly one being with the person's full set of memories, personality, and capacities. This being possesses continuous personal identity as defined by psychological continuity. Its existence has no temporal end, since each copy is replaced before it perishes. And it retains consciousness, memory, and volition. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that the original person is immortal, for each copy is a numerically distinct organism, and the survival of a pattern is not the survival of the individual.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the continuity of personal identity be grounded in the persistence of the same individual subject, rather than in the mere replication of psychological characteristics across numerically distinct bearers.
passage: One might define immortality as the possession of continuous personal identity, temporally unending existence, and the retention of the capacities constituting personal life. This characterisation captures the identity, duration, and qualitative requirements that we associate with genuine survival beyond death. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from a thought experiment concerning replication. Suppose a person's complete psychological profile — memories, personality traits, patterns of reasoning, habits of volition — is perfectly copied into a new biological body each time the current body approaches death. At every moment there exists exactly one being in possession of the full set of the original person's psychological characteristics. The being possesses continuous personal identity as judged by any criterion of psychological continuity: it remembers what the original remembered, desires what the original desired, and reasons as the original reasoned. Its existence has no temporal end, since a new body is provided before the old one perishes. And it retains consciousness, memory, and volition in undiminished form. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the original person is not immortal in any meaningful sense. Each copy is a numerically distinct organism; the survival of a pattern of memories and dispositions across a succession of different bodies is not the survival of the individual. The definition, it appears, mistakes the preservation of a psychological profile for the persistence of a person, and thereby fails to require that personal identity be grounded in the continued existence of the same individual subject rather than in the serial replication of that subject's characteristics.

definition: An experience E constitutes a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) E involves a vivid sense of contact with a reality that transcends the ordinary empirical world, (ii) E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's attitudes and behaviour, and (iii) the subject of E is psychologically stable and not under the influence of intoxicants.
conditions: E involves a vivid sense of contact with a reality that transcends the ordinary empirical world
E produces a lasting transformation in the subject's attitudes and behaviour
The subject of E is psychologically stable and not under the influence of intoxicants
counterexample: Suppose a psychologically stable and sober mountaineer, upon reaching the summit of a great peak, experiences a vivid and overwhelming sense of contact with a reality transcending the ordinary — a feeling of unity with the cosmos, of boundless significance, of contact with something beyond the empirical. The experience produces a lasting transformation: the mountaineer subsequently devotes his life to environmental conservation and reports that the summit experience fundamentally altered his sense of values. All three conditions are met. Yet the experience, however profound, appears to be an instance of the well-documented phenomenon of altitude-induced euphoria combined with the psychological effects of extreme physical exertion, rather than a genuine contact with a transcendent reality.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the experience not be fully explicable by known naturalistic causes — that there be no sufficient natural explanation, such as physiological or environmental factors, that would account for the phenomenological character and transformative effects of E without positing contact with a transcendent reality.
passage: One might propose that a genuine religious experience involves a vivid sense of contact with a transcendent reality, produces lasting transformation in the subject, and occurs in a psychologically stable individual free from intoxicants. This characterisation captures the phenomenological intensity, existential impact, and psychological credibility that we associate with authentic religious experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the experiences of mountaineers at extreme altitudes. Suppose a psychologically stable and entirely sober climber, upon reaching the summit of a Himalayan peak, is overwhelmed by a vivid sense of contact with a reality transcending the ordinary empirical world — a feeling of cosmic unity, of participation in something infinitely greater than himself, attended by a sense of profound peace and significance. The experience produces a lasting transformation: the climber devotes the remainder of his life to environmental conservation, attributes his conversion to the summit experience, and reports that it fundamentally reconstituted his sense of values and priorities. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the experience is of a kind well documented in the physiological literature: altitude-induced hypoxia, combined with extreme physical exertion, sensory deprivation, and the emotional intensity of summiting, is known to produce vivid feelings of transcendence, unity, and euphoria. The experience is fully explicable by naturalistic means without any appeal to contact with a transcendent reality. It appears that the definition, by requiring only phenomenological vividness, lasting transformation, and psychological stability, fails to demand that the experience not be fully accountable by known natural causes. Without this requirement, experiences produced entirely by physiological and environmental factors qualify as genuine religious experiences on equal footing with those for which no naturalistic explanation is available.

definition: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone
A employs only valid deductive inferences
A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists
counterexample: Suppose a philosopher constructs an argument from the premise that existence is a perfection and the premise that we can conceive of a being possessing all perfections, proceeding by valid deductive steps to the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists. The premises are presented as knowable by reason alone, the inferences are valid, and the conclusion is as stated. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the argument depends upon treating existence as a property that can be included among the perfections of a being, an assumption that is by no means self-evident. If existence is not a genuine predicate — if saying that something exists is not attributing a property to it in the same way as saying it is wise or powerful — then the premise that existence is a perfection is not known to be true by reason alone, despite appearing to be so.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises be genuinely self-evident or demonstrably true, rather than merely appearing to be knowable by reason alone, so that an argument resting upon a contested or question-begging premise is not counted as sound merely because the premise has the superficial character of a rational truth.
passage: One might define a sound argument for the existence of a divine being as one that proceeds from premises known by reason alone, employs valid deductive inferences, and concludes that a being possessing all perfections exists. This characterisation captures the a priori, deductively rigorous, and theologically substantive character that we associate with the most celebrated arguments of natural theology. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the tradition of ontological reasoning. Suppose a philosopher constructs an argument beginning from the premise that existence is a perfection and the premise that we can conceive of a being possessing all perfections. By valid deductive steps, the philosopher arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists. The premises are presented as accessible to reason alone, the inferences are valid, and the conclusion is as required. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the argument's soundness depends upon the assumption that existence is a genuine property or predicate that can be included among the perfections of a being. If existence is not a real predicate — if to say that something exists is not to attribute an additional property to it but rather to assert that a concept is instantiated — then the premise that existence is a perfection is not known to be true by reason alone, however natural it may at first appear. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that premises be known by reason alone, fails to demand that this rational knowability be genuine rather than merely apparent, and thereby cannot exclude arguments that rest upon contested or question-begging assumptions.

definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by a divine agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is brought about by a divine agent
E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Suppose a devout community prays for rain during a severe drought, and rain falls the following day. The community believes the rain was brought about by divine intervention and interprets it as serving a clear religious purpose: the reward of faithful prayer. Yet the rain was produced by entirely natural meteorological processes — a change in atmospheric pressure, the movement of a weather front — and violates no law of nature. The community regards the event as miraculous, and it serves a discernible religious purpose, but the first condition is not satisfied. The definition excludes events that are experienced as miraculous and serve a religious purpose but are accomplished through natural means.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the possibility that a miracle may consist not in the violation of natural law but in the extraordinary coincidence of a natural event with a specific religious context, so that the event's miraculous character lies in its timing and significance rather than in the suspension of natural regularity.
passage: One might define a miracle as an event that violates a law of nature, is brought about by a divine agent, and serves a discernible religious or moral purpose. This characterisation captures the supernaturality, divine agency, and purposiveness that we associate with the miraculous. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from ordinary religious experience. Suppose a devout community, afflicted by a severe drought, gathers in prayer and petitions the divine for rain. The following day, rain falls in abundance, ending the drought. The community interprets this event as a miracle: a divine response to faithful prayer, serving the clear religious purpose of demonstrating divine solicitude. Yet investigation reveals that the rain was produced by entirely natural meteorological processes — the arrival of a weather front whose approach was already detectable by atmospheric measurements. No law of nature was violated. The community nevertheless experiences the event as miraculous, and the event undeniably serves a discernible religious purpose within their understanding. It appears that the definition, by requiring the violation of a law of nature, excludes events that are experienced and understood as miraculous within a religious community but are accomplished through natural means whose timing coincides remarkably with the religious context. One might conclude that the definition draws the category of miracle too narrowly, failing to recognise that the miraculous may reside not in the suspension of natural regularity but in the extraordinary conjunction of natural events with moments of religious significance.

definition: A person S has faith in a proposition P if and only if (i) S believes P to be true, (ii) S lacks sufficient evidence to establish P by rational demonstration, and (iii) S's belief in P is accompanied by a feeling of trust or confidence.
conditions: S believes P to be true
S lacks sufficient evidence to establish P by rational demonstration
S's belief in P is accompanied by a feeling of trust or confidence
counterexample: Suppose a man believes that his estranged friend, who has given every outward indication of hostility, nevertheless harbours goodwill towards him. He lacks sufficient evidence to demonstrate this by rational means, and his belief is accompanied by a feeling of trust. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might question whether this is faith in the religious or philosophical sense, or merely a psychological disposition of optimism. The man's trust may be sustained by nothing more than a temperamental inclination to think well of others, lacking the volitional commitment and existential significance that we associate with faith.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's belief involve a deliberate commitment of the will that goes beyond mere psychological confidence, and that the belief bear upon matters of existential or ultimate significance, so that faith is distinguished from mere optimistic credulity.
passage: One might define faith as believing a proposition to be true in the absence of sufficient rational evidence, accompanied by a feeling of trust or confidence. This characterisation captures the belief, evidential deficit, and affective quality that are often cited as the hallmarks of faith. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from ordinary interpersonal life. Suppose a man believes that his estranged friend, who has given every outward sign of hostility and resentment, nevertheless harbours genuine goodwill towards him. The man lacks sufficient evidence to establish this belief by rational demonstration; the available evidence, if anything, tells against it. Yet he maintains the belief with a feeling of trust and confidence. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the man has faith in the sense that is of interest to the philosophy of religion. His trust may be sustained by nothing more than an optimistic temperament — a psychological disposition to think well of others regardless of the evidence. It lacks the volitional commitment, the deliberate engagement of the will in the face of uncertainty, and the existential gravity that we associate with faith in its philosophically significant sense. It appears that the definition, by requiring only belief, evidential insufficiency, and a feeling of trust, fails to distinguish faith from mere optimistic credulity, and neglects the volitional and existential dimensions that are essential to the concept.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, (ii) A expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or adoration, and (iii) the person performing A believes that the divine being is capable of hearing and responding.
conditions: A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being
A expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or adoration
The person performing A believes that the divine being is capable of hearing and responding
counterexample: Suppose a monk, following the discipline of his order, engages in a prolonged practice of silent contemplation in which he deliberately empties his mind of all verbal content, makes no petition, expresses no thanksgiving, and offers no adoration, but simply rests in what he describes as the presence of the divine. He directs no verbal or mental address to the divine being, yet he and his tradition regard this practice as the highest form of prayer. None of the three conditions are satisfied, yet the practice is recognised, within a substantial religious tradition, as genuinely and indeed supremely prayerful.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate contemplative or apophatic forms of prayer in which the practitioner transcends verbal and conceptual content altogether, and in which the prayerful character of the act consists in a receptive orientation towards the divine rather than in any communicative address.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, expressing petition, thanksgiving, or adoration, and performed by one who believes the divine being can hear and respond. This characterisation captures the communicative, expressive, and theistic dimensions that we commonly associate with prayer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the contemplative traditions of Christian monasticism. Suppose a monk, following the discipline of his order, engages in a prolonged practice of silent contemplation. He deliberately empties his mind of all verbal content. He makes no petition, expresses no thanksgiving, and offers no adoration. He directs no address, whether verbal or mental, towards the divine being. He simply rests in what he describes as the presence of the divine, maintaining a posture of receptive stillness. None of the three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the monk and his entire tradition regard this practice as the highest form of prayer — as a mode of communion with the divine that transcends the limitations of discursive thought and verbal expression. It appears that the definition, by requiring verbal or mental address and expressive content, cannot accommodate the contemplative or apophatic forms of prayer in which the practitioner transcends conceptual and linguistic activity altogether. One might conclude that the prayerful character of an act may consist not in its communicative structure but in its receptive orientation towards the divine.

definition: An object or place O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use by a religious community, (ii) O is associated with a divine being, event, or revelation, and (iii) O is treated with ritualised reverence by the members of the community.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use by a religious community
O is associated with a divine being, event, or revelation
O is treated with ritualised reverence by the members of the community
counterexample: Consider a grove of ancient trees that is regarded as sacred by a community whose religious tradition makes no reference to a divine being, divine event, or revelation. The community sets the grove apart from ordinary use, prohibiting logging and agriculture within its boundaries. The grove is treated with ritualised reverence: seasonal ceremonies are held there, and certain behaviours are prescribed and proscribed within its precincts. Yet the grove's sacredness, in the community's understanding, derives not from any association with a divine being or event but from the grove's perceived participation in a vital spiritual power inherent in the natural world. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second is not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate forms of the sacred that arise outside of theistic frameworks, in which the sacredness of an object or place derives from a perceived spiritual quality, vital power, or numinous character that does not require association with a personal divine being or specific revelatory event.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use by a religious community, associated with a divine being, event, or revelation, and treated with ritualised reverence. This characterisation captures the separation, divine connection, and ceremonial respect that we commonly attribute to sacred objects and places. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the religious practices of certain communities whose traditions do not centre upon a personal divine being. Suppose a community regards a grove of ancient trees as sacred. The grove is set apart from ordinary use: no timber may be felled within it, and agricultural activity is forbidden. The community treats the grove with ritualised reverence, holding seasonal ceremonies within its precincts and prescribing specific behaviours for those who enter. The first and third conditions are manifestly satisfied. Yet the grove's sacredness, in the community's understanding, derives not from any association with a divine being, a divine event, or a revelation, but from the grove's perceived participation in a vital spiritual power inherent in the natural world — a power that is impersonal and diffuse. The second condition is not satisfied. Yet the grove is undeniably sacred within the community's religious life. It appears that the definition, by requiring association with a divine being or revelation, draws the concept of the sacred too narrowly, excluding the sacredness that attaches to objects and places within non-theistic or animistic traditions.

definition: A communication R constitutes divine revelation if and only if (i) R originates from a divine being, (ii) R conveys propositional content that could not be discovered by unaided human reason, and (iii) R is received by a human recipient who recognises its divine origin.
conditions: R originates from a divine being
R conveys propositional content that could not be discovered by unaided human reason
R is received by a human recipient who recognises its divine origin
counterexample: Suppose a prophet receives a communication from a divine being instructing him that murder is wrong. The prophet recognises the divine origin of the communication. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the propositional content — that murder is wrong — is arguably discoverable by unaided human reason; it is a conclusion that natural moral reasoning can reach without divine assistance. If we insist on the second condition, we must deny that this is a genuine divine revelation, yet the religious traditions that report such communications typically regard them as paradigmatic instances of revelation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that divine revelation may serve to confirm, clarify, or invest with authority truths that are in principle accessible to human reason, and that the revelatory character of a communication may consist in its divine source and authoritative force rather than in the novelty of its propositional content.
passage: One might define divine revelation as a communication originating from a divine being, conveying propositional content undiscoverable by unaided human reason, and received by a human recipient who recognises its divine origin. This characterisation captures the divine source, cognitive novelty, and human reception that we associate with revelatory experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the moral content of purported revelations. Suppose a prophet receives a communication from a divine being instructing him that murder is wrong. The prophet recognises the communication as divine in origin. The communication originates from a divine being and is received by a human who recognises its source. Yet the propositional content — that murder is wrong — is arguably accessible to unaided human reason. Moral philosophy, proceeding without any appeal to divine authority, has arrived at this conclusion by a variety of rational routes. If we insist that genuine revelation must convey content undiscoverable by reason alone, we are compelled to deny revelatory status to a communication that the religious tradition in question regards as paradigmatically revelatory. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the content be inaccessible to reason, fails to recognise that revelation may serve not to disclose entirely novel truths but to confirm, clarify, or invest with divine authority truths that natural reason could in principle discover but that, in practice, human beings may fail to apprehend with sufficient clarity or conviction.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by a divine being, (ii) the divine being's command is communicated through an authoritative source recognised by the relevant religious tradition, and (iii) the divine being issuing the command is perfectly good.
conditions: A is commanded by a divine being
The divine being's command is communicated through an authoritative source recognised by the relevant religious tradition
The divine being issuing the command is perfectly good
counterexample: Suppose a divine being, acknowledged to be perfectly good, commands through an authoritative scripture that a certain group of people be shown no mercy in battle. The command is communicated through a source recognised as authoritative by the religious tradition. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the command prescribes conduct that, by the standards of any reflective moral judgement, appears gravely wrong. One is faced with the dilemma of either accepting that the merciless treatment of prisoners is morally obligatory because a perfectly good being has commanded it, or questioning whether the command can be genuinely attributed to a perfectly good being, in which case the definition becomes circular.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide an independent criterion for evaluating the moral content of divine commands, so that a command whose content appears morally repugnant cannot be rendered obligatory merely by being attributed to a divine source, and the assessment of the divine being's goodness does not depend circularly upon the content of the commands issued.
passage: One might define moral obligation as arising from the commands of a perfectly good divine being, communicated through an authoritative source recognised by the relevant tradition. This characterisation captures the divine source, authoritative transmission, and moral perfection of the commander that are central to divine-command theories. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the scriptural traditions in which a divine being commands the merciless treatment of enemies in battle. Suppose a divine being, acknowledged by the tradition to be perfectly good, commands through an authoritative scripture that a certain group of people be shown no mercy. The command is issued by a divine being, it is communicated through a recognised authoritative source, and the divine being is held to be perfectly good. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the command prescribes conduct that, by the standards of any reflective moral judgement, appears gravely wrong. One is confronted with a dilemma: either the merciless treatment of prisoners is morally obligatory because a perfectly good being has commanded it, or the command cannot genuinely be attributed to a perfectly good being. If we take the first horn, we are compelled to accept as obligatory conduct that conscience condemns. If we take the second, the assessment of the divine being's goodness turns upon the moral content of the commands, and the definition becomes circular. It appears that the definition, by making obligation depend entirely upon divine command without providing an independent moral criterion, cannot resolve this dilemma.

definition: A person S survives bodily death if and only if (i) there exists, after the death of S's body, an entity that possesses S's memories, (ii) this entity is conscious and capable of thought, and (iii) this entity stands in a relation of causal continuity with S's pre-mortem mental life.
conditions: There exists after the death of S's body an entity that possesses S's memories
This entity is conscious and capable of thought
This entity stands in a relation of causal continuity with S's pre-mortem mental life
counterexample: Suppose that, at the moment of a person's death, a divine being creates two distinct entities, each possessing the complete set of the deceased's memories, each conscious and capable of thought, and each standing in a relation of causal continuity with the deceased's pre-mortem mental life. All three conditions are satisfied by each entity. Yet both cannot be identical with the original person, for they are not identical with each other. The definition provides no resources for determining which, if either, is the surviving person, and the case suggests that the conditions are insufficient to secure personal identity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the post-mortem entity be unique — that there be exactly one such entity satisfying the conditions — so that the possibility of duplication does not generate the absurd result that a single person survives as two numerically distinct individuals.
passage: One might define survival of bodily death as the post-mortem existence of a conscious entity possessing the deceased's memories and standing in causal continuity with the deceased's pre-mortem mental life. This characterisation captures the mnemonic, psychological, and causal dimensions that we associate with personal persistence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by imagining divine intervention. Suppose that, at the moment of a person's death, a divine being creates two distinct entities, each possessing the complete set of the deceased's memories, each fully conscious and capable of thought, and each standing in a relation of causal continuity with the deceased's pre-mortem mental states. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied by each entity. Yet the two entities are not identical with each other — they occupy different locations, may diverge in subsequent experience, and are numerically distinct. Both cannot be identical with the original person, for identity is a relation that a thing bears to itself and to nothing else. The definition provides no resources for determining which, if either, is the person who has survived. It appears that the conditions are not jointly sufficient for survival, since their satisfaction by multiple entities generates a result that is logically incoherent. The definition fails to require that the post-mortem entity be unique, and this omission exposes it to the duplication problem.

definition: An experience E constitutes a veridical religious experience if and only if (i) E involves an awareness or perception of a divine presence, (ii) E produces in the subject a profound sense of awe, peace, or transformation, and (iii) E is consistent with the theological teachings of the subject's religious tradition.
conditions: E involves an awareness or perception of a divine presence
E produces in the subject a profound sense of awe, peace, or transformation
E is consistent with the theological teachings of the subject's religious tradition
counterexample: Suppose a person ingests a substance known to produce altered states of consciousness and subsequently reports an overwhelming awareness of divine presence accompanied by profound peace and a sense of spiritual transformation. The experience is consistent with the theological teachings of her religious tradition. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the experience was produced by the pharmacological action of a chemical compound upon the nervous system, not by the actual presence of the divine. The conditions do not distinguish between experiences that are caused by a genuine divine presence and experiences that are caused by chemical manipulation of the brain but are phenomenologically identical.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the experience be produced by the actual presence or action of the divine being perceived, rather than by some other cause — pharmacological, neurological, or psychological — that merely produces an indistinguishable phenomenological state.
passage: One might define a veridical religious experience as one involving an awareness of a divine presence, producing profound awe, peace, or transformation, and consistent with the subject's theological tradition. This characterisation captures the perceptual, affective, and doctrinally harmonious aspects that we associate with genuine religious experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of altered states of consciousness. Suppose a person ingests a substance known to produce powerful changes in conscious experience and subsequently reports an overwhelming awareness of divine presence, a profound sense of peace, and a transformative conviction of spiritual significance. The experience is consistent with the theological teachings of her religious tradition. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to pronounce the experience veridical. It was produced by the pharmacological action of a chemical compound upon the nervous system, not by the actual presence or action of a divine being. The subjective quality of the experience may be indistinguishable from that of an experience produced by a genuine divine encounter, yet its causal origin is entirely different. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenological character of the experience, the affective response of the subject, and the consistency with theological teaching, fails to require that the experience be produced by the entity whose presence it purports to reveal. Without such a causal requirement, the definition cannot distinguish genuinely veridical religious experiences from pharmacologically induced states that are phenomenologically identical.

definition: A theodicy T constitutes an adequate response to the existence of suffering if and only if (i) T identifies a morally sufficient reason for which a perfectly good, omnipotent, and omniscient being might permit suffering, (ii) the reason cited by T applies to all instances of suffering, and (iii) T does not diminish or deny the reality of the suffering it seeks to explain.
conditions: T identifies a morally sufficient reason for which a perfectly good, omnipotent, and omniscient being might permit suffering
The reason cited by T applies to all instances of suffering
T does not diminish or deny the reality of the suffering it seeks to explain
counterexample: Suppose a theodicy argues that suffering is permitted because it provides the occasion for the development of moral virtues such as courage, compassion, and perseverance. The reason is morally weighty, and the theodicy does not deny the reality of suffering. Yet the reason does not apply to the suffering of infants or animals, who are incapable of developing moral virtues. The second condition is not satisfied: there exist instances of suffering — the slow death of a fawn in a forest fire, the agony of a newborn with a fatal defect — to which the soul-making justification cannot apply. The theodicy covers many cases but leaves an irreducible residue unexplained.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the morally sufficient reason cited by T be applicable to instances of suffering borne by beings who cannot benefit from it — such as non-rational animals and infants — and thereby cannot account for the full scope of suffering that a theodicy must address.
passage: One might define an adequate theodicy as one that identifies a morally sufficient reason for the divine permission of suffering, applies that reason to all instances of suffering, and does not diminish the reality of the suffering explained. This characterisation captures the moral seriousness, universality, and honesty that we demand of any response to the problem of suffering. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the soul-making tradition. Suppose a theodicy argues that suffering is permitted because it provides the occasion for the development of moral virtues: courage, compassion, patience, and perseverance. The reason is morally weighty, and the theodicy does not deny or minimise the reality of the suffering it addresses. Yet the reason manifestly does not apply to all instances of suffering. Consider the slow death of a fawn trapped in a forest fire, or the agony of a newborn afflicted with a fatal congenital defect. These beings are incapable of developing moral virtues; no soul-making can occur in them. The theodicy, however compelling for the case of rational adult human beings, leaves an irreducible residue of suffering to which its justification cannot apply. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the reason apply to all instances of suffering, sets a standard that the soul-making theodicy cannot meet — yet rather than revealing a defect in the theodicy alone, this failure exposes a deficiency in the definition itself, which does not specify what would count as a morally sufficient reason for suffering borne by beings who are constitutionally incapable of benefiting from it.

definition: An argument A constitutes a sound demonstration of the existence of a supreme being if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or known with certainty, (ii) each inferential step in A is deductively valid, and (iii) the conclusion of A asserts the existence of a being possessing the traditional divine attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness.
conditions: A proceeds from premises that are self-evident or known with certainty
Each inferential step in A is deductively valid
The conclusion asserts the existence of a being with omnipotence omniscience and perfect goodness
counterexample: Suppose a philosopher constructs an argument beginning from the self-evident premise that something exists, proceeding through a chain of deductively valid steps concerning the impossibility of an infinite regress of contingent beings, and concluding that there exists a necessary being possessing omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the argument establishes at most the existence of a necessary ground of contingent beings; nothing in the chain of reasoning warrants the attribution of perfect goodness to this being, for the argument concerns only the modal status of existence, not the moral character of the entity whose existence is demonstrated. The conclusion oversteps the premises by appending an attribute that the reasoning does not support.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that each attribute asserted in the conclusion be individually supported by the premises and the chain of reasoning, so that an argument may validly establish the existence of a necessary being while illicitly attributing to it moral perfections that the premises do not warrant.
passage: One might propose that a sound demonstration of the existence of a supreme being is an argument proceeding from self-evident premises through deductively valid steps to a conclusion asserting the existence of an omnipotent, omniscient, and perfectly good being. This characterisation captures the certainty, logical rigour, and theological content that we associate with the most ambitious demonstrations in natural theology. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a philosopher begins from the self-evident premise that something exists and proceeds through a chain of deductively valid inferences concerning the impossibility of an infinite regress of contingent beings. He concludes that there must exist a necessary being, and he attributes to this being the traditional divine properties of omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the premises are self-evident, the inferences are valid, and the conclusion asserts the existence of a being with the requisite attributes. Nevertheless, one might observe that the argument establishes at most the existence of a necessary ground of contingent beings. Nothing in the chain of reasoning warrants the attribution of perfect goodness, for the argument concerns only the modal status of existence and not the moral character of the entity in question. The conclusion oversteps the premises by appending an attribute that the reasoning does not support. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the conclusion assert the relevant attributes, fails to demand that each attribute be individually supported by the premises and the chain of reasoning.

definition: A theodicy T constitutes an adequate response to the existence of suffering if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the divine permission of every instance of suffering, (ii) T is consistent with the traditional attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness, and (iii) T does not appeal to mystery or incomprehensibility as a substitute for rational explanation.
conditions: T provides a morally sufficient reason for the divine permission of every instance of suffering
T is consistent with traditional divine attributes of omnipotence omniscience and perfect goodness
T does not appeal to mystery or incomprehensibility as a substitute for rational explanation
counterexample: Suppose a theodicy argues that suffering is permitted because it is necessary for the development of moral virtue: courage cannot exist without danger, compassion cannot exist without distress, and patience cannot exist without adversity. This provides a morally sufficient reason for the divine permission of suffering, is consistent with the traditional divine attributes, and does not appeal to mystery. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet consider the suffering of an infant who dies of a painful disease before reaching the age at which moral virtue can be developed. The infant's suffering does not serve the purpose of moral development, for the infant has no capacity to develop virtue. The theodicy provides a morally sufficient reason for suffering in general but not for this particular instance, and the definition's requirement of a reason for every instance is met only if one stretches the notion of soul-making beyond its natural application.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the morally sufficient reason be applicable to the specific character of each instance of suffering, rather than merely to suffering in general, so that a theodicy may satisfy all conditions by providing a plausible general justification while failing to account for instances whose specific features render the justification inapplicable.
passage: One might define an adequate theodicy as one that provides a morally sufficient reason for every instance of suffering, is consistent with the traditional divine attributes, and does not appeal to mystery as a substitute for rational explanation. This characterisation captures the moral, theological, and rational demands that we place upon any serious attempt to reconcile suffering with divine perfection. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the suffering of those who cannot benefit from it. Suppose a theodicy argues that suffering is divinely permitted because it is necessary for the development of moral virtue: courage requires danger, compassion requires distress, patience requires adversity. This provides a morally sufficient reason, is consistent with omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness, and does not appeal to mystery. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, consider the suffering of an infant who dies of a painful disease before reaching the age at which any moral virtue could be developed. The infant's suffering does not serve the purpose of soul-making, for the infant has no capacity to develop the virtues in question. The theodicy provides a plausible general justification for suffering but not for this particular instance, whose specific character renders the justification inapplicable. It appears that the definition, by requiring a morally sufficient reason for every instance, fails to demand that the reason be specifically applicable to the features of each instance rather than merely applicable to suffering in general.

definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) E is brought about by the direct intervention of a divine agent, and (iii) E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E is brought about by the direct intervention of a divine agent
E serves a discernible religious or moral purpose
counterexample: Suppose that in a remote village a spring of water suddenly appears in a previously arid location. The event violates no known law of nature; geological investigation reveals an underground aquifer that was breached by a minor seismic shift. The event is not brought about by any divine agent; it is entirely explicable by natural geological processes. And yet the appearance of the spring saves the village from a drought and is interpreted by the inhabitants as having profound religious significance. They build a shrine, and the event transforms the spiritual life of the community. Now suppose, conversely, that a genuine violation of a natural law occurs: a body of water spontaneously divides, in contravention of fluid dynamics, by the direct intervention of a divine agent, for the purpose of allowing a religious community to cross safely. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet an observer with no knowledge of the religious context witnesses the event and has no means of distinguishing it from an extraordinarily improbable but naturally possible fluctuation. The definition requires divine agency, yet the event admits of no observational mark by which divine agency can be distinguished from natural anomaly.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide any criterion by which the divine origin of an event can be distinguished from an extremely improbable natural occurrence, so that an event satisfying all three conditions remains indistinguishable, from the standpoint of any observer, from a natural anomaly of extraordinary rarity.
passage: One might propose that a miracle is an event that violates a law of nature, is brought about by direct divine intervention, and serves a discernible religious or moral purpose. This characterisation captures the supernatural, agential, and purposive dimensions that we associate with the miraculous. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the problem of identification. Suppose a body of water spontaneously divides, in manifest contravention of the laws of fluid dynamics, by the direct intervention of a divine agent, for the purpose of allowing a community of believers to cross safely. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that an observer without prior knowledge of the religious context has no means of distinguishing this event from an extraordinarily improbable but naturally possible fluctuation. The laws of statistical mechanics do not absolutely prohibit such a fluctuation; they render it vanishingly unlikely, but not impossible. The definition requires divine agency as a condition, yet it provides no criterion by which divine agency can be identified in practice. It appears that the definition, by stipulating divine intervention without supplying an observational mark of such intervention, fails to distinguish genuine miracles from natural anomalies of extraordinary rarity. Without such a criterion, the concept of a miracle remains in principle applicable yet in practice unverifiable.

definition: A person P possesses genuine religious faith if and only if (i) P holds certain propositions concerning the divine to be true, (ii) P maintains these beliefs in the absence of conclusive empirical evidence, and (iii) P's beliefs are accompanied by a disposition to act in accordance with the demands those propositions impose.
conditions: P holds certain propositions concerning the divine to be true
P maintains these beliefs in the absence of conclusive empirical evidence
P is disposed to act in accordance with the demands those propositions impose
counterexample: Suppose a man holds it to be true that a benevolent deity governs the universe and that this deity commands charitable conduct. He maintains these beliefs without conclusive empirical evidence. And he is disposed to act charitably, donating generously and assisting those in need. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the man's disposition to act charitably is motivated entirely by the social prestige that charitable conduct confers in his community, and were the social rewards to be removed, he would abandon both his charitable conduct and the beliefs that ostensibly ground it. His faith is indistinguishable, by the proposed definition, from genuine religious commitment, yet it is in truth a calculus of social advantage rather than an authentic orientation of the soul towards the divine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the disposition to act in accordance with the propositions be motivated by the beliefs themselves rather than by extrinsic considerations such as social advantage, so that a person whose conforming behaviour is instrumentally rather than intrinsically motivated may satisfy all conditions without possessing genuine faith.
passage: One might define genuine religious faith as the holding of certain propositions concerning the divine to be true, maintained in the absence of conclusive empirical evidence, and accompanied by a disposition to act in accordance with the demands those propositions impose. This characterisation captures the cognitive, evidential, and practical dimensions that we associate with authentic faith. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man holds it to be true that a benevolent deity governs the universe and that this deity commands charitable conduct. He maintains these beliefs without conclusive empirical evidence. And he is disposed to act charitably, donating generously to those in need. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the man's charitable disposition is motivated entirely by the social prestige that charitable conduct confers in his community. Were the social rewards to be withdrawn, he would abandon both his charitable conduct and the beliefs that ostensibly sustain it. His faith is, by the proposed definition, indistinguishable from genuine religious commitment; yet it is in truth a calculus of social advantage rather than an authentic orientation of the soul towards the divine. It appears that the definition, by requiring only a disposition to act in accordance with the propositions, fails to demand that the disposition be motivated by the beliefs themselves rather than by extrinsic considerations. Without such a requirement, the analysis admits cases of instrumental conformity as instances of genuine faith.

definition: An act A constitutes genuine prayer if and only if (i) A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, (ii) A expresses a petition, thanksgiving, or adoration, and (iii) A is performed with the sincere belief that the divine being is capable of hearing and responding.
conditions: A is a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being
A expresses a petition thanksgiving or adoration
A is performed with sincere belief that the divine being can hear and respond
counterexample: Suppose a monk in a contemplative order sits in silence, emptying his mind of all verbal content, all petitionary desire, and all conceptual imagery of the divine. He does not address any being; he does not express petition, thanksgiving, or adoration; and he holds no specific belief about whether any being is hearing him. He is engaged in what his tradition calls the prayer of silence or the prayer of emptying. By the proposed definition, this act fails to qualify as prayer, for it satisfies none of the three conditions. Yet the contemplative tradition from which he comes regards this practice as the highest form of prayer, surpassing in spiritual depth all verbal petitions and expressions of adoration.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that certain forms of prayer are non-verbal, non-petitionary, and non-conceptual, involving an emptying of mental content rather than an addressing of a divine being, so that the most exalted forms of contemplative prayer are excluded by conditions that capture only the discursive and petitionary modes of the practice.
passage: One might define genuine prayer as a verbal or mental address directed towards a divine being, expressing petition, thanksgiving, or adoration, and performed with the sincere belief that the divine being is capable of hearing and responding. This characterisation captures the communicative, expressive, and believing dimensions that we commonly associate with prayer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the contemplative traditions of certain religious orders. Suppose a monk sits in silence, emptying his mind of all verbal content, all petitionary desire, and all conceptual imagery of the divine. He does not address any being, for his practice consists precisely in the abandonment of address. He does not express petition, thanksgiving, or adoration, for his exercise consists in the cessation of all expressive acts. And he holds no specific belief about whether any being is hearing him, for the practice requires the suspension of conceptual thought. By the proposed definition, this act fails to qualify as prayer on every count. Yet the contemplative tradition from which he comes regards this practice as the highest form of prayer, surpassing in spiritual depth all verbal petitions and expressions of adoration. It appears that the definition, by requiring verbal or mental address, expressive content, and propositional belief, fails to accommodate those forms of prayer that consist in the emptying of mental content and the suspension of discursive thought. Without this accommodation, the analysis excludes the very practices that many traditions regard as the most profound.

definition: A communication C constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) C originates from a divine source rather than from human invention, (ii) C conveys truths that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason, and (iii) C is delivered through a recognised prophetic or scriptural medium.
conditions: C originates from a divine source rather than from human invention
C conveys truths that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason
C is delivered through a recognised prophetic or scriptural medium
counterexample: Suppose an individual who is not a recognised prophet and who produces no scriptural text nevertheless receives, in a moment of intense spiritual experience, a communication that originates from a divine source and conveys a truth that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, for the communication is not delivered through any recognised prophetic or scriptural medium. By the proposed definition, the communication fails to qualify as a divine revelation. Yet if the content of the communication is genuinely of divine origin and genuinely beyond the reach of human reason, it seems arbitrary to deny it the status of revelation merely because it arrives through an unrecognised channel.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that genuine divine communications may arrive through channels not previously recognised as prophetic or scriptural, so that the restriction to recognised media illicitly narrows the scope of revelation by imposing a condition of institutional or traditional recognition that may exclude authentic divine communications.
passage: One might define divine revelation as a communication originating from a divine source, conveying truths beyond the reach of unaided human reason, and delivered through a recognised prophetic or scriptural medium. This characterisation captures the supernatural origin, cognitive content, and authoritative transmission that we associate with the great revelations of the world's religions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering the diversity of channels through which the divine might communicate. Suppose an individual who is not a recognised prophet and who produces no scriptural text nevertheless receives, in a moment of intense spiritual experience, a communication that genuinely originates from a divine source and conveys a truth that could not have been discovered by unaided human reason. The first two conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the communication is of divine origin and its content transcends what reason alone could supply. Yet the third condition is not met, for the communication arrives through no recognised prophetic or scriptural medium. By the proposed definition, this communication fails to qualify as divine revelation. Yet it seems arbitrary to deny revelation to a communication that is genuinely divine in origin and genuinely beyond human reason, merely because it does not pass through the channels that tradition happens to recognise. It appears that the definition, by restricting revelation to recognised media, imposes a condition of institutional or traditional recognition that may exclude authentic divine communications arriving through channels that have not yet been acknowledged.

definition: An action A is morally obligatory if and only if (i) A is commanded by a divine being, (ii) the divine being's command is promulgated to rational creatures through a discernible medium such as scripture or prophetic utterance, and (iii) the command is consistent with the divine being's nature as perfectly good.
conditions: A is commanded by a divine being
The command is promulgated through a discernible medium such as scripture or prophetic utterance
The command is consistent with the divine being's nature as perfectly good
counterexample: Suppose two sacred texts, both accepted within a single religious tradition as genuine records of divine commands, issue contradictory directives: one commands the taking of interest on loans, and the other forbids it. Each command satisfies all three conditions: each is attributed to the divine being, each is promulgated through an accepted scriptural medium, and each can be interpreted as consistent with perfect goodness, the first on grounds of economic stewardship and the second on grounds of compassion for debtors. A rational creature confronted with both commands cannot obey both, yet the definition provides no means of adjudicating between them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide a criterion for resolving conflicts between apparently genuine divine commands, so that when two divinely attributed commands contradict one another and each satisfies all three conditions, the analysis offers no guidance as to which command constitutes the genuine moral obligation.
passage: One might propose that an action is morally obligatory if it is commanded by a divine being, promulgated through a discernible medium such as scripture or prophetic utterance, and consistent with the divine being's perfectly good nature. This characterisation captures the theistic, communicative, and moral dimensions that ground the idea of divine moral legislation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the interpretation of sacred texts. Suppose two scriptures, both accepted within a single religious tradition as genuine records of divine commands, issue contradictory directives: one commands the taking of interest on loans as an exercise of responsible stewardship, and the other forbids it as an act of cruelty towards the poor. Each command satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. Each is attributed to the divine being, each is promulgated through an accepted scriptural medium, and each can be interpreted as consistent with perfect goodness when viewed under the appropriate moral description. A rational creature confronted with both commands cannot obey both, yet the definition provides no means of adjudicating between them. It appears that the definition, by requiring only divine origin, discernible promulgation, and consistency with divine goodness, fails to supply a criterion for resolving conflicts between apparently genuine divine commands. Without such a criterion, the analysis leaves the moral agent without guidance precisely when guidance is most needed.

definition: An object or place O is sacred if and only if (i) O is set apart from ordinary use by a religious community, (ii) O is regarded by that community as a locus of divine presence or power, and (iii) O is treated with rituals of reverence, including prohibitions on profane contact.
conditions: O is set apart from ordinary use by a religious community
O is regarded as a locus of divine presence or power
O is treated with rituals of reverence including prohibitions on profane contact
counterexample: Suppose a natural spring in a remote forest is set apart from ordinary use by a community that regards it as a locus of divine power and treats it with elaborate rituals of reverence, including strict prohibitions on profane contact. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the community's reverence is directed not towards a divine being but towards the spirit of the spring itself, understood as an impersonal natural force with no personal or theistic character. On the proposed definition the spring is sacred, yet the sacral quality derives not from any divine presence but from an impersonal animistic power that the definition's second condition seems to exclude when it speaks of divine presence.
