missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate forms of sacredness grounded in impersonal, animistic, or non-theistic conceptions of spiritual power, so that objects or places revered as loci of impersonal spiritual forces are either excluded or accommodated only by stretching the concept of the divine beyond its natural meaning.
passage: One might define the sacred as that which is set apart from ordinary use by a religious community, regarded as a locus of divine presence or power, and treated with rituals of reverence including prohibitions on profane contact. This characterisation captures the distinctness, numinous quality, and ritual treatment that we associate with the sacred in the great theistic traditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from animistic practices. Suppose a natural spring in a remote forest is set apart from ordinary use by a community. The community regards the spring as a locus of spiritual power and treats it with elaborate rituals of reverence, including strict prohibitions on profane contact. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the community's reverence is directed not towards a divine being but towards the spirit of the spring itself, understood as an impersonal natural force with no personal or theistic character. The sacral quality derives not from divine presence in any ordinary sense but from an impersonal animistic power. It appears that the definition, by speaking of divine presence or power, fails to accommodate forms of sacredness grounded in non-theistic or impersonal conceptions of spiritual force. Without this accommodation, the analysis either excludes a vast range of genuinely sacred phenomena or can accommodate them only by stretching the concept of the divine beyond its natural and intended application.

definition: A person P survives bodily death if and only if (i) there exists after P's bodily death an entity that possesses P's memories and personality traits, (ii) this entity is conscious and capable of experience, and (iii) there is a continuous causal chain linking P's pre-mortem mental states to the post-mortem entity's mental states.
conditions: There exists after bodily death an entity possessing P's memories and personality traits
This entity is conscious and capable of experience
There is a continuous causal chain linking pre-mortem to post-mortem mental states
counterexample: Suppose that at the moment of a person's death, a divine agent creates two distinct entities, each possessing all of the person's memories and personality traits, each conscious and capable of experience, and each linked to the person's pre-mortem mental states by a continuous causal chain that branches at the moment of death. All three conditions are satisfied by each of the two entities. Yet the person cannot be identical with both, for the two post-mortem entities are numerically distinct from one another: they occupy different locations and will go on to have divergent experiences. If survival requires identity, then the person survives as neither, for identity does not admit of branching.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the post-mortem entity be unique, so that cases of branching or fission, in which two or more entities satisfy all three conditions simultaneously, render the concept of survival incoherent by producing multiple equally legitimate claimants to the identity of the deceased person.
passage: One might propose that a person survives bodily death if there exists after death an entity possessing the person's memories and personality traits, this entity is conscious and capable of experience, and a continuous causal chain links the pre-mortem mental states to those of the post-mortem entity. This characterisation captures the psychological, experiential, and causal continuity that we naturally associate with the survival of personal identity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by imagining a scenario of branching. Suppose that at the moment of a person's death, a divine agent creates two distinct entities, each possessing all of the person's memories and personality traits, each conscious and capable of experience, and each linked to the person's pre-mortem mental states by a continuous causal chain that branches at the instant of death. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied by each of the two entities. Yet the person cannot be identical with both, for the two post-mortem entities are numerically distinct: they occupy different locations and will go on to have divergent experiences. If survival requires identity, then the person survives as neither, for identity does not admit of branching. It appears that the definition, by failing to require that the post-mortem entity be unique, allows cases of fission to satisfy every condition while rendering the concept of survival incoherent. Two equally legitimate claimants to the identity of the deceased person cannot both be that person, yet neither has a stronger claim than the other.

definition: An experience E constitutes a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) the subject of E reports an immediate awareness of a reality transcending the ordinary empirical world, (ii) E produces in the subject a lasting transformation of character or outlook, and (iii) E is not explicable by any known natural cause such as disease, intoxication, or extreme fatigue.
conditions: The subject reports immediate awareness of a transcendent reality
E produces a lasting transformation of character or outlook
E is not explicable by any known natural cause
counterexample: Suppose a woman reports an immediate awareness of a transcendent reality during a period of solitary meditation. The experience produces a profound and lasting transformation of her character: she becomes more compassionate, more serene, and more devoted to acts of charity. And the experience is not explicable by any known natural cause: she was in good health, was not intoxicated, and was not fatigued. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that, unknown to the woman and to her physicians, she possesses a rare neurological condition that, under the specific conditions of prolonged meditation, produces vivid experiences of transcendence. The cause is natural but has not yet been identified by medical science. The experience satisfies the third condition only because the relevant natural cause is unknown, not because no natural cause exists.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the absence of a known natural explanation and the genuine absence of any natural explanation, so that an experience produced by an as yet undiscovered natural cause may satisfy all conditions simply because the cause has not yet been identified by science.
passage: One might define a genuine religious experience as one in which the subject reports immediate awareness of a transcendent reality, the experience produces a lasting transformation of character, and the experience is not explicable by any known natural cause. This characterisation captures the phenomenological, transformative, and inexplicable dimensions that we associate with the most profound religious experiences. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the gap between what is currently known and what may eventually be discovered. Suppose a woman reports an immediate awareness of a reality transcending the empirical world during a period of solitary meditation. The experience produces a profound and lasting transformation of her character: she becomes more compassionate, more serene, and more devoted to charitable works. And the experience is not explicable by any known natural cause, for she was in good health, was not intoxicated, and was not unduly fatigued. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, suppose that she possesses a rare and as yet unidentified neurological condition that, under the specific conditions of prolonged meditation, produces vivid experiences of transcendence. The cause is entirely natural but has not yet been discovered by medical science. The experience satisfies the third condition only because the relevant natural explanation is currently unknown, not because no natural explanation exists. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the experience not be explicable by known natural causes, fails to distinguish between what is genuinely inexplicable by nature and what is merely unexplained by the current state of scientific knowledge.

definition: A moral prohibition is divinely grounded if and only if (i) the prohibition is traceable to an explicit command issued by a divine being, (ii) the command applies universally to all rational creatures regardless of time or place, and (iii) the command is accompanied by a sanction for disobedience that reflects the gravity of the transgression.
conditions: The prohibition is traceable to an explicit command from a divine being
The command applies universally to all rational creatures regardless of time or place
The command is accompanied by a sanction for disobedience reflecting the gravity of the transgression
counterexample: Suppose a divine command explicitly prohibits the consumption of a particular animal, applies this prohibition universally to all rational creatures, and prescribes a severe sanction for disobedience. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the command was issued in a context in which the animal in question carried a disease that posed a genuine threat to human health. In a later period, the disease has been eradicated, and the consumption of the animal poses no risk. The command satisfies the condition of universal applicability, yet its rationale has been overtaken by changing circumstances. The prohibition binds all rational creatures regardless of time or place, yet the reason for the prohibition no longer obtains in the later context.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the rationale underlying the command remain applicable across the times and places to which the command is said to apply, so that a command whose justification is tied to a specific historical circumstance may satisfy the condition of universality in form while lacking universality in substance.
passage: One might propose that a moral prohibition is divinely grounded if it is traceable to an explicit divine command, applies universally to all rational creatures regardless of time or place, and is accompanied by a sanction for disobedience. This characterisation captures the divine authority, universality, and enforceability that we associate with divinely grounded moral law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from dietary prohibitions. Suppose a divine command explicitly prohibits the consumption of a particular animal. The prohibition applies universally to all rational creatures, and a severe sanction is prescribed for disobedience. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the command was issued in a context in which the animal in question carried a disease posing a genuine threat to human health. In a later period, the disease has been eradicated, and the consumption of the animal poses no risk whatsoever. The command remains formally universal, applying to all rational creatures regardless of time or place, yet the rationale underlying it no longer obtains. It appears that the definition, by requiring only formal universality, fails to demand that the justification for the command remain applicable across the circumstances in which it purports to bind. Without such a requirement, a prohibition whose substance is tied to a particular historical circumstance may satisfy every condition while its binding force, considered from the standpoint of reason, has been undermined by the passage of time.

definition: A being is God if and only if (i) it is the necessary ground of all contingent existence, (ii) it possesses maximal power over the natural order, and (iii) it is perfectly good.
conditions: It is the necessary ground of all contingent existence
It possesses maximal power over the natural order
It is perfectly good
counterexample: Suppose that there exists a being which is the necessary ground of all contingent existence and which possesses maximal power over the natural order. This being, however, is morally indifferent, neither good nor evil, sustaining the cosmos as an expression of its nature without any moral concern for its creatures. It meets the first two conditions but fails the third. Yet many philosophical theologians in the tradition of Aristotle and certain Neoplatonists would not hesitate to call such a being God or the divine, understanding the divine as a metaphysical principle rather than a moral agent.
missing_condition: The definition must account for conceptions of the divine that are non-moral or transpersonal, or else it must be acknowledged as applying only within a particular theological tradition that identifies God with moral perfection.
passage: The question of what it is to be God, prior to and distinct from the question of whether God exists, is of fundamental importance in the philosophy of religion. A familiar analysis holds that God is the necessary ground of all contingent existence, omnipotent over the natural order, and perfectly good. One might observe that this analysis captures the conception prevalent in much of the Western theistic tradition. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that it is adequate to all serious uses of the term. Consider a case in which a philosopher, reflecting on the structure of contingent existence, concludes that there must be a necessary being upon which all else depends. This being, she further argues, possesses maximal power, for it sustains the entirety of the natural order. However, she finds no ground for attributing moral qualities to this being; it is, as it were, beyond good and evil, sustaining the cosmos not out of benevolence but as an expression of its own nature. Such a conception is by no means eccentric; it finds precedent in the Unmoved Mover of Aristotle and in certain Neoplatonic accounts of the One. The being in question satisfies the first two conditions of our analysis but fails the third. It appears that many serious thinkers would nevertheless call this being divine. We are led to conclude that the analysis is too narrow, reflecting the commitments of a particular theological tradition rather than capturing the concept of the divine as it figures in philosophical thought more broadly.

definition: An event constitutes an instance of evil requiring theodical explanation if and only if (i) it involves the suffering of a sentient being, (ii) the suffering is undeserved, and (iii) the suffering serves no discernible purpose.
conditions: It involves the suffering of a sentient being
The suffering is undeserved
The suffering serves no discernible purpose
counterexample: Suppose that a child suffers a painful but ultimately treatable illness. The suffering is undeserved, being visited upon an innocent child, and it appears to serve no purpose. Yet through the experience, the child's parents are moved to establish a charitable foundation that alleviates the suffering of thousands of other children. The suffering, in retrospect, served a discernible purpose. By the analysis, it is no longer an instance of evil requiring theodical explanation, yet one might strongly feel that the child's suffering remains a genuine evil regardless of the good consequences that happened to follow from it.
missing_condition: The analysis must recognise that the moral character of suffering is not retroactively altered by contingent beneficial consequences, and that suffering may constitute genuine evil even when it incidentally produces good.
passage: The problem of evil has been recognised as among the most formidable challenges to theistic belief. In addressing it, one must first determine what counts as an instance of evil that stands in need of theodical explanation. A natural analysis holds that such an instance consists of the undeserved suffering of a sentient being, where that suffering serves no discernible purpose. One might observe that this analysis identifies the cases that seem most troubling to the theist. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition without reservation. Consider a case in which a young child contracts a painful illness. The child is innocent and the suffering plainly undeserved. At the time of the illness, no purpose can be discerned. Subsequently, however, the child's parents, moved by the experience, establish a charitable foundation that brings relief to thousands of similarly afflicted children. In retrospect, the suffering served a discernible purpose, and by our analysis it therefore ceases to be an instance of evil requiring theodical explanation. Yet it appears that this conclusion does violence to our moral sensibility. The child suffered, and suffered without deserving to; the fact that good consequences happened to flow from this suffering does not annul its character as evil. The beneficial outcome was contingent and could easily have failed to materialise. We are led to conclude that the moral character of suffering is not retroactively altered by subsequent goods, and that the analysis must be revised to recognise that suffering may constitute genuine evil even when it proves incidentally beneficial.

definition: An event is a miracle if and only if (i) it is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) it is brought about by a divine agent, and (iii) it serves a revelatory or salvific purpose.
conditions: It is a violation of a law of nature
It is brought about by a divine agent
It serves a revelatory or salvific purpose
counterexample: Suppose that a drought-stricken region receives sudden, abundant rain after weeks of communal prayer. The rain is entirely consistent with meteorological laws; no natural law is violated. Yet the community regards it as a direct divine response to their prayers, and it serves what they take to be a salvific purpose, delivering them from famine. This event satisfies the second and third conditions in the experience of the community but fails the first. Many religious believers would nonetheless call it a miracle.
missing_condition: The analysis must allow for miracles that operate within the natural order through providential timing or arrangement, not only through violation of natural law.
passage: The analysis of miracles has occupied philosophers of religion since at least the early modern period. A familiar definition, owing much to Hume, holds that a miracle is a violation of a law of nature brought about by a divine agent for a revelatory or salvific purpose. One might observe that this analysis captures the dramatic biblical miracles, such as the parting of the sea or the raising of the dead. Yet we should hesitate to suppose that it exhausts the concept as it operates within religious life. Consider a case in which a community, afflicted by a prolonged and devastating drought, gathers in communal prayer for relief. After several weeks of earnest supplication, abundant rain arrives. The meteorologist can explain the rainfall entirely in terms of atmospheric conditions; no law of nature has been violated. Nevertheless, the community regards the event as a direct response to their prayers, an act of divine providence that has delivered them from starvation. The event satisfies the second and third conditions as understood from within the community's religious framework, for it is attributed to a divine agent and it serves a salvific purpose. Yet it fails the first condition, since no natural law has been transgressed. It appears that many sincere and thoughtful believers would call this event a miracle, understanding it as an instance of divine action operating within rather than against the natural order. We are thus led to conclude that the analysis is too narrow, and that miracles may include events that are naturally possible but divinely timed or arranged.

definition: A person has faith if and only if (i) they hold a belief concerning a matter that cannot be conclusively established by evidence, (ii) they maintain this belief with steadfast commitment, and (iii) the belief concerns a transcendent or divine reality.
conditions: They hold a belief concerning a matter that cannot be conclusively established by evidence
They maintain this belief with steadfast commitment
The belief concerns a transcendent or divine reality
counterexample: Suppose that a scientist holds a steadfast commitment to the belief that a unified theory of fundamental forces will eventually be discovered, a matter that cannot be conclusively established by current evidence. This belief does not concern a transcendent or divine reality; it concerns the natural order. Yet one might observe that her attitude exhibits all the hallmarks of what is ordinarily called faith: a deep, unwavering trust in something not yet proved. The analysis excludes this case because it fails the third condition, yet the attitude seems genuinely to be an instance of faith.
missing_condition: Faith need not be directed at a transcendent or divine reality; it may be directed at any matter of deep personal commitment that transcends the available evidence, including commitments within the natural order.
passage: The nature of faith is a central question in the philosophy of religion, and it is natural to analyse it in terms of belief beyond evidence, steadfast commitment, and orientation towards the transcendent. One might observe that this analysis captures the paradigmatic case of religious faith, in which a believer maintains trust in God despite the absence of conclusive proof. Yet we should hesitate to restrict the concept so narrowly. Consider a case in which a theoretical physicist devotes her career to the search for a unified theory of the fundamental forces. She holds, with deep and unwavering conviction, that such a theory exists and will be found, though the evidence currently available cannot establish this. Her commitment is steadfast; she persists through decades of failed attempts and the scepticism of colleagues. Her belief, however, concerns the natural order rather than any transcendent reality. By our analysis, her attitude does not qualify as faith, for it fails the third condition. Yet it appears that her posture towards her work is precisely what we mean by faith in many ordinary and important uses of the word: a sustained trust in something not yet demonstrated, animating a life of devoted inquiry. We are led to conclude that the analysis is unduly restrictive in confining faith to the transcendent, and that the concept extends to any domain in which deep commitment outruns the available evidence.

definition: An act constitutes prayer if and only if (i) it is a verbal or mental address directed to a divine being, (ii) it involves a petition or request, and (iii) the person performing it believes that the divine being can and might grant the request.
conditions: It is a verbal or mental address directed to a divine being
It involves a petition or request
The person believes the divine being can and might grant the request
counterexample: Consider a case in which a devout contemplative sits in silence, emptying her mind of all specific thoughts and petitions, seeking only to rest in the presence of the divine. She makes no request whatever; her practice is one of pure receptivity and adoration. She regards this as the highest form of prayer, and her spiritual tradition affirms this. Yet by the analysis, her practice fails to count as prayer because it involves no petition. This seems to exclude the very form of prayer that many traditions regard as most exalted.
missing_condition: Prayer need not involve petition; it may also take the form of adoration, contemplation, thanksgiving, or silent communion with the divine.
passage: The concept of prayer is of evident importance in the philosophy of religion, yet its analysis proves less straightforward than one might suppose. A familiar proposal holds that prayer is a verbal or mental address to a divine being, involving a petition, where the supplicant believes the divine being capable of granting it. One might observe that this captures the most common form of prayer in popular religious practice. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as exhaustive. Consider a case in which a contemplative nun, following the tradition of the great mystics, sits in silence before the altar. She empties her mind of all particular thoughts and desires, making no request of any kind. Her aim is not to ask but to attend, not to speak but to listen, not to petition but to adore. She regards this practice as prayer of the most profound sort, and the spiritual tradition in which she has been formed agrees emphatically. By the proposed analysis, however, her silent contemplation does not count as prayer, for it involves no petition. It appears that this conclusion is deeply unsatisfactory. Many of the most revered spiritual writers, from the author of the Cloud of Unknowing to the Carmelite mystics, have insisted that petitionary prayer is merely a beginning and that the higher forms consist in wordless communion with the divine. We are led to conclude that the analysis errs in making petition a necessary condition, and that a fuller account must encompass adoration, contemplation, and thanksgiving alongside petition.

definition: A place or object is sacred if and only if (i) it has been formally consecrated by a religious authority, (ii) it is set apart from ordinary use, and (iii) it is treated with reverence by a community of believers.
conditions: It has been formally consecrated by a religious authority
It is set apart from ordinary use
It is treated with reverence by a community of believers
counterexample: Suppose that a natural spring in a remote forest has been regarded with profound reverence by an indigenous community for centuries. It is set apart from ordinary use, and the community treats it with deep religious respect. Yet it has never been formally consecrated by any recognised religious authority, for the community's spiritual practices do not include formal rites of consecration. By the analysis, the spring is not sacred, yet it functions as the most sacred site in the community's religious life.
missing_condition: Sacredness may arise from longstanding communal experience of numinous presence or spiritual significance, and need not depend upon formal consecration by an institutional religious authority.
passage: The concept of the sacred is central to the philosophy of religion, yet it resists easy definition. One might propose that a place or object is sacred when it has been formally consecrated, set apart from ordinary use, and treated with communal reverence. This analysis appears to capture the essential features of such paradigmatic sacred sites as cathedrals and temples. Yet we should hesitate to regard formal consecration as a necessary condition. Consider a case in which an indigenous community in a remote region has, for as long as memory reaches, regarded a natural spring as the dwelling-place of a spiritual presence. The spring is set apart from all ordinary purposes; no one bathes in it or draws water for domestic use. The community approaches it with profound reverence, performing songs and offerings at its edge. Yet the community's spiritual practices do not include any formal rite of consecration, for their understanding of the sacred is not mediated by institutional religious authority. By the proposed analysis, the spring fails to qualify as sacred, since the first condition is unmet. This conclusion appears plainly inadequate. The spring functions as the most sacred site in the community's entire religious world. We are led to conclude that sacredness may arise from longstanding communal encounter with what is felt as numinous or spiritually significant, quite independently of any formal act of institutional consecration.

definition: An event constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) it communicates propositional content about the nature of the divine, (ii) its source is a divine being, and (iii) its recipient is able to comprehend the communicated content.
conditions: It communicates propositional content about the nature of the divine
Its source is a divine being
Its recipient is able to comprehend the communicated content
counterexample: Consider a case in which a mystic, during intense contemplation, experiences an overwhelming sense of the presence of the divine that transforms her life and conviction entirely. She is unable to articulate what has been communicated; she says only that she was in the presence of something unutterably holy. No propositional content was conveyed, yet she and her tradition regard this as the most profound revelation she has ever received. The experience satisfies the second condition and arguably the third, but fails the first, since no propositional content about the divine was communicated.
missing_condition: Revelation need not be propositional in character; it may consist in a non-propositional experiential encounter with the divine that transforms understanding without conveying articulable content.
passage: The nature of divine revelation is a question of first importance in the philosophy of religion. A standard analysis holds that revelation occurs when a divine being communicates propositional content about its nature to a recipient who can comprehend it. One might observe that this analysis fits many scriptural accounts, in which God speaks to prophets in words that can be recorded and transmitted. Yet we should hesitate to confine revelation to the propositional. Consider a case in which a contemplative, absorbed in prayer, experiences what she can only describe as an overwhelming encounter with the holy. She is unable to formulate in words what has been communicated; indeed, she insists that the experience transcended all categories of thought and language. She cannot state any proposition about the divine that she did not hold before, yet she is profoundly changed. Her conviction, her orientation, her very manner of perceiving the world have been transformed. She and the spiritual tradition to which she belongs regard this as an instance of revelation of the highest order. By the proposed analysis, however, her experience fails to qualify, for no propositional content was communicated. It appears that this exclusion is unduly narrow. The mystical traditions of many religions testify to forms of divine disclosure that are experiential rather than propositional, transforming the recipient without furnishing articulable doctrines. We are thus led to conclude that the analysis must be broadened to encompass non-propositional revelation.

definition: An action is morally obligatory if and only if (i) it is commanded by God, (ii) the command is issued with the intention that it be obeyed, and (iii) the person to whom the command is addressed is aware of the command.
conditions: The action is commanded by God
The command is issued with the intention that it be obeyed
The person to whom the command is addressed is aware of the command
counterexample: Suppose that God commands a father to sacrifice his innocent son. The command is genuine, issued with the intention that it be obeyed, and the father is fully aware of it. By the analysis, the sacrifice is morally obligatory. Yet one might observe that the killing of an innocent child appears to be a paradigmatic instance of moral wrongness, and that no command, whatever its source, can make it morally obligatory. The analysis generates the conclusion that a manifestly abhorrent act is obligatory, which reveals its insufficiency.
missing_condition: The analysis must include a constraint that the commanded action not violate independently recognisable moral principles, or else it must address how divine commands relate to prior moral truths.
passage: The thesis that moral obligation is constituted by divine command has a long history in philosophical theology. One might formalise this thesis by holding that an action is morally obligatory when it is commanded by God with the intention that it be obeyed and when the person addressed is aware of the command. This analysis has the virtue of providing a clear foundation for moral obligation. Yet we should hesitate to embrace it without considering a well-known difficulty. Consider a case in which God commands a father to take the life of his innocent son. The command is unambiguous, it is issued with the clear intention that the father comply, and the father is fully aware of what is asked of him. By the proposed analysis, the sacrifice of the child is morally obligatory. It appears, however, that the deliberate killing of an innocent child is among the most clearly impermissible acts one can conceive. No sensible moral agent, one might think, should be prepared to accept that this act becomes obligatory merely because it has been commanded, even by a divine being. The case reveals a tension at the heart of the analysis: either moral obligation is genuinely constituted by divine command, in which case any commanded act is obligatory regardless of its character, or there exist moral truths independent of divine commands that constrain what God may coherently command. We are led to conclude that the analysis requires a further condition stipulating that the commanded action must not violate independently recognisable moral principles.

definition: A person survives death if and only if (i) there exists after death an entity that possesses the memories of the deceased, (ii) this entity is conscious and capable of experience, and (iii) there is a continuous causal chain linking the pre-mortem person to the post-mortem entity.
conditions: There exists after death an entity that possesses the memories of the deceased
This entity is conscious and capable of experience
There is a continuous causal chain linking the pre-mortem person to the post-mortem entity
counterexample: Suppose that, at the moment of a person's death, God creates an entirely new entity endowed with a perfect copy of all the deceased person's memories. This entity is conscious and capable of experience. However, there is no continuous causal chain linking it to the original person; it is a fresh creation furnished with duplicate memories. By the analysis, the person has not survived death, since the third condition is not met. Yet from the entity's own perspective, it experiences complete continuity with the life of the deceased and would sincerely claim to be the same person.
missing_condition: The analysis must address whether subjective continuity of experience and self-identification can constitute survival even in the absence of an underlying causal chain, or else explain why such continuity is insufficient.
passage: The question of whether personal survival of death is even coherent, let alone actual, is of perennial concern in the philosophy of religion. A natural analysis holds that a person survives death when a post-mortem entity possesses the deceased person's memories, is conscious, and is connected to the pre-mortem person by a continuous causal chain. One might observe that this analysis combines memory, consciousness, and causal continuity in a manner that seems to capture what is ordinarily meant by survival. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as wholly satisfactory. Consider a case in which, at the moment of a person's death, a divine being creates an entirely new entity and furnishes it with a perfect copy of all the deceased person's memories. This new entity is conscious and experientially rich. From its own perspective, it recalls the deceased person's life with complete vividness and sincerely regards itself as the same individual. It possesses every psychological attribute of the deceased. However, it is not causally connected to the original person; it is a wholly new creation, produced ex nihilo at the moment of death. By the stated analysis, the person has not survived, for the third condition fails. Yet the entity itself would protest that it has survived, and its experience of continuity is indistinguishable from what genuine survival would feel like. We are thus led to ask whether subjective continuity and self-identification might suffice for survival in the absence of a causal chain, or whether the causal requirement captures something essential that mere psychological resemblance cannot supply.

definition: An experience is a genuine religious experience if and only if (i) it involves a vivid sense of the presence of a transcendent reality, (ii) it produces lasting moral or spiritual transformation in the subject, and (iii) it is consistent with the doctrines of an established religious tradition.
conditions: It involves a vivid sense of the presence of a transcendent reality
It produces lasting moral or spiritual transformation in the subject
It is consistent with the doctrines of an established religious tradition
counterexample: Suppose that a person who belongs to no religious tradition and professes no creed undergoes, while walking alone in a forest, an overwhelming experience of the presence of something transcendent and sacred. The experience transforms her life profoundly: she becomes more compassionate, more attentive to others, and more committed to the alleviation of suffering. Yet her experience is not consistent with the doctrines of any established religious tradition; it defies all her attempts to fit it into a doctrinal framework. By the analysis, her experience is not genuinely religious, yet it possesses exactly the phenomenological and transformative character that makes religious experiences significant.
missing_condition: A genuine religious experience need not be consistent with the doctrines of any established tradition; it may stand outside or even challenge existing doctrinal frameworks while retaining its religious character.
passage: The status of religious experience as evidence for the existence of a transcendent reality has been much discussed. A common analysis holds that a genuine religious experience involves a vivid sense of transcendent presence, produces lasting transformation, and accords with the doctrines of an established religious tradition. One might observe that this analysis captures many of the classic cases described in the literature of religious experience. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition as necessary. Consider a case in which a woman of no religious affiliation, walking alone in a forest, is suddenly overwhelmed by a sense of the presence of something immeasurably greater than herself. The experience is vivid, unmistakable, and unlike anything she has previously known. In the weeks and months that follow, she is profoundly changed: she becomes gentler, more attentive to the suffering of others, and more purposeful in her commitments. The transformation is lasting and deep. However, she finds that no established religious doctrine adequately describes what she experienced. She cannot identify her experience with the Christian encounter with the Holy Spirit, nor with the Buddhist apprehension of emptiness, nor with any other doctrinal category. Her experience resists assimilation. By our analysis, it is not a genuine religious experience, for it fails the third condition. Yet this conclusion seems deeply unsatisfying, for her experience possesses precisely the phenomenological vividness and transformative power that make religious experiences philosophically significant. We are led to conclude that the analysis must not require doctrinal consistency, and that genuine religious experiences may transcend or challenge existing frameworks.
